ri:filename="package-ui.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Create a label to show the runtime time value by clicking <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Label</strong> on the diagram toolbar. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Label Created on the User Interface Modeling Diagram" ac:alt="Label Created on the User Interface Modeling Diagram"><ri:attachment ri:filename="package-ui-2.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Create five buttons to send the signals to the runtime object of the stopwatch model by clicking <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Button</strong> on the diagram toolbar.</li><li>Name the buttons: <strong>Start</strong>, <strong>stop</strong>, <strong>split</strong>, <strong>unsplit</strong>, and <strong>reset</strong>. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Buttons Created on the User Interface Modeling Diagram" ac:alt="Buttons Created on the User Interface Modeling Diagram"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav15cc35ab7ecdbd6e363cc911d64736af.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Create a UI Prototype by dragging the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> Class from the containment browser to the created UI frame on the diagram. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Dragging the StopWatch Class to the Frame" ac:alt="Dragging the StopWatch Class to the Frame"><ri:attachment ri:filename="stopwatch-ui-b.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Create a Runtime Value Prototype by dragging the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">time</strong> property of the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> Class from the containment browser to the created label on the diagram. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Dragging the Time Property to the Label" ac:alt="Dragging the Time Property to the Label"><ri:attachment ri:filename="stopwatch-ui-c.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Create a Signal Instance Prototype by dragging the Signal from the containment browser to each of the buttons. The buttons will send the dragged signals to the runtime object when you click them during the execution. Therefore, each signal in the stopwatch model will be dragged to each button that has a corresponding name. For example, the start signal will be dragged to the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">start</strong> button, and the stop signal will be dragged to the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stop</strong> button. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Dragging the Signals to the Buttons" ac:alt="Dragging the Signals to the Buttons"><ri:attachment ri:filename="stopwatch-ui-d.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Open the Specification window of the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">SilentStopwatch</strong> Simulation Configuration to add the ready user interface mockup for the stopwatch to the SimulationConfig for the StopWatch.</li><li>Edit the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">UI</strong> field by clicking the <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="3-dot-btn.png" /></ac:image> button. The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Select</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">«UI»</strong> dialog will open.</li><li>Select the created <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Frame</strong> in the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">ui</strong> package and click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">OK</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="select-ui-dialog.png" /></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> The modified SilentStopwatch is now ready to run the stopwatch with the UI mockup. Next, you need to run the SilentStopwatch Simulation Configuration. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> To run the SilentStopwatch Simulation Configuration</p><hr /><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Completed SimulationConfig.png" /></ac:image></p><p><br /></p><ol><li>Right-click the SilentStopwatch Simulation Configuration and select <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Run</strong>. A simulation session will be created to execute the StopWatch class.</li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Run</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> button on the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> window toolbar. The execution of the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> class will start with the designed UI mockup. The time value displayed in the label is now zero (0).<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The UI Mockup Panel when the SilentStopwatch Execution Starts" ac:alt="The UI Mockup Panel when the SilentStopwatch Execution Starts"><ri:attachment ri:filename="stopwatch-ui-e.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li></ol><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><br class="atl-forced-newline" />You can click the <strong>start</strong> button to start the stopwatch. You can use the buttons on the UI mockup to send the signals to the runtime object of the <strong>StopWatch</strong> class. The time value displayed on the label of the UI mockup will increment by one every second. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:title="The UI Mockup Panel with Time Value Increments" ac:alt="The UI Mockup Panel with Time Value Increments"><ri:attachment ri:filename="stopwatch-ui-f.png" /></ac:image><br /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Click the <strong>stop</strong> button to stop the execution and click it again to go to the final stage, which is the termination of the execution.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570241 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00577: Creating values and objects in the variables pane

- page_id: `249570241`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570241/Creating+values+and+objects+in+the+variables+pane
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:09.528+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation debugging > Runtime Value Monitoring
- labels: ['creating-values-in-variables-tab', 'creating-objects-in-variables-tab']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

39503046

39503048

39503047

You can create a new value or object through the shortcut menu **Add** **Value** of a property typed by a primitive type, a Class, or a block in the **Variables** pane. The shortcut menu will be available only when the number of existing values or objects of the selected property is less than the upper multiplicity of the property.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Using the Add Value shortcut menu to add a new value.

If a property is typed by a primitive type (and SysML primitive value types), Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will add a new value, which is a default value of the primitive type, as the value of that property.

If a property is typed by a class or a Block, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will show a list of Classes or Blocks containing the Class or the Block that types the property and all of its subtypes, which are not abstract Classes or abstract Blocks. When you select a Class or a Block from the list, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will initialize an object from that Class or Block and set the object as the value of the property.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Selecting a Class or a Block to initialize an object.

39503045

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Variables pane' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="68c7614c-09f6-4005-9db0-d30ae4b81765"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">39503046</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="da58126e-db0f-44f5-a366-222e946fc99a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">39503048</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2db62b72-10f5-4347-921c-c75b68ef0f0f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">39503047</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can create a new value or object through the shortcut menu <strong>Add</strong> <strong>Value</strong> of a property typed by a primitive type, a Class, or a block in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane. The shortcut menu will be available only when the number of existing values or objects of the selected property is less than the upper multiplicity of the property. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Context Menu to Add a New Value" ac:alt="Context Menu to Add a New Value"><ri:attachment ri:filename="addValue_menu.png" /></ac:image><br />Using the Add Value shortcut menu to add a new value.</h6><p>If a property is typed by a primitive type (and SysML primitive value types), Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will add a new value, which is a default value of the primitive type, as the value of that property. </p><p>If a property is typed by a class or a Block, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will show a list of Classes or Blocks containing the Class or the Block that types the property and all of its subtypes, which are not abstract Classes or abstract Blocks. When you select a Class or a Block from the list, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will initialize an object from that Class or Block and set the object as the value of the property. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Selecting a Class or a Block to Initialize an Object" ac:alt="Selecting a Class or a Block to Initialize an Object"><ri:attachment ri:filename="classblocks_to_initObj.png" /></ac:image><br />Selecting a Class or a Block to initialize an object.</h6><p> </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1c60a695-f941-474f-a75b-d7b4dbf912db"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">39503045</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Variables pane" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249569867 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00578: CSV export

- page_id: `249569867`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249569867/CSV+export
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-03-11T13:05:58.612+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling
- labels: ['csv-export', 'exporting-simulation-results-to-csv']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

33

CSV Export configuration allows you to export simulation results to a CSV file.

To export simulation results to a CSV file

1. Open a project. Here the <modeling_tool_install_root>\samples\simulation\HingeMonteCarloAnalysis sample project is used.
2. In the Simulation Configuration diagram, create the CSV Export configuration.
3. Open the Specification window of the CSV Export configuration and specify the following properties:
  - Record Time - if set to true , time will be recorded in the first column of the exported CSV file.
  - Write At The End (optional) - if set to true , values are recorded only once at the end of execution before termination.
  - File Name - the name of the file where the exported results are written. By default, the file is saved in the project directory.
  - **Represents** - the Classifier represented by the CSV Export configuration.
  - **Values** - the values which will be written to the exported CSV file.
  - **Export States**-**** If set to *true*, active State names are exported for the selected part. If set to *false*, the instance name of the part is exported.
  - **Load in Player** - if set to *true*, the exported CSV file is automatically loaded to the Results Player after the simulation terminates. [ATTACHMENT filename='Specification of CSVExport clearance.png']
4. Set the created CSV Export as the value of the Execution Listeners property of the Simulation Configuration. This step is important for the results to be written to file. See the CSV export configuration below: [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Run the simulation and then stop it. The results will be written to the file specified in Step 3. See the parameters of CSV Export configuration below: [ATTACHMENT filename='CSV Export Result.png']

##### Support of Part properties

Not only can CSV export the values of properties with primitive types, e.g., Real, Integer, etc., but it can also export the types or active states of the properties that are not primitive. Selected Part names in the **value** tag will be used as headers, and type names will be used as values of exported CSVs as shown in the figure below. You can also export the active State names for the selected part, if you set the Export States property to *true.*If you set the Export States property to *false*, the instance name is exported.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Selected Part names used as headers and type names used as values of exported CSVs.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Representing data from a CSV file in a line chart' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Exporting plots data to a CSV file' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

Here the <em>&lt;modeling_tool_install_root&gt;\samples\simulation\HingeMonteCarloAnalysis</em> sample project is used.</li><li>In the Simulation Configuration diagram, create the CSV Export configuration.</li><li><p>Open the Specification window of the CSV Export configuration and specify the following properties:</p><ul><li><strong>Record Time</strong> - if set to <em>true</em>, time will be recorded in the first column of the exported CSV file.</li><li><strong>Write At The End</strong> (optional) - if set to <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">true</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">, values are recorded</span> only once at the end of execution before termination.</li><li><strong>File Name</strong> - the name of the file where the exported results are written. By default, the file is saved in the project directory.</li><li><p><strong>Represents</strong> - the Classifier represented by the CSV Export configuration.</p></li><li><p><strong>Values</strong> - the values which will be written to the exported CSV file.</p></li><li><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);"><strong>Export States </strong>-<strong> </strong> If set to <em>true</em>, active State names are exported for the selected part. If set to <em>false</em>, the instance name of the part is exported.</span></li><li><p><strong>Load in Player</strong> - if set to <em>true</em>, the exported CSV file is automatically loaded to the Results Player after the simulation terminates.<br /><br /></p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Specification of CSVExport clearance.png" /></ac:image><br /><p><br /></p></li></ul></li><li><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Set the created CSV Export as the value of the Execution Listeners property of the Simulation Configuration.</span> This step is important for the results to be written to file. The results will be written to the file specified in Step 3. See the parameters of CSV Export configuration below:<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="CSV Export Result.png" /></ac:image></li></ol><h3>Support of Part properties</h3><p>Not only can CSV export the values of properties with primitive types, e.g., Real, Integer, etc., but it can also export the types or active states of the properties that are not primitive. Selected Part names in the <strong>value</strong> tag will be used as headers, and type names will be used as values of exported CSVs as shown in the figure below. You can also export the active State names for the selected part, if you set the Export States property to <em>true. </em>If you set the Export States property to <em>false</em>, the instance name is exported.<span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);"><br /><br /></span></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Part Properties Supported.PNG" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Selected Part names used as headers and type names used as values of exported CSVs.</h6></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing data from a CSV file in a line chart" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Exporting plots data to a CSV file" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570046 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00579: Customizing animation

- page_id: `249570046`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570046/Customizing+animation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:08.335+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Animation
- labels: ['customizing-animation', 'animation-customization']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Information

1441516382

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Information

1441516385

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Information

1441516384

There are four kinds of annotated elements in a model simulation: (i) Active, (ii) Visited, (iii) Breakpoint, and (iv) Last Visited elements. By default, active elements will be annotated with red, visited elements with green, last visited elements with orange, and breakpoints with yellow. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to customize the colors of these annotated elements through the **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Project options' space='']** dialog in the **Animation** group.

The **Auto** **Open** **Diagrams** option in the **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Project options' space='']** dialog and Simulation window allows Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit to open a tab where you can see a diagram that contains a currently active Element in a non-silent simulation (animation is enabled). If you want to enable this option, you need to select **true** as its value. This is useful when you want to track the simulation current status. Select **false** as the value of **Auto****Open** **Diagrams** when you would like to focus on just one diagram and avoid automatic switching or opening of diagrams.

If the value of **Auto****Open****Diagrams** is **false** and you run a silent-mode simulation of an activity diagram that has a decision node without any decision input, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will open the diagram and highlight all elements including the decision node and open a **Question** dialog to ask for a decision when the simulation reaches the decision node. However, if the value of **Auto****Open****Diagrams** is false, and you are running a non-silent simulation, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will open the diagram and highlight only the decision node.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

The default value of **Auto****Open****Diagrams** is **false**.

To select the **Auto****Open****Diagrams** option in the **Simulation** window

- Click the Auto Open Diagrams toggle button in the Simulation window as follows

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### INFO: Information

Information

When animation with selected **Auto Open Diagrams** is used, it must open animated diagrams in the same tab and must not open new diagram windows as new tabs. If a diagram is already open in a tab other than the active tab, the other tab will close, and the diagram animation will be shown in the active tab.

1441516381

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Project options' space='']

If you want to enable this option, you need to select <strong>true</strong> as its value. This is useful when you want to track the simulation current status. Select <strong>false</strong> as the value of <strong>Auto </strong><strong>Open</strong> <strong>Diagrams</strong> when you would like to focus on just one diagram and avoid automatic switching or opening of diagrams. </p><p>If the value of <strong>Auto </strong><strong>Open </strong><strong>Diagrams</strong> is <strong>false</strong> and you run a silent-mode simulation of an activity diagram that has a decision node without any decision input, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will open the diagram and highlight all elements including the decision node and open a <strong>Question</strong> dialog to ask for a decision when the simulation reaches the decision node. However, if the value of <strong>Auto </strong><strong>Open </strong><strong>Diagrams</strong> is false, and you are running a non-silent simulation, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will open the diagram and highlight only the decision node. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dfbd5162-7a95-4d00-b8d5-f2e9cd7dbb1a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The default value of <strong>Auto </strong><strong>Open </strong><strong>Diagrams</strong> is <strong>false</strong>. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To select the <strong>Auto </strong><strong>Open </strong><strong>Diagrams</strong> option in the <strong>Simulation</strong> window </p><hr /><ul><li>Click the <strong>Auto </strong><strong>Open </strong><strong>Diagrams</strong> toggle button in the <strong>Simulation</strong> window as follows</li></ul><h6 style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Auto Open Diagrams button" ac:alt="Auto Open Diagrams button"><ri:attachment ri:filename="110041_2016-06-24+15_23_53-Photos.png" /></ac:image></h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ec1cbc1c-e000-41dd-8703-db447586369a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Information</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>When animation with selected <strong>Auto Open Diagrams</strong> is used, it must open animated diagrams in the same tab and must not open new diagram windows as new tabs. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570144 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00580: Debug process simulation

- page_id: `249570144`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570144/Debug+process+simulation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:08.683+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation debugging
- labels: ['debug-buttons']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

30123672

30123675

30123674

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to control a model simulation using the debug buttons such as **Suspend**, **Resume**, **Step** **into**, and **Step** **over**. The table below explains the functions of all of the debug buttons.

| Button | Name | Shortcut key | Function |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | Start Simulation | F8 | To start simulating an object of the initialized session shown in the Sessions pane. |
|  | Restart | F8 | To restart the last simulated element. |
|  | Suspend | F8 | To pause a running simulation session in the Sessions. |
|  | Resume | F8 | To resume a simulation session. |
|  | Step into | F5 | To simulate and animate a currently active element in a selected simulation session in the Sessions pane. |
|  | Step over | F6 | To simulate a currently active element in a selected simulation session and run animation in the background. |
|  | Terminate | None | To stop a session in the Sessions pane. If the selected session contains sub-sessions, all of the sub-sessions will also be terminated. |

You can examine and edit variables in the **Variables** pane (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Variables pane' space='']), pause the a model simulation at predefined breakpoints (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Breakpoints' space='']), or simulate one element at a time using the **Step** **into** or **Step** **over** button.

The **Debugger** pane includes a player-like control panel for a step-by-step simulation (see the table above), threads or Behaviors with an expandable stack trace (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding simulation sessions' space='']), input/output console for custom commands or expressions evaluation (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Simulation console' space='']), **Variables** pane/runtime structure (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Runtime Value Monitoring' space='']), and **Breakpoints** pane.

30123671

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Variables pane' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Breakpoints' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding simulation sessions' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Simulation console' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Runtime Value Monitoring' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570381 space=MMA version=3 -->
## PAGE 00581: Deferred Events

- page_id: `249570381`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570381/Deferred+Events
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-09-12T15:08:37.838+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > State Machine simulation > Adapting models for State Machine simulation
- labels: ['event-deffering', 'deffering-events']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Important

122554799

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Important

122554801

122554798

**On this page**

22

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Important

122554800

UML State Machines provide a special mechanism for deferring Events in States. In every State, you can include a clause [Event list]/defer. If an Event in the current State’s deferred Event list occurs, the Event will be saved (deferred) for future processing until a State is entered that does not list the Event in its deferred Event list. Upon entry to such a State, the UML State Machine will automatically recall any saved Event(s) that are no longer deferred and will then either consume or discard these Events.

- A deferred Event is activated as soon as it enters a State where it is not deferred.
- A deferred Event can only be added to a State Machine diagram at this time.

#### Deferrable Trigger

A list of Triggers that are candidates to be retained by the State Machine if they trigger no transitions out of the State (not consumed). A deferred Trigger is retained until the State Machine reaches a State configuration where it is no longer deferred.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Specification window for Event State moving - showing the editable Deferrable Trigger tag.

As in the State Machine diagram in the following figure, the Elevator**'open'** Event is deferred as long as the Elevator is moving. Only when the State transitions to **'stopped'** State again, the **'open'** Event will be executed because it is no longer deferred at this point.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 A deferred clause added to Event moving which causes the Event to delay until it is no longer deferred.

#### WARNING: Important

Important

This version of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit does not support **Deferred Events** for Activity diagrams. They can now only be added to a State in a State Machine diagram.

122943877

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State Machine' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State Machine diagram' space='MT']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="61558ab5-bfc1-4cde-9ead-c4ea664e519f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">122554799</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="73b04c3b-e4ed-4fa5-8be1-1da11bbdf0da"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">122554801</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="13ca05ee-7924-408a-bd47-32c99feb82f7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">122554798</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b384a4d1-f744-4524-aedc-45f7f60c0e0d"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">2</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="minLevel">2</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3e42a51a-3848-436e-8d1c-3da033a70b43"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">122554800</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>UML State Machines provide a special mechanism for deferring Events in States. In every State, you can include a clause [Event list]/defer. If an Event in the current State’s deferred Event list occurs, the Event will be saved (deferred) for future processing until a State is entered that does not list the Event in its deferred Event list. Upon entry to such a State, the UML State Machine will automatically recall any saved Event(s) that are no longer deferred and will then either consume or discard these Events.</p><ul><li>A deferred Event is activated as soon as it enters a State where it is not deferred. </li><li>A deferred Event can only be added to a State Machine diagram at this time. </li></ul><h2>Deferrable Trigger</h2><p>A list of Triggers that are candidates to be retained by the State Machine if they trigger no transitions out of the State (not consumed). A deferred Trigger is retained until the State Machine reaches a State configuration where it is no longer deferred.</p><p><br /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Deferable Trigger" ac:alt="Deferable Trigger"><ri:attachment ri:filename="spec-of-state-moving.png" /></ac:image><br />The Specification window for Event State moving - showing the editable Deferrable Trigger tag.</h6><p>As in the State Machine diagram in the following figure, the Elevator<strong> 'open'</strong> Event is deferred as long as the Elevator is moving. Only when the State transitions to <strong>'stopped'</strong> State again, the <strong>'open'</strong> Event will be executed because it is no longer deferred at this point.</p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Simulating deferred events" ac:alt="Simulating deferred events"><ri:attachment ri:filename="deferred-events-b.png" /></ac:image><br />      A deferred clause added to Event moving which causes the Event to delay until it is no longer deferred. </h6><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7629a836-d769-4cdc-a90e-83952b10907f"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Important</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>This version of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit does not support <strong>Deferred Events</strong> for Activity diagrams. They can now only be added to a State in a State Machine diagram.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="691d9796-6ca0-48fc-82b8-6d01a42304ff"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">122943877</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="State Machine" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="State Machine diagram" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249571319 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00582: Defining the stopwatch Classifier Behavior

- page_id: `249571319`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249571319/Defining+the+stopwatch+Classifier+Behavior
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:23.865+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Tutorial > Stopwatch model sample > Creating an executable stopwatch model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A Class Element, which can be executed with Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit, must have its Classifier Behavior defined. The Classifier Behavior can be any kind of Behavior such as Activity, State Machine, and Interaction. This tutorial uses the State Machine Behavior to define the Behavior of the stopwatch.

To define the Classifier Behavior for the **StopWatch** Class, you have to create the Behavior on the StopWatch, which will be assigned later as the Classifier Behavior of the **StopWatch** automatically. 
 
 To define the Classifier Behavior for the **StopWatch** Class

1. Right-click the StopWatch symbol on the Class diagram and select New Diagram > State-Machine Diagram... . A new State Machine Element will be created under the StopWatch Class with a new State Machine diagram.
2. Name the State Machine diagram "StopWatch".
3. Make sure that the StopWatch State machine is the Classifier Behavior of the StopWatch Class. To see the Classifier Behavior of the StopWatch Class, right-click the StopWatch symbol on the Class diagram and select Specification from the context menu. The Specification window will open.
4. Select All from the Properties drop-down menu. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-class-stopwatch.png']
5. Scroll down to see the Classifier Behavior row, and you will see that the Classifier Behavior of the StopWatch Class is defined by the StopWatch State Machine. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-transition-event.png'] [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-class-stopwatch-c.png']

Next, we are going to create the StopWatch initial and final stages, and the four States (**Ready**, **Running**, **Paused**, and **Stopped**) in the initial stage as well.

To add an **Initial**stage to the **StopWatch**State Machine

- Click Initial on the State Machine diagram toolbar, and then click any area on the State Machine diagram to place the Initial stage. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavfc807e5dad35a6dc8eb8a2e2334bb90d.png']

To add the ready, running, paused, and stopped States

1. Click State on the State Machine diagram toolbar, and then click any area on the diagram to place a State. Name the created State "ready" .
2. Repeat Step 1 to create the " running" , " paused" , and " stopped" States. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavdb2618531755fa8d4b674c2de40b0379.png']

To add a final stage to the State machine diagram

- Click Final State on the State Machine diagram toolbar, and then click any area on the diagram to place the final stage. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav7c8723378243ad324a3743c62dca401d.png']

Now that the initial and final stages have been created, you will need to add the transitions between them and among the four States in the initial stage. 
 
To add a transition from the **Initial**stage to the **ready**State

1. Click a stage (the Initial stage in this example) that will be the source of the transition to be created. The smart manipulator toolbar will open.
2. Click the Transition icon on the smart manipulator toolbar. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddava072afa3d5c17146c05715796f526339.png']
3. Click a State (the ready State in this example) that will be the target of the transition. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav60294b81f5996dcd6bfa1060c5cb598a.png']
4. Repeat Steps 1 to 3 to create more transitions between the following States: from the ready to running State from the running to paused State from the running to stopped State from the paused to running State from the paused to stopped State from the stopped to ready State from the stopped State to the Final stage [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav0b3243bea9e2b36c84b6875f3524ffdf.png']
5. Create a looping transition to the running State by clicking the Transition to self icon on the smart manipulator toolbar. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav43be35b8d4a2413da8149a015837abdb.png']
6. Now your StopWatch State machine will look similar to the one shown in the following figure [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavb2013b68b56b9e5b343057299cb23010.png']

You will see that some of the created transitions have the SignalEvent triggers. These triggers need signal Elements and a package that is required to store those signal Elements. You can add a package and create the signal Elements by using the browser context menu. 
 
To add a new package for the signal Elements

1. Right-click the Model node in the containment browser and select New Element > Package .
2. Name the created package "signals" . [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav02f2e9636d8edccd1414fa29212758ed.png']

To create signal Elements

1. Right-click the signals package and select New Element > Signal .
2. Name the created signal "reset" . The reset signal will be created and stored in the signals package.
3. Repeat Steps 1 to 2 to create the split , start , stop , and unsplit signal Elements. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavbb4a178543c51f8a6410935f98cbc0ab.png']

To create signal Events for the transitions on the StopWatch State Machine diagram

- Drag a signal Element from the containment browser to a transition on the State Machine diagram. The signal Event will later be specified as the trigger of the transition. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav98b0652dae8cdc9b9239a0b08b2abf68.png']

To create a signal Event for each transition

1. Drag the start signal to the transition between the ready and running States.
2. Drag the split signal to the transition between the running and paused States.
3. Drag the stop signal to the transition between the running and stopped States.
4. Drag the unsplit signal to the transition between the paused and running States.
5. Drag the stop signal to the transition between the paused and stopped States.
6. Drag the reset signal to the transition between the stopped and ready States.
7. Drag the stop signal to the transition between the stopped and Final States. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav6e722749fb60fdc6e89a10de0981a858.png']

Now each and every transition between the States has its own signal Event. Next, you will also need to create a signal Event for the **transition****to****self** of the running State. The signal Event that will be created as the trigger for the **transition****to****self** is called **TimeEvent**. 
 
To create a signal Event for the **transition****to****self**of the running State

1. Right-click the Transition to self of the running State and select Specification to open its Specification window.
2. Click the Categorized View button to open the properties of the transition in the categorized view.
3. Click the Event Type row in the Trigger category and select TimeEvent from the drop-down menu. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-transition-event.png']
4. Click the When row in the Trigger category and type "1s" to specify that the trigger will send the time Event every second. [ATTACHMENT filename='specification-of-transition-event-b.png']
5. Click Close . You will see all of the created stages, States, transitions, and signal Events are shown on the StopWatch State Machine diagram. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav1cfe524dfbea755379071dbc78160048.png']

The signal Event that will be created as the trigger for the <strong>transition</strong><strong>to</strong><strong>self</strong> is called <strong>TimeEvent</strong>. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To create a signal Event for the <strong>transition </strong><strong>to </strong><strong>self </strong>of the running State</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the <strong>Transition</strong><strong>to</strong><strong>self</strong> of the running State and select <strong>Specification</strong> to open its Specification window.</li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Categorized View</strong> button to open the properties of the transition in the categorized view.</li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Event</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Type</strong> row in the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Trigger</strong> category and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">TimeEvent</strong> from the drop-down menu.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Specifying Time Event as the Signal Event for the Transition to Self of the Running State" ac:alt="Specifying Time Event as the Signal Event for the Transition to Self of the Running State"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-transition-event.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">When</strong> row in the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Trigger</strong> category and type <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;1s&quot;</em> to specify that the trigger will send the time Event every second.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Specifying the Value for the When Attribute in the Trigger Category" ac:alt="Specifying the Value for the When Attribute in the Trigger Category"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification-of-transition-event-b.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Close</strong>. You will see all of the created stages, States, transitions, and signal Events are shown on the StopWatch State Machine diagram.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The StopWatch State Machine with All of the Defined Stages,States, Transitions, and Signal Events" ac:alt="The StopWatch State Machine with All of the Defined Stages,States, Transitions, and Signal Events" ac:height="330" ac:width="344"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav1cfe524dfbea755379071dbc78160048.png" /></ac:image></li></ol><p> </p>
