<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249571342 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00583: Defining the stopwatch operations by using Activities

- page_id: `249571342`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249571342/Defining+the+stopwatch+operations+by+using+Activities
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-10-09T04:19:02.564+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Tutorial > Stopwatch model sample > Creating an executable stopwatch model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

984249231

984249234

984249232

This section defines the values of the elapsed time among the States in the StopWatch State Machine. When the stopwatch enters the **ready** State, the elapsed time defined by the Attribute **time:Integer[1]** should be reset to zero. In addition, the elapsed time should increment by 1 every second while the StopWatch is at the **running** State. Therefore, we need to add two new operations: (i) ResetTime and (ii) increaseTime to the StopWatch Class to define the elapsed time at the ready and running States. The **resetTime** operation will reset the elapsed time to zero, and the **increaseTime** operation will increment the elapsed time by one.

984249230

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="39f5646d-0bc3-47dd-901b-a69f559a0b60"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">984249231</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3c14916f-a570-4a4c-b221-f076761ccb8c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">984249234</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="459be496-a0fc-4b5d-bc77-6dae7f097baf"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">984249232</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>This section defines the values of the elapsed time among the States in the StopWatch State Machine. When the stopwatch enters the <strong>ready</strong> State, the elapsed time defined by the Attribute <strong>time:Integer[1]</strong> should be reset to zero. In addition, the elapsed time should increment by 1 every second while the StopWatch is at the <strong>running</strong> State. Therefore, we need to add two new operations: (i) ResetTime and (ii) increaseTime to the StopWatch Class to define the elapsed time at the ready and running States. The <strong>resetTime</strong> operation will reset the elapsed time to zero, and the <strong>increaseTime</strong> operation will increment the elapsed time by one.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a4acebe4-25bd-4647-af0f-63230fba9da9"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">984249230</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating resetTime Operation and resetTime Activity" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating  increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Creating increaseTime Operation and increaseTime Activity]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570341 space=MMA version=5 -->
## PAGE 00584: Defining Triggers on Transitions

- page_id: `249570341`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570341/Defining+Triggers+on+Transitions
- version_number: 5
- version_date: `2025-09-16T10:14:19.839+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > State Machine simulation > Adapting models for State Machine simulation
- labels: ['triggers-on-transitions']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

If you want a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Context, runtime object, and runtime value' space=''] to change its [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State' space='MED'] on a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Transition' space='MED'], you need to define a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State Machine diagram elements' space='MT'] on the Transition by [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Assigning Event Type' space='MED']. A runtime object will change its State when it receives a Trigger on the Transition. A Trigger can be a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Signal Event' space='MED'], a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Event' space='MED'], or a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Event' space='MED'].

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The StopWatch's State Machine diagram with defined Triggers on the Transitions.

#### TIP: Tip

Tip

Optionally, you can also use a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Send Signal Action' space='MED'] of an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity' space=''] as a Signal Event of Transition. As shown in the following figure, the *stopped* State can wait for the *off* Send Signal Action of the *do*Activity to proceed to the final State.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The off Send Signal Action of the do Activity as a Signal Event of Transition.

#### WARNING: Warning

Warning

- According to UML Version 2.5.1, the initial Transition must not have Triggers or Guards. If the Initial Transition violates the rule, Simulation will print a warning message in the Console pane .
- Simulation also provides the validation rule to prevent the model error by displaying a warning in the Validation Results panel . You can either select the Check Model Before Execution option through validation and verification before running the simulation or select Pre-execution constraints from the [Validation Suite](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Validation+dialog#Validationdialog-descrDescriptionofValidationdialogoptions) drop-down list in the **Validation** dialog and click Validate .

**Related page**

- Signal properties mapping to behavior parameters

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>If you want a <ac:link ac:anchor="Runtimeobject"><ri:page ri:content-title="Context, runtime object, and runtime value" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[runtime object]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> to change its <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="State" /></ac:link> on a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Transition" /></ac:link>, you need to define a <ac:link ac:anchor="Trigger"><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="State Machine diagram elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Trigger]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> on the Transition by <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Assigning Event Type" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[assigning an Event Type to a Transition]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. A runtime object will change its State when it receives a Trigger on the Transition. A Trigger can be a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Signal Event" /></ac:link>, a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Event" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Time Event]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, or a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Event" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Change Event]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p><br /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="StopWatch State Machine.png" /></ac:image>The StopWatch's State Machine diagram with defined Triggers on the Transitions.</h6><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ef68b471-2890-49cd-b761-4690446070d2"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Tip</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Optionally, you can also use a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Send Signal Action" /></ac:link> of an <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Activity" /></ac:link> as a Signal Event of Transition. As shown in the following figure, the <em>stopped</em> State can wait for the <em>off</em> Send Signal Action of the <em>do </em>Activity to proceed to the final State.</p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Send Signal Action.PNG" /></ac:image>The off Send Signal Action of the do Activity as a Signal Event of Transition.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="85d9561c-851f-4963-9b5b-d06c07870df8"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Warning</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li data-uuid="2b9e3250-046e-4f7e-833f-ed8eaae2e313">According to UML Version 2.5.1, the initial Transition must not have Triggers or Guards. If the Initial Transition violates the rule, Simulation will print a warning message in the <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/Console+pane">Console pane</a>.</li><li data-uuid="3516589d-d825-46d4-99c2-b4324653e1d9">Simulation also provides the validation rule to prevent the model error by displaying a warning in the <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Validation+Results+panel">Validation Results panel</a>. You can either select the <strong>Check Model Before Execution</strong> option through <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/Validation+and+verification">validation and verification</a> before running the simulation or select <strong>Pre-execution constraints</strong> from the <strong><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Validation+dialog#Validationdialog-descrDescriptionofValidationdialogoptions">Validation Suite</a></strong> drop-down list in the <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Validation+dialog"><strong>Validation</strong> dialog</a> and click <strong>Validate</strong>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="edbf6741-c53b-4d10-88db-d1f6ffc929e3"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/Signal+properties+mapping+to+behavior+parameters">Signal properties mapping to behavior parameters</a></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249569601 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00585: Diagram

- page_id: `249569601`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249569601/Diagram
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:05.491+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Model simulation > Elements simulation
- labels: ['diagram-simulation', 'simulating-diagrams']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can use the shortcut menu or the diagram toolbar to simulate a diagram. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will simulate the element, which is the context of the selected diagram, the same way it simulates a Behavior or a Class depending on the element which is the context of diagram. You can also specify the diagram as a target of a Simulation Configuration (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Running a Simulation Configuration' space=''] for more details).

To simulate a diagram do one of the following

- Right-click a diagram and select either Simulation > Run or Simulation > Run with Context ( if the context of the diagram is a Behavior). 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
- Click the **Run** button in the diagram toolbar and select either **Run** or **Run with Context** (if the context of the diagram is a Behavior). 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To terminate diagram simulation do one of the following

- Click the **Terminate** button in the diagram toolbar. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
- Click the **Terminate** button in the **Simulation** pane. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can use the shortcut menu or the diagram toolbar to simulate a diagram. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will simulate the element, which is the context of the selected diagram, the same way it simulates a Behavior or a Class depending on the element which is the context of diagram. You can also specify the diagram as a target of a Simulation Configuration (see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Running a Simulation Configuration" /></ac:link> for more details).</p><p><br /></p><p>To simulate a diagram do one of the following</p><hr /><ul><li>Right-click a diagram and select either <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Run</strong> or <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Run with Context</strong> (<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">if the context of the diagram is a Behavior).<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="simulating_diagram_from_context_menu.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Click the <strong>Run</strong> button in the diagram toolbar and select either <strong>Run</strong> or <strong>Run with Context</strong> (<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">if the context of the diagram is a Behavior).</span><br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="simulating_diagram_from_diagram_toolbar.png" /></ac:image><br /></span></li></ul><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><br /></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">To terminate diagram simulation do one of the following</span></p><p /><hr /><ul><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Click the <strong>Terminate</strong> button in the diagram toolbar.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="terminating_simulation_from_diagram_toolbar.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Click the <strong>Terminate</strong> button in the <strong>Simulation</strong> pane.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="terminating_simulation_from_simulation_pane.png" /></ac:image></span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><br /></span></li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=255629771 space=MMA version=8 -->
## PAGE 00586: Diagram animation on HTML UI

- page_id: `255629771`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/255629771/Diagram+animation+on+HTML+UI
- version_number: 8
- version_date: `2025-09-13T11:05:11.804+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > HTML UI
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Behavioral diagrams (Activity diagrams, State Machine diagrams, and Sequence Diagrams) and Composite Structure diagrams (Internal Block diagrams) can be embedded into HTML-generated user interfaces and animated.

Composite Structure diagrams only display activations and not run-time values.

##### Creating a UI Mockup with a State Machine Diagram

The Joystick.mdzip sample model is used to demonstrate how to create a UI Mockup with a State Machine Diagram.

To create a UI Mockup with a State Machine Diagram

1. Open the <modeling_tool_install_dir>\samples\simulation\Joystick.mdzip sample model.
2. Create a User Interface Modeling Diagram.
3. In the diagram, create a Frame and create a Panel inside the Frame. To learn more about the components of a User Interface Modeling Diagram, refer to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UI modeling diagram simulation' space=''] .
4. Drag a State Machine Diagram to the Panel using a long drop and select the Create Diagram Knob Symbol command. [ATTACHMENT filename='Drag and Drop State Machine Diagram.png']
5. Click the diagram symbol in the Panel to open the smart manipulator toolbar and click [ATTACHMENT filename='Eye Symbol.png'] to view the diagram in the Panel. [ATTACHMENT filename='Show Diagram Overview Content.png']
6. Drag the Classifier from the Containment tree to the Frame. [ATTACHMENT filename='Drag and Drop Classifier.png']
7. Drag the Signals from the Containment tree to the Frame to create buttons. [ATTACHMENT filename='Drag and Drop Signals.png'] After completing the steps above, the diagram should look like the one below. [ATTACHMENT filename='Model.png']
8. Drag the Frame from the Containment Tree to a Simulation Configuration. [ATTACHMENT filename='Drag and Drop Frame on SimConfig.png']
9. In the Specification window of the Simulation Configuration, set the Show UI As HTML property to true .
10. Run the Simulation Configuration to simulate the created UI Mockup in the embedded browser. [ATTACHMENT filename='Embedded Browser.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Behavioral diagrams (Activity diagrams, State Machine diagrams, and Sequence Diagrams) and Composite Structure diagrams (Internal Block diagrams) can be embedded into HTML-generated user interfaces and animated.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b3eda1cc-d707-49f0-9011-f68b11c52b7a"><ac:rich-text-body>Composite Structure diagrams only display activations and not run-time values.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Creating a UI Mockup with a State Machine Diagram</h3><p>The Joystick.mdzip sample model is used to demonstrate how to create a UI Mockup with a State Machine Diagram.</p><p><br /></p><p>To create a UI Mockup with a State Machine Diagram</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="bac564f3-dcdb-4683-91bf-5f42ab7c60fb">Open the <em>&lt;modeling_tool_install_dir&gt;\samples\simulation\Joystick.mdzip</em> sample model.</li><li data-uuid="75b506a8-e31f-4f97-8543-48c6eba8c913">Create a User Interface Modeling Diagram.</li><li data-uuid="91a62db8-1dcb-4552-b3fe-517046266895">In the diagram, create a Frame and create a Panel inside the Frame. To learn more about the components of a User Interface Modeling Diagram, refer to the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="UI modeling diagram simulation" /></ac:link>.</li><li data-uuid="464c39b4-5a44-4dd9-895b-1cc9d0b11e06">Drag a State Machine Diagram to the Panel using a long drop and select the <strong>Create Diagram Knob Symbol</strong> command.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:height="157"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Drag and Drop State Machine Diagram.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li data-uuid="fc05716e-cdec-45aa-b146-c50b0298f228">Click the diagram symbol in the Panel to open the smart manipulator toolbar and click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="20"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Eye Symbol.png" /></ac:image>to view the diagram in the Panel.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:height="142"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Show Diagram Overview Content.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li data-uuid="ec202050-fdd4-4807-a7ba-c206a5f59370">Drag the Classifier from the Containment tree to the Frame.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Drag and Drop Classifier.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li data-uuid="01310b0a-9e30-4dc1-91e9-e19dd9d23fd5">Drag the Signals from the Containment tree to the Frame to create buttons.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Drag and Drop Signals.png" /></ac:image><br /><br />After completing the steps above, the diagram should look like the one below.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Model.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li data-uuid="bfc1e979-0c78-44e8-9a54-00456a8cf186">Drag the Frame from the Containment Tree to a Simulation Configuration.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Drag and Drop Frame on SimConfig.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li data-uuid="f655047d-6623-410c-a5b4-57aaf5f81202">In the Specification window of the Simulation Configuration, set the <strong>Show UI As HTML</strong> property to <em>true</em>.</li><li data-uuid="dab9b7ad-72d5-46b3-b813-746b48aadcfa">Run the Simulation Configuration to simulate the created UI Mockup in the embedded browser.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Embedded Browser.png" /></ac:image></li></ol><p><br /></p><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570323 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00587: Disabling updates in Simulation panes

- page_id: `249570323`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570323/Disabling+updates+in+Simulation+panes
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-09-28T10:11:17.968+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation debugging
- labels: ['turning-off-updates-in-simulation-pane', 'disabling-updates-in-simulation-pane']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can turn off automatic updates of panes in the **Simulation** window by clicking [IMAGE alt='' src='']. Turning off auto-updates of panes will cause all of the panes (Sessions, Console, Variables, and Breakpoints) in the Simulation window to be disabled and the simulation speed to increase. This can be seen in the comparison of the Simulation window before turning off the auto-update option and after turning off the auto-update option in the following figures respectively.

The following figure shows when the auto-update of panes is enabled

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Simulation window of the Calculator.mdzip sample with the auto-update of panes option is enabled.

The following figure shows when the auto-update of panes is disabled

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Simulation window of the Calculator.mdzip sample with the auto-update of panes option is disabled.

After the auto-update of panes option is disabled, you can work on the model, make changes, or run it, but the actions that you are performing will not be displayed in any of the panes in the Simulation window. If you enable the option, the Actions that you have performed will appear in the panes.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can turn off automatic updates of panes in the <strong>Simulation</strong> window by clicking <span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Disable_autoupdate_button.png" /></ac:image></span>. Turning off auto-updates of panes will cause all of the panes (Sessions, Console, Variables, and Breakpoints) in the Simulation window to be disabled and the simulation speed to increase. This can be seen in the comparison of the Simulation window before turning off the auto-update option and after turning off the auto-update option in the following figures respectively.</p><p>The following figure shows when the auto-update of panes is enabled</p><p><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="1200"><ri:attachment ri:filename="LockUpdate_button.png" /></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Simulation window of the Calculator.mdzip sample with the auto-update of panes option is enabled. </h6><p>The following figure shows when the auto-update of panes is disabled</p><p><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="1200"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DisabledUpdates.png" /></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Simulation window of the Calculator.mdzip sample with the auto-update of panes option is disabled.</h6><p>After the auto-update of panes option is disabled, you can work on the model, make changes, or run it, but the actions that you are performing will not be displayed in any of the panes in the Simulation window. If you enable the option, the Actions that you have performed will appear in the panes.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570098 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00588: Displaying simulation information

- page_id: `249570098`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570098/Displaying+simulation+information
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2026-06-08T16:10:01.395+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Animation > Simulation information in diagrams
- labels: ['active-states-in-diagrams', 'active-state-images', 'runtime-values-in-diagrms', 'flowing-information-in-diagrams', 'animation-options']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

When simulating a model, you can choose to display simulation information not only in the Simulation window but in diagrams as well. For example, you can see runtime values directly on Part shapes or a flowing item with its name on a path.

#### INFO: Types of diagrams

Types of diagrams

You can display simulation information only in diagrams that are based on a Composite Structure Diagram. However, the flowing information on paths can also be shown in an Activity Diagram.

##### Displaying active States

In diagrams, active States can be displayed on the shapes ofall initialized Parts a similar way they are displayed on the Variables pane of the Simulation window (in square brackets).

#### NOTE: Exception

Exception

Active States are not displayed for Parts with the multiplicity of more than one.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The diagram illustrates how active States are displayed on Part shapes.

If you do not want to see active States in diagrams during simulation, you can disable the **Show Active States on Part Shapes**animation option. For more information, see .

##### Displaying active State images

You can display active State images on Part shapes by applying an image to a State or by assigning an Image Switcher to the Simulation Configuration from which you execute your model. When States change during simulation, State images change as well.

#### NOTE: Important

Important

If you specify State images both by applying them to States and assigning an Image Switcher to the Simulation Configuration, the images of the Image Switcher will be used when running the Simulation Configuration.

To display active State images when executing a model

1. Open the Specification window of the State you want to create an image for.
2. Click the specification cell of the Image property and select the image you want to use for a specific State. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='image_library.png'] to select an image from the default image library, click [ATTACHMENT filename='custom_image.png'] to select an image from your file system, or click [ATTACHMENT filename='image_URL.png'] to specify an image URL.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Here you can see how active State images are displayed when simulating a model.

If you run a Simulation Configuration, use an Image Switcher to display active State images as described below.

To display active State images when running a Simulation Configuration

1. Open the Simulation Configuration Diagram in which the Simulation Configuration is displayed and create an Image Switcher.
2. In the Specification window of the Image Switcher, click the Represents property specification cell and select the element for the states of which you want to create images.
3. For every active State image you want to create, create an Active Image element as the attribute of the Image Switcher. Creating Active Image attributesTo create an Active Image attribute for an Image Switcher, click **Active Image** in the diagram palette, then click the Image Switcher shape.
4. In the Specification window of every Active Image specify the following properties:
  - **Image** property - select the image you want to use for a specific State. Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to select an image from the default image library, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to select an image from your file system, or click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to specify an image URL.
  - **Active Element** property - select the State which the Active Image represents.
5. Drag the Image Switcher created in step 1 to the Simulation Configuration shape. This way the Image Switcher is specified as the value of the UI property of the Simulation Configuration.

##### Displaying runtime values

In diagrams, you can display the runtime values of Part properties and Port labels the same way they are displayed in the Variables pane of the Simulation window. Runtime values are displayed next to property names on Part shapes and Port labels, instead of the default property values. Note that when you run a simulation, property types are not displayed.

Runtime values can be displayed only for the following types of properties:

- Primitive
- ValueType
- Enumeration
- Custom DataType

#### INFO: Automated Requirement verification

Automated Requirement verification

Runtime values on Part shapes are highlighted in green (the Requirement is satisfied) or red (the Requirement is not satisfied) when the automated Requirement verification is performed.

In addition, a tooltip with the text of unsatisfied Requirements or constraints appears when you hover the mouse pointer over Part or Value Property shapes. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### In this diagram, you can see how runtime values are displayed on Part shapes during model simulation.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### In this diagram, you can see how runtime values are displayed on Port labels during model simulation.

#### TIP: Resizing shapes to fit runtime values

Resizing shapes to fit runtime values

If a runtime value does not fit in a shape and is cut off, resize the shape as described below.

1. Select the shape that you want to resize.
2. Do one of the following:
  1. Click the Resize to Fit Runtime Values icon on the shape (displayed below). [ATTACHMENT filename='resizing_shapes.png']
  2. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='resize_to_fit_runtme_values.png'] in the diagram toolbar.

In addition, you can [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Manipulating simulation information' space=''] directly in diagrams and change simulation results in real-time. If you do not want to see runtime values in diagrams during simulation, you can disable the animation options. For more information, see .

##### Displaying flowing information

You can choose to display flowing information on animated paths when executing a model. In diagrams based on a Composite Structure Diagram, the flowing item is displayed as a moving triangle with a label showing the item name (see the image below). In Activity Diagrams, the flowing item is displayed as a bubble.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### In this Internal Block Definition Diagram, you can see the *neutral* signal flowing from the *GearShift* to the *TCU*.

You can also [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Manipulating simulation information' space=''] directly from a Part shape in a diagram and see the flowing item on a path. If you do not want to see flowing information in diagrams during simulation, you can disable the **Show Flowing Information** animation option. For more information, see .

##### Enabling and disabling animation options

You can disable or enable animation options, such as displaying active States and runtime values on Part shapes. The steps below explain how to change option values for the whole project and a specific Simulation Configuration. Note that by default all simulation animation options are enabled.

****

To disable or enable animation options for a project

1. In the main menu, go to Options > Project .
2. On the left side of the Project Options dialog, expand the General option group and select Simulation . [ATTACHMENT filename='Project Options.png']
3. In the option specification area on the right side of the dialog, disable or enable the following animation options:
  - Show Annotations- if the option set to true , the simulation annotations in the diagrams are enabled . Set the option to false to disable it.
  - Show Active States on Part Shapes - if the option set to true , active States are displayed on Part shapes. Set the option to false to disable it.
  - Show Runtime Values on Part Shapes - if the option set to true , runtime values are displayed on Part shapes. Set the option to false to disable it.
  - **Show Runtime Values on Port Labels**- if the option set to true , runtime values are displayed on Port labels. Set the option to false to disable it.
  - Show Flowing Information - if the option set to true , the label of a flowing item is shown on paths. If the option is set to false , paths are only highlighted when an item is flowing.
  - Show Active State Images on Part Shapes - if the option set to true , active State images are displayed on Part shapes.
4. Click OK to save the changes.

To disable or enable animation options for a Simulation Configuration

1. Open the Specification window of a Simulation Configuration element. [ATTACHMENT filename='Simulation Configuration.png']
2. Disable or enable the following animation options located in the Animation category:
  - Show Annotations- if the option set to true , the simulation annotations in the diagrams are enabled . Set the option to false to disable it.
  - Show Active States on Part Shapes - if the option set to true , active States are displayed on Part Property shapes. Set the option to false to disable it.
  - **Show Active State Images**- Select**On Part shapes**to show active State images on Part shapes. Select**In the Simulation UI**to show active State images in the simulation UI dialog. To show images both on Part shapes and in the simulation UI dialog, select**Both**.
  - Show Runtime Values on Part Shapes - if the option set to true , runtime values are displayed on Part shapes. Set the option to false to disable it.
  - **Show Runtime Values on Port Labels**- if the option set to true , runtime values are displayed on Port labels. Set the option to false to disable it.
  - Show Flowing Information - if the option set to true , the label of a flowing item is shown on paths. If the option is set to false , paths are only highlighted when an item is flowing.
3. Close the Specification window.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>When simulating a model, you can choose to display simulation information not only in the Simulation window but in diagrams as well. For example, you can see runtime values directly on Part shapes or a flowing item with its name on a path.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b7ccb91d-9af8-48b0-844b-b94332515836"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Types of diagrams</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can display simulation information only in diagrams that are based on a Composite Structure Diagram. However, the flowing information on paths can also be shown in an Activity Diagram.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><h3>Displaying active States</h3><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span>In diagrams, active States can be displayed on the shapes of</span><span> all initialized Parts a similar way they are displayed on the Variables pane of the Simulation window (in square brackets).</span><br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e80180d2-eafd-427f-bbb6-394ed6909ce1"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Exception</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Active States are not displayed for Parts with the multiplicity of more than one.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p style="text-align: center;"><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="displaying_active_states.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The diagram illustrates how active States are displayed on Part shapes.</h6><p>If you do not want to see active States in diagrams during simulation, you can disable the <strong>Show Active States on Part Shapes </strong>animation option. For more information, see <ac:link ac:anchor="Disabling and enabling animation options" />.<br /><br /></p><h3>Displaying active State images</h3><p>You can display active State images on Part shapes by applying an image to a State or by assigning an Image Switcher to the Simulation Configuration from which you execute your model. When States change during simulation, State images change as well.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dd7fafed-9d4e-4eb2-bb42-aa26442bd219"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Important</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you specify State images both by applying them to States and assigning an Image Switcher to the Simulation Configuration, the images of the Image Switcher will be used when running the Simulation Configuration.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p>To display active State images when executing a model</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the Specification window of the State you want to create an image for.</li><li>Click the specification cell of the <strong>Image</strong> property and select the image you want to use for a specific State. Click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="11"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image_library.png" /></ac:image> to select an image from the default image library, click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="11"><ri:attachment ri:filename="custom_image.png" /></ac:image> to select an image from your file system, or click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="11"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image_URL.png" /></ac:image> to specify an image URL.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="displaying_active_state_image.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Here you can see how active State images are displayed when simulating a model.</h6><p>If you run a Simulation Configuration, use an Image Switcher to display active State images as described below.</p><p><br /></p><p>To display active State images when running a Simulation Configuration</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the Simulation Configuration Diagram in which the Simulation Configuration is displayed and create an Image Switcher.</li><li>In the Specification window of the Image Switcher, click the <strong>Represents</strong> property specification cell and select the element for the states of which you want to create images.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">For every active State image you want to create, create an Active Image element as the attribute of the Image Switcher.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3aad6edb-c8c2-4d60-af53-8ce5d842075a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Creating Active Image attributes</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>To create an Active Image attribute for an Image Switcher, click <strong>Active Image</strong> in the diagram palette, then click the Image Switcher shape.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the Specification window of every Active Image specify the following properties:</p><ul><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Image</strong> property - select the image you want to use for a specific State. Click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="11"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image_library.png" /></ac:image> to select an image from the default image library, click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="11"><ri:attachment ri:filename="custom_image.png" /></ac:image> to select an image from your file system, or click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="11"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image_URL.png" /></ac:image> to specify an image URL.</p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Active Element</strong> property - select the State which the Active Image represents.</p></li></ul></li><li>Drag the Image Switcher created in step 1 to the Simulation Configuration shape. This way the Image Switcher is specified as the value of the <strong>UI</strong> property of the Simulation Configuration.</li></ol><h3><br />Displaying runtime values</h3><p>In diagrams, you can display the runtime values of Part properties and Port labels the same way they are displayed in the Variables pane of the Simulation window. Runtime values are displayed next to <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">property names on Part shapes and Port labels, instead of the default property values. Note that when you run a simulation, property types are not displayed</span>. </p><p>Runtime values can be displayed only for the following types of properties:</p><ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Primitive                                                                                                                                                                                                   </span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">ValueType</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Enumeration</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Custom DataType<br /><br /></span></li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a9e64253-ef5e-4f1b-b993-3655a8f6799c"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Automated Requirement verification</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Runtime values on Part shapes are highlighted in green (the Requirement is satisfied) or red (the Requirement is not satisfied) when the automated Requirement verification is performed.</p><p>In addition, a tooltip with the text of unsatisfied Requirements or constraints appears when you hover the mouse pointer over Part or Value Property shapes.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="tooltip_for_unsatisfied_requirements.png" /></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="displaying_runtime_values.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">In this diagram, you can see how runtime values are displayed on Part shapes during model simulation.</h6><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Port Labels_Runtime values.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">In this diagram, you can see how runtime values are displayed on Port labels during model simulation.</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cb88eaa9-0561-4390-b038-4e380dd212c9"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Resizing shapes to fit runtime values</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If a runtime value does not fit in a shape and is cut off, resize the shape as described below.</p><ol><li>Select the shape that you want to resize.</li><li>Do one of the following:<ol><li>Click the <strong>Resize to Fit Runtime Values</strong> icon on the shape (displayed below).<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="resizing_shapes.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="resize_to_fit_runtme_values.png" /></ac:image> in the diagram toolbar.</li></ol></li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>In addition, you can <ac:link ac:anchor="Changing runtime values"><ri:page ri:content-title="Manipulating simulation information" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[modify runtime values]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> directly in diagrams and change simulation results in real-time. If you do not want to see runtime values in diagrams during simulation, you can disable the animation options. For more information, see <ac:link ac:anchor="Disabling and enabling animation options" />.</p><h3><span>Displaying flowing information</span></h3><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">You can choose to display flowing information on animated paths when executing a model. In diagrams based on a Composite Structure Diagram, the flowing item is displayed as a moving triangle with a label showing the item name (see the image below). In Activity Diagrams, the flowing item is displayed as a bubble.</span></p><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><span><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="displaying_flowing_information.png" /></ac:image><br /></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">In this Internal Block Definition Diagram, you can see the <em>neutral</em> signal flowing from the <em>GearShift</em> to the <em>TCU</em>.</h6><p>You can also <ac:link ac:anchor="Sending a trigger"><ri:page ri:content-title="Manipulating simulation information" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[send a trigger]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> directly from a Part shape in a diagram and see the flowing item on a path. If you do not want to see flowing information in diagrams during simulation, you can disable the <strong>Show Flowing Information</strong> animation option. For more information, see <ac:link ac:anchor="Disabling and enabling animation options" />.<br /><br /></p><h3>Enabling and disabling animation options</h3><p>You can disable or enable animation options, such as displaying active States and runtime values on Part shapes. The steps below explain how to change option values for the whole project and a specific Simulation Configuration. Note that by default all simulation animation options are enabled.</p><p><strong> </strong></p><p>To disable or enable animation options for a project</p><hr /><ol><li>In the main menu, go to <strong>Options</strong> &gt; <strong>Project</strong>.</li><li>On the left side of the <strong>Project Options</strong> dialog, expand the <strong>General</strong> option group and select <strong>Simulation</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Project Options.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>In the option specification area on the right side of the dialog, disable or enable the following animation options:<br /><ul><li><strong>Show Annotations-</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172B4D);"> </span>if the option set to <em>true</em>, <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">the simulation annotations in the diagrams are enabled</span>. Set the option to <em>false</em> to disable it.</li><li><strong>Show Active States on Part Shapes</strong> - if the option set to <em>true</em>, active States are displayed on Part shapes. Set the option to <em>false</em> to disable it.</li><li><strong>Show Runtime Values on Part Shapes</strong> - if the option set to <em>true</em>, runtime values are displayed on Part shapes. Set the option to <em>false</em> to disable it.</li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172B4D);"><strong>Show Runtime Values on Port Labels</strong>- </span>if the option set to <em>true</em>, runtime values are displayed on Port labels. Set the option to <em>false</em> to disable it.</li><li><strong>Show Flowing Information</strong> - if the option set to <em>true</em>, the label of a flowing item is shown on paths. If the option is set to <em>false</em>, paths are only highlighted when an item is flowing.</li><li><strong>Show Active State Images on Part Shapes </strong>- if the option set to <em>true</em>, active State images are displayed on Part shapes.</li></ul></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong> to save the changes.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To disable or enable animation options for a Simulation Configuration</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the Specification window of a Simulation Configuration element.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Simulation Configuration.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Disable or enable the following animation options located in the <strong>Animation</strong> category:<br /><ul><li><strong>Show Annotations-</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172B4D);"> </span>if the option set to <em>true</em>, <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">the simulation annotations in the diagrams are enabled</span>. Set the option to <em>false</em> to disable it.</li><li><strong>Show Active States on Part Shapes</strong> - if the option set to <em>true</em>, active States are displayed on Part Property shapes. Set the option to <em>false</em> to disable it.</li><li><p><strong>Show Active State Images</strong><span> - Select </span><strong>On Part shapes</strong><span> to show active State images on Part shapes. Select </span><strong>In the Simulation UI</strong><span> to show active State images in the simulation UI dialog. To show images both on Part shapes and in the simulation UI dialog, select </span><strong>Both</strong><span>.</span></p></li><li><strong>Show Runtime Values on Part Shapes</strong> - if the option set to <em>true</em>, runtime values are displayed on Part shapes. Set the option to <em>false</em> to disable it.</li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172B4D);"><strong>Show Runtime Values on Port Labels</strong>- </span>if the option set to <em>true</em>, runtime values are displayed on Port labels. Set the option to <em>false</em> to disable it.</li><li><strong>Show Flowing Information</strong> - if the option set to <em>true</em>, the label of a flowing item is shown on paths. If the option is set to <em>false</em>, paths are only highlighted when an item is flowing.</li></ul></li><li>Close the Specification window.</li></ol><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570917 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00589: Distribution Extensions

- page_id: `249570917`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570917/Distribution+Extensions
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:20.273+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation of SysML models
- labels: ['non-normative-extensions']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A Distributed Property is a property of a Block or a Value Type used to apply a probability distribution to the values of the property. Specific distributions can be defined by applying a Subclass of the «DistributedProperty» stereotype to a property according to[OMG SysML 1.4](http://sysml.org/docs/specs/OMGSysML-v1.4-15-06-03.pdf), E.7 Distribution Extensions. The Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports three types of distributions:Uniform, Normal, and Triangular.

To set a distributed property

1. Select a property and double-click it to open the Specification window.
2. Select Applied Stereotype and click [...] to include a distribution property.
3. Specify the required properties. These include **Mean**, **Standard Deviation**, etc. for «normal» and **Min**, **Max**, etc. for other distributed properties. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Click Close . The distributed property will be applied.
5. Run the simulation model. Depending on the applied stereotype, the distributed properties will be initialized with a random value, such as normal distribution, constant distribution between min & max values. You can review sampling results by running the model with association end multiplicity (e.g., 100), and keep the result with «CSVExport» for analysis.
6. Results of the distributed property «normal» (with 100000 samples) with a Mean value of 10 and a Standard Deviation value of 2. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. You can apply a «uniform» distributed property stereotype with Min and Max properties (e.g., 0 and 100) to plot a uniform distribution chart. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
8. You can apply a «triangular» distributed property stereotype with Min, Max, and Peak properties to plot a triangular distribution chart. 
The sample project used is *<modeling_tool_installation_directory>\samples\simulation\AircraftProjectAnalysis.mdzip*. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Supported SysML elements' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Requirements traceability from the Variables pane' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

Specific distributions can be defined by applying a Subclass of the «DistributedProperty» stereotype to a property according to </span><a style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;" href="http://sysml.org/docs/specs/OMGSysML-v1.4-15-06-03.pdf">OMG SysML 1.4</a><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">, E.7 Distribution Extensions. The Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports t</span><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">hree types of distributions: </span><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">Uniform, Normal, and Triangular.</span></p><p class="Standard"><br /></p><p class="Standard">To set a distributed property</p><hr /><ol><li>Select a property and double-click it to open the Specification window.</li><li>Select <strong>Applied Stereotype</strong> and click [...] to include a distribution property.</li><li><p>Specify the required properties. These include <strong>Mean</strong>, <strong>Standard Deviation</strong>, etc. for «normal» and <strong>Min</strong>, <strong>Max</strong>, etc. for other distributed properties.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="non-normativeExt.PNG" /></ac:image><br /><br /></p></li><li>Click <strong>Close</strong>. The distributed property will be applied.</li><li>Run the simulation model. Depending on the applied stereotype, the distributed properties will be initialized with a random value, such as normal distribution, constant distribution between min &amp; max values. You can review sampling results by running the model with association end multiplicity (e.g., 100), and keep the result with «CSVExport» for analysis.</li><li><p style="text-align: left;">Results of the distributed property «normal» (with 100000 samples) with a Mean value of 10 and a Standard Deviation value of 2.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Distribution.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></p></li><li><p><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">You can apply a «uniform» distributed property stereotype with Min and Max properties (e.g., 0 and 100) to plot a uniform distribution chart.</span><br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Unifrom_Distribution.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></p></li><li><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">You can apply a «triangular» distributed property stereotype with Min, Max, and Peak properties to plot a triangular distribution chart.<br />The sample project used is <em style="text-align: left;">&lt;modeling_tool_installation_directory&gt;\samples\simulation\AircraftProjectAnalysis.mdzip</em>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Triangular_Distribution.png" /></ac:image></span></li></ol></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Supported SysML elements" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Requirements traceability from the Variables pane" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570520 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00590: Dummy tokens of Actions through Output Pins

- page_id: `249570520`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570520/Dummy+tokens+of+Actions+through+Output+Pins
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2021-11-22T08:02:03.498+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Activity simulation > Execution of incomplete or dummy models
- labels: ['dummy-tokens']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1918270852

1918270855

1918270854

Simulation Toolkit has an option to support dummy tokens of CallBehaviorActions that do not have a Behavior. Business-level abstract diagrams can be executed and animated because Simulation will automatically create new objects according to type of those Output Pins if unspecified, even though this action will break fUML semantics. You can set the **Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin** option (true by default) in the **Project Options Simulation** dialog.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Objects are automatically created according to type of unspecified Output Pins, e.g., *order*.

From the figure above, the Output Pin returns an object (*ro* is *order@203c1eec*). If the **Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin** option is set false, the Output Pin will have no value.

To set the **Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin** option

1. From the main menu, click Options > Project . The Project Options dialog opens.
2. On the left pane, click **Simulation**.
3. Under the **fUML Engine** group, set the **Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin** option to **true**.
4. Select **OK**. [ATTACHMENT filename='Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin.png'] The Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin option in the Project Options Simulation dialog.

1918270850

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Execution of incomplete or dummy models' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity simulation' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f2506be0-3743-47cb-8617-703db7498485"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1918270852</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="468b24ee-7774-4dde-b64b-a3229ce758d2"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1918270855</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="55c592be-5669-4206-a9dd-076a634a537b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1918270854</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Simulation Toolkit has an option to support dummy tokens of CallBehaviorActions that do not have a Behavior. Business-level abstract diagrams can be executed and animated because Simulation will automatically create new objects according to type of those Output Pins if unspecified, even though this action will break fUML semantics. You can set the <strong>Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin</strong> option (true by default) in the <strong>Project Options Simulation</strong> dialog.</p><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Dummy Token of Action through Output Pin.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Objects are automatically created according to type of unspecified Output Pins, e.g., <em>order</em>.</h6><p>From the figure above, the Output Pin returns an object (<em>ro</em> is <em>order@203c1eec</em>). If the <strong>Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin</strong> option is set false, the Output Pin will have no value.</p><p><br /></p><p>To set the <strong>Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin</strong> option</p><hr /><ol><li>From the main menu, click <strong>Options</strong> &gt;<strong> Project</strong>. The <strong>Project Options</strong> dialog opens.</li><li><p>On the left pane, click <strong>Simulation</strong>.</p></li><li><p>Under the <strong>fUML Engine</strong> group, set the <strong>Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin</strong> option to <strong>true</strong>.</p></li><li><p>Select <strong>OK</strong>.<br /><br /></p><ac:image ac:width="700"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin.png" /></ac:image><br /><h6>The Auto Create fUML Object of Output Pin option in the Project Options Simulation dialog.</h6></li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ece79763-d0bd-4ecd-ab7f-ce9f27dabee7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1918270850</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Execution of incomplete or dummy models" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Activity simulation" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570480 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00591: Duration analysis

- page_id: `249570480`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570480/Duration+analysis
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:12.539+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Activity simulation
- labels: ['duration-analysis']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit is capable of calculating the minimum, maximum, and average duration of any Activity. It uses the values taken from duration constraints applied to each element in an Activity as the basis to calculate the Activity's duration.

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit can perform an analysis of activity duration in the two following scenarios

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

It uses the values taken from duration constraints applied to each element in an Activity as the basis to calculate the Activity's duration. </p><p>Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit can perform an analysis of activity duration in the two following scenarios</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="2f38baab-e7a6-4770-aab1-ca7e9f439e4b" /></p><p><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570486 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00592: Duration analysis on executed traces

- page_id: `249570486`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570486/Duration+analysis+on+executed+traces
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-09-29T11:07:20.516+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Activity simulation > Duration analysis
- labels: ['executed-trace-duration']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

144626827

144626829

144626828

You can also perform an analysis of Activity duration after an Activity has been simulated. Prior to the analysis, be sure that the simulation log has been configured and the value of a Record Activation tag is **true** (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Simulation log' space=''] for more information about the simulation log).

To see the duration of a visited element

1. Right-click the simulation log and select **Simulation** > **Analyze Duration** **of** **Executed Trace**. The **Select Execution** **Session** **and** **Activity** dialog will open (see the second figure below). [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Analyze Duration of Executed Trace Menu on the Simulation Log Context Menu. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Select Execution Session and Activity dialog.
2. Select a session and an Activity and click the **Calculate Duration** button. The result will appear in the Simulation **Console** pane. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Duration of executed trace in the Simulation Console pane.

144626826

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Duration analysis' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Duration analysis on visited elements' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570481 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00593: Duration analysis on visited elements

- page_id: `249570481`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570481/Duration+analysis+on+visited+elements
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-09-29T11:07:38.523+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Activity simulation > Duration analysis
- labels: ['visited-element-duration']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

144449098

144449101

144449099

The duration analysis on visited elements feature is not enabled by default. To enable it, you need a TimeHandler and register it in the execution listener of the <<SimulationConfig>>. You can create the TimeHandler by creating a new Class and apply the <<TimeHandler>> stereotype.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Enabling duration analysis on visited elements in the <<SimulationConfig>>.

You can view the duration of a running Activity by using the diagram's context menu **Analyze Duration** **of** **Visited** **Elements**.

To see the duration of visited elements

1. Pause a currently running Activity by either adding a breakpoint or clicking the Pause button.
2. Right-click the diagram and select **Simulation** > **Analyze Duration****of****Visited****Elements**. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Analyze duration on visited elements menu on the diagram context menu.
3. The minimum, maximum, and average duration of the visited elements together with the simulated trace listing all of the visited elements will appear in the Simulation **Console** pane. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Duration of visited elements in the Simulation Console.

144449097

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Duration analysis' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Duration analysis on executed traces' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570575 space=MMA version=3 -->
## PAGE 00594: Duration constraint

- page_id: `249570575`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570575/Duration+constraint
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-09-12T15:08:40.213+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Interaction simulation > Supported elements in interaction simulation
- labels: ['duration-constraint']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

380966344

380966346

380966345

Simulation allows you to evaluate duration constraints between Call Messages and Reply Messages, e.g., you can specify that a Call Message must get a Reply Message in 500 ms, so the actual duration of operation method execution must be compared with the duration constraint of 500 ms. The comparison is done as an evaluation of the constraint and will generate a fail value for *testcases* or stop at breakpoints on the [Constraint Failure As Breakpoint option](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/SimulationConfig+stereotype). However, it does not support the delay time of a message.

The default time unit of the duration constraint is millisecond (ms), but you can also use other time units by specifying them after the duration value, e.g., you can use 10s to denote 10 seconds. If you specify both the minimum and maximum values, Simulation will use the maximum value of the duration constraint to delay the delivery of a Message.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A Sequence diagram showing how a Duration Constraint {500} can be used to evaluate between the Call Message and Reply Message.

The figure above shows that *cons* sends a Call Message to *bank* to verify an account (12345678) with an expected duration constraint of 500 ms from the Reply Message. The evaluation in the first run is failed because the actual duration (431 ms) is less than 500 ms, while another run passes because the actual duration (710 ms) is greater than 500 ms.

380966342

**Related pages**

- Message
- Internal simulation clock
- Specification window

The comparison is done as an evaluation of the constraint and will generate a fail value for <em>testcases</em> or stop at breakpoints on the <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/SimulationConfig+stereotype">Constraint Failure As Breakpoint option</a>. However, it does not support the delay time of a message.</p><p>The default time unit of the duration constraint is millisecond (ms), but you can also use other time units by specifying them after the duration value, e.g., you can use 10s to denote 10 seconds. If you specify both the minimum and maximum values, Simulation will use the maximum value of the duration constraint to delay the delivery of a Message.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sequence Diagram.PNG" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">A Sequence diagram showing how a Duration Constraint {500} can be used to evaluate between the Call Message and Reply Message.</h6><p>The figure above shows that <em>cons</em> sends a Call Message to <em>bank</em> to verify an account (12345678) with an expected duration constraint of 500 ms from the Reply Message. The evaluation in the first run is failed because the actual duration (431 ms) is less than 500 ms, while another run passes because the actual duration (710 ms) is greater than 500 ms.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="034ebc7d-ba8c-46c8-ba73-bfcfcceaf3ed"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">380966342</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/Message">Message</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/Internal+simulation+clock">Internal simulation clock</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Specification+window">Specification window</a></li></ul><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570741 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00595: Dynamic constraint

- page_id: `249570741`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570741/Dynamic+constraint
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2026-04-13T12:33:27.387+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator
- labels: ['dynamic-constraint']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The concept of dynamic constraint is to allow parametric calculation of a Constraint Property with another constraint rather than one from its type (Constraint Block). Such constraint can be dynamically obtained during the simulation from typing Constraint Block or one of its subtypes. This section uses the *ForwardContractValuation.mdzip* sample to demonstrate this concept.

To dynamically apply different constraints to a Constraint Property

1. Create a Constraint Block with all needed Constraint Parameter(s).
2. Create Constraint Blocks which inherit the Constraint Block created in step 1.
3. Specify the constraint expression of each Constraint Blocks created in step 2.
4. Type the Constraint Property with the Constraint Block created in step 1.
5. Create a behavior, for example State Machine, that will assign the runtime object of different Constraint Blocks created in step 2 to the Constraint Property.

In *ForwardContractValuation.mdzip*, we will dynamically assign a constraint to the **Valuation_Rule** Constraint Property, which is typed by the **Valuation** Constraint Block (having two subtypes: **Long** **Valuation** and **Short** **Valuation** Constraint Blocks with two different constraint expressions) as shown in the following figure. The context of this simulation will be the **System** Block.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
A Valuation Constraint Block as the Type of the Valuation_Rule Constraint Property.

In the following figure, the state machine is used as the classifier behavior of the **System** block. The **Calculate Contract Value** **for Long** **Position** and **Calculate Contract Value** **for Short** **Position** states apply different valuations to the **System** block via its entry activity.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
System Classifier Behavior State Machine.

The **Calculate Contract Value** **for Long** **Position**'s entry activity assigns the runtime value of the **Long** **Valuation** Constraint Block to the **Valuation_Rule** Constraint Property (see the first figure below). Consequently, the values of the constraint parameters will be calculated with the constraint expression specified in **Long** **Valuation**.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Entry Activity of Calculate Contract Value for Long Position State.

Similarly, the second figure below demonstrates the **Short** **Valuation** dynamic assignment. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Entry Activity of Calculate Contract Value for Short Position State.

#### Sample model

The model used in the figures on this page is the *ForwardContractValuation.mdzip* sample model that comes with the modeling tool. You can find it in *<*install_root*>\samples\simulation\Parametrics\ForwardContractValuation.mdzip.*

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying the language for the expression' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Value binding' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Evaluating expressions' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Manual value updates using the Parametric Evaluator' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Communicating with evaluators through simulation console' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Built-in Math' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Integration with external Evaluators' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Trade study analysis' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sample project' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

Such constraint can be dynamically obtained during the simulation from typing Constraint Block or one of its subtypes. This section uses the <em>ForwardContractValuation.mdzip</em> sample to demonstrate this concept. </p><p>To dynamically apply different constraints to a Constraint Property</p><hr /><ol><li>Create a Constraint Block with all needed Constraint Parameter(s).</li><li>Create Constraint Blocks which inherit the Constraint Block created in step 1.</li><li>Specify the constraint expression of each Constraint Blocks created in step 2.</li><li>Type the Constraint Property with the Constraint Block created in step 1.</li><li>Create a behavior, for example State Machine, that will assign the runtime object of different Constraint Blocks created in step 2 to the Constraint Property. </li></ol><p>In <em>ForwardContractValuation.mdzip</em>, we will dynamically assign a constraint to the <strong>Valuation_Rule</strong> Constraint Property, which is typed by the <strong>Valuation</strong> Constraint Block (having two subtypes: <strong>Long</strong> <strong>Valuation</strong> and <strong>Short</strong> <strong>Valuation</strong> Constraint Blocks with two different constraint expressions) as shown in the following figure. The context of this simulation will be the <strong>System</strong> Block. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="A Valuation Constraint Block as the Type of the Valuation_Rule Constraint Property" ac:alt="A Valuation Constraint Block as the Type of the Valuation_Rule Constraint Property"><ri:attachment ri:filename="dynamicConstraint01.png" /></ac:image><br />A Valuation Constraint Block as the Type of the Valuation_Rule Constraint Property.</h6><p>In the following figure, the state machine is used as the classifier behavior of the <strong>System</strong> block. The <strong>Calculate Contract Value</strong> <strong>for Long</strong> <strong>Position</strong> and <strong>Calculate Contract Value</strong> <strong>for Short</strong> <strong>Position</strong> states apply different valuations to the <strong>System</strong> block via its entry activity. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="System Classifier Behavior State Machine" ac:alt="System Classifier Behavior State Machine"><ri:attachment ri:filename="dynamicConstraint02.png" /></ac:image><br />System Classifier Behavior State Machine.</h6><p>The <strong>Calculate Contract Value</strong> <strong>for Long</strong> <strong>Position</strong>'s entry activity assigns the runtime value of the <strong>Long</strong> <strong>Valuation</strong> Constraint Block to the <strong>Valuation_Rule</strong> Constraint Property (see the first figure below). Consequently, the values of the constraint parameters will be calculated with the constraint expression specified in <strong>Long</strong> <strong>Valuation</strong>. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Entry Activity of Calculate Contract Value for Long Position State" ac:alt="Entry Activity of Calculate Contract Value for Long Position State"><ri:attachment ri:filename="dynamicConstraint03.png" /></ac:image><br />Entry Activity of Calculate Contract Value for Long Position State.</h6><p>Similarly, the second figure below demonstrates the <strong>Short</strong> <strong>Valuation</strong> dynamic assignment. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Entry Activity of Calculate Contract Value for Short Position State" ac:alt="Entry Activity of Calculate Contract Value for Short Position State"><ri:attachment ri:filename="dynamicConstraint04.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Entry Activity of Calculate Contract Value for Short Position State.</h6><h2>Sample model</h2><p>The model used in the figures on this page is the <em>ForwardContractValuation.mdzip</em> sample model that comes with the modeling tool. You can find it in <em>&lt;<em style="text-align: left;">install_root</em>&gt;\samples\simulation\Parametrics\ForwardContractValuation.mdzip.</em></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specifying the language for the expression" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Value binding" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Evaluating expressions" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Manual value updates using the Parametric Evaluator" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Communicating with evaluators through simulation console" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Built-in Math" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Integration with external Evaluators" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Trade study analysis" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Sample project" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
