<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249569555 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00596: Elements simulation

- page_id: `249569555`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249569555/Elements+simulation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:05.021+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Model simulation
- labels: ['element-simulation', 'simulating-elements', 'unrestored-unknown-attachment']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1331316376

1331316378

1331316375

**On this page**

1331316377

You can run a model simulation through the shortcut menu and the diagram toolbar.

To run a model simulation through the shortcut menu

1. Do one of the following:
  - Right-click an element shape on a diagram and select Simulation > Run . [ATTACHMENT filename='Figure_0001.png']
  - Right-click an element in the Containment tree and select Simulation > Run. [ATTACHMENT filename='Figure_0002.png']
2. In the toolbar of the open Simulation window, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to run the model. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To run a model through the diagram toolbar

1. Do any of the following:
  - In the diagram toolbar, click the Run or Run with Context button to run a diagram whose context is Class or Behavior (Composite Structure diagram, Activity diagram, State-Machine diagram, Use Case diagram, or Sequence diagram). Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will run the class or the behavior which is the context of the diagram. [ATTACHMENT filename='running_from_diagram_toolbar.png']
  - In a Class or a Package diagram, select the element you want to run and click the Run or Run with Context button on the diagram toolbar. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will run the selected element on the diagram. [ATTACHMENT filename='running_from_diagram_toolbar2.png']
2. In the toolbar of the open Simulation window, click the Start button to run the model.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8b328fcb-44bb-42d1-a7c0-e3fbe0622a56"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1331316376</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2061f100-5be3-4010-b1fa-51178b4afe08"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1331316378</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="74b44605-2988-44e2-91b5-1696a701f5f7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1331316375</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="a720081e-f587-4415-bee9-4eb7ac008297" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="86fb79fd-6ed9-48b9-9320-4b9b791f2771"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1331316377</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can run a model simulation through the shortcut menu and the diagram toolbar.</p><p><br /></p><p>To run a model simulation through the shortcut menu</p><hr /><ol><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li>Right-click an element shape on a diagram and select <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Run</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Running a Model through the Context Menu in a Diagram " ac:alt="Running a Model through the Context Menu in a Diagram "><ri:attachment ri:filename="Figure_0001.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Right-click an element in the Containment tree and select <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Run.<br /></strong><br /><ac:image ac:title="Running a Model through the Context Menu in the Containment Browser" ac:alt="Running a Model through the Context Menu in the Containment Browser"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Figure_0002.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li></ul></li><li>In the toolbar of the open <strong>Simulation</strong> window, click<span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> </span><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size" style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="IconRun.png" /></ac:image></span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> to run the model.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="starting_simulation (2).png" /></ac:image><br /></span></li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To run a model through the diagram <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="69c5ca91-630c-4064-bc6f-b57fd4de282a">toolbar</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><hr /><ol><li>Do any of the following:<br /><ul><li>In the diagram toolbar, click the <strong>Run</strong> or <strong>Run</strong> <strong>with</strong> <strong>Context</strong> button to run a diagram whose context is Class or Behavior (Composite Structure diagram, Activity diagram, State-Machine diagram, Use Case diagram, or Sequence diagram). Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will run the class or the behavior which is the context of the diagram.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="running_from_diagram_toolbar.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>In a Class or a Package diagram, select the element you want to run and click the <strong>Run</strong> or <strong>Run</strong> <strong>with</strong> <strong>Context</strong> button on the diagram toolbar. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will run the selected element on the diagram.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="running_from_diagram_toolbar2.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li></ul></li><li>In the toolbar of the open <strong>Simulation</strong> window, click the <strong>Start</strong> button to run the model. </li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

## PAGE 00597: Evaluating an expression

- space_key: `MMA`

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can use the following API to evaluate an expression

```text

```

The following code fragment shows how to evaluate expressions in ALH API:

```text

```

If no language is defined, the default language will be used (JavaScript Rhino).

The following scripting languages are available by default:

- BeanShell Engine [beanshell, bsh]
- Built-in Math [Built-in Math, math, Math Engine, math engine, built-in]
- Groovy Scripting Engine [groovy, Groovy]
- JEXL Engine [JEXL, Jexl, jexl, JEXL2, Jexl2, jexl2]
- JavaScript Rhino [rhino, rhino-nonjdk, js, JavaScript, javascript, ECMAScript, ecmascript, JavaScript Rhino]
- jython [python, jython]

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
ALH.evaluate("language", "expression")
]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The following code fragment shows how to evaluate expressions in ALH API:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="98841b13-e01d-4feb-a58b-fc2d375b13ca"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[result = ALH.evaluate("a+b");
result = ALH.evaluate("Python", "a+b")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>If no language is defined, the default language will be used (JavaScript Rhino).</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="00d7b7a5-90f8-45e3-b8ca-09d42aebd349"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The following scripting languages are available by default:</p><ul><li>BeanShell Engine [beanshell, bsh]</li><li>Built-in Math [Built-in Math, math, Math Engine, math engine, built-in]</li><li>Groovy Scripting Engine [groovy, Groovy]</li><li>JEXL Engine [JEXL, Jexl, jexl, JEXL2, Jexl2, jexl2]</li><li>JavaScript Rhino [rhino, rhino-nonjdk, js, JavaScript, javascript, ECMAScript, ecmascript, JavaScript Rhino]</li><li>jython [python, jython]</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570707 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00598: Evaluating expressions

- page_id: `249570707`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570707/Evaluating+expressions
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:15.277+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator
- labels: ['expression-evaluation', 'evaluating-expressions']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

402791423

402791426

1240126456

**On this page**

4

402791425

This section explains how to evaluate the mathematical equations and logical expressions in Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit.

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit provides constraint parameters substitution in the expressions to improve the human readability of the constraint parameters. All user interface objects (including tooltips, Console panel, and Variables panel related to a constraint property connected to a value property) will display the value property name (actual binding) instead of the constraint parameter name. This feature is applicable at the user interface level only, not at the original constraint property. For example, “Width” and “Height” (value properties) are used instead of “w” and “h” (parameter properties) in constraints, as shown in the following figure.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Constraint parameters substitution in expressions.

##### Parametric updates when States change

Simulation can check the object State during the evaluation. For example, if an elevator is moving, the door must be closed, or if the car is moving, the speed must be more than 0.

- Y ou can use [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Action scripts APIs' space=''] (or _*context*_ ) in the Specification of a constraint in order to access the runtime object of the owner of the Constraint property.
- If the State check, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Checking Behaviors' space=''] , is used in the Specification of a constraint, the constraint will be re-evaluated on a State change with the parametric context and any objects passed as parameters into the constraint. The State change supported on simple, composite, orthogonal, and submachine.
- The Parametric update occurs after the State activation, so it depends on the selected [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State activation semantics' space=''] option in the Project Option dialog:
  - 
    - If Before entry (by default) is set, the following sequence applies: the State will be activated, the Parametric will be updated, and the Entry behavior will be simulated.
    - If After entry is set, the following sequence applies: the Entry behavior will be simulated, the state will be activated, and the Parametric will be updated.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The constraint re-evaluated on a State change in the Parametric of the context object

##### Parametric updates when Activities start or end

Simulation can check whether there is a specified Activity being executed at that time in Object_ by using the **performs(activity String)** function, e.g., *self.performs("Activity_1")* will return **true** when *Activity_1* is being executed under itself and return **false** if it is not executed under other Object_ or anywhere else. When the condition expression is evaluated at the first time, a listener for Activity execution will register the specified Activity in the *performs("Activity_1")* function. The listener will re-evaluate the condition expression when the context object starts or ends executing Activities.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e4f42111-687a-4748-b5d3-81a587afae04"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402791423</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c45b9e53-7c68-40ef-9eaf-7b925e0725fe"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402791426</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c24489a2-4d22-42bb-9442-a563aa2cc2ce"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1240126456</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8cc367ee-3cd0-4caf-91db-cd6a844fef3f"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="66bb8bf7-3b02-4c09-b2aa-d8e6dc365783"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402791425</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="Textbody">This section explains how to evaluate the mathematical equations and logical expressions in Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit.</p><p class="Textbody">Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit provides constraint parameters substitution in the expressions to improve the human readability of the constraint parameters. will be updated.</span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><br /></span><br /><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><br /></span></li></ul></li></ul></li></ul><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="1338"><ri:attachment ri:filename="SIM-16490.PNG" /></ac:image><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="380768b9-4762-48ca-9fa4-f9a31ee56770">The constraint re-evaluated on a State change in the Parametric of the contex</ac:inline-comment-marker>t object</h6><h3 style="text-align: left;">Parametric updates when Activities start or end</h3><p>Simulation can check whether there is a specified Activity being executed at that time in Object_ by using the <strong>performs(activity String)</strong> function, e.g., <em>self.performs(&quot;Activity_1&quot;)</em> will return <strong>true</strong> when <em>Activity_1</em> is being executed under itself and return <strong>false</strong> if it is not executed under other Object_ or anywhere else. When the condition expression is evaluated at the first time, a listener for Activity execution will register the specified Activity in the <em>performs(&quot;Activity_1&quot;)</em> function. The listener will re-evaluate the condition expression when the context object starts or ends executing Activities.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570764 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00599: Evaluating strings from command input

- page_id: `249570764`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570764/Evaluating+strings+from+command+input
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-10-03T05:13:00.251+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator > Built-in Math
- labels: ['evaluating-strings']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can type generic mathematical equations directly in command input of the Simulation Console when it prompts for the built-in math. The examples are as follows

- math» x = 10;
- math» y = 20;
- math» z = x + y

z = 30.0000 (the calculation result) will be displayed on the simulation console.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

The result of a calculation expression that ends with a semicolon (;) in the built-in Math will be assigned to the corresponding variable in the selected built-in math environment. It will not be displayed in the simulation Console tab.

Or, if you type, e.g., in command input of the Simulation Console, the examples are as follows

- math» a = true;
- math» b = false;
- math» c = a & b;

If **false** is the result of a calculation, it will be assigned to the variable c, but it will not be displayed in the simulation Console tab.

If an expression does not contain any assignment operators, the result will be assigned to the variable 'ans'. The examples are as follows

- math» x = 10;
- math» 20 + x
- ans = 30.0000 will be displayed in the simulation Console tab.

You can calculate multiple expressions at the same time by ending each expression with a semicolon (;). The examples are as follows

- math» x = 10; y = 20; z = x + y; a = z / x
- a = 3 will be displayed in the simulation Console tab.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can type generic mathematical equations directly in command input of the Simulation Console when it prompts for the built-in math. The examples are as follows </p><ul><li>math» x = 10;</li><li>math» y = 20;</li><li>math» z = x + y </li></ul><p style="margin-left: 90.0px;"><br class="atl-forced-newline _mce_tagged_br" />z = 30.0000 (the calculation result) will be displayed on the simulation console. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e7dc5773-083b-42e2-adb0-4f38479bcf86"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The result of a calculation expression that ends with a semicolon (;) in the built-in Math will be assigned to the corresponding variable in the selected built-in math environment. It will not be displayed in the simulation Console tab. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" />Or, if you type, e.g., in command input of the Simulation Console, the examples are as follows</p><ul><li>math» a = true;</li><li>math» b = false;</li><li>math» c = a &amp; b; </li></ul><p>If <strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">false</strong> is the result of a calculation, it will be assigned to the variable c, but it will not be displayed in the simulation Console tab. </p><p>If an expression does not contain any assignment operators, the result will be assigned to the variable 'ans'. The examples are as follows</p><ul><li>math» x = 10;</li><li>math» 20 + x</li><li>ans = 30.0000 will be displayed in the simulation Console tab. </li></ul><p>You can calculate multiple expressions at the same time by ending each expression with a semicolon (;). The examples are as follows</p><ul><li>math» x = 10; y = 20; z = x + y; a = z / x </li><li>a = 3 will be displayed in the simulation Console tab. </li></ul><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249571079 space=MMA version=3 -->
## PAGE 00600: Excel Helper APIs

- page_id: `249571079`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249571079/Excel+Helper+APIs
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-09-12T15:08:38.636+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Action languages > Action scripts APIs
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit provides APIs to import data from an Excel file into runtime values, and export the runtime values to an Excel file. Either a «DiagramTableMapToDataSource», an Instance Table linked to an Excel file, or an[«ImportMap»](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Saving+an+Import+Map)can be used as the*mappingElement*.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The provided APIs in Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit are given in the following pages

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit provides APIs to import data from an Excel file into runtime values, and export the runtime values to an Excel file. <span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">Either a «DiagramTableMapToDataSource», an Instance Table linked to an Excel file, or an </span><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Saving+an+Import+Map">«ImportMap»</a><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);"> can be used as the<span> </span></span><em style="text-align: left;">mappingElement</em><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">.</span></p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:height="178"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Excel Helper APIs.png" /></ac:image></p><p><br /></p><p>The provided APIs in Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit are given in the following pages</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="7ca20eb3-a3ed-4464-a16f-0c04fff1269b" /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570453 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00601: Executing Activities

- page_id: `249570453`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570453/Executing+Activities
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2021-12-03T03:03:45.181+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Activity simulation
- labels: ['activity-execution', 'executing-activities']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

143637310

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

143637312

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

143637311

You can add some breakpoints to the model created when [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a model for Activity simulation' space=''] before executing it. This section demonstrates how to suspend the model simulation at some specific points with breakpoints. You can use either the diagram or browser context menu to add a breakpoint to an element.

The following example shows you how to add breakpoints to pins **a** and **b** of the action **print**. Once the model simulation has reached these pins, the simulation will be suspended.

To add a breakpoint to an element and simulate the model

1. Right-click an element and select **Simulation** > **Add****Breakpoint(s)**. The breakpoints will be shown in the **Breakpoints** pane of the Simulation window. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Breakpoints pane in the Simulation window. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Clicking Window > Simulation on the main menu to open the Simulation window.

2. Right-click **instance** in the containment browser and select **Simulation** > **Run** to simulate the model from **instance**, which is the InstanceSpecification of the **SumPrinter** Classifier.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

3. A new simulation session will be created and displayed in the **Sessions** pane of the Simulation window. The symbol of the elements with breakpoints attached will be highlighted in yellow by default. Click the **Start** button on the **Simulation** window toolbar to animate the simulation on the **Print-Sum** Activity diagram.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

4. The simulation will be suspended when pin **a** or **b** of the **print** action is activated. You can hover your mouse pointer over the active element to see its runtime value.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

5. Click the **Resume** button on the **Simulation** window toolbar to continue the simulation.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

6. The simulation will be suspended again when pin **b** is activated. Click the **Resume** button to continue the simulation. In the **Console** pane of the **Simulation** window, you can see the printed value of 10, which is the summation between 2 and 8.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### NOTE: Note

Note

If you do not want to display animation (silent simulation), you can create **Simulation****Configuration** to customize the simulation, select **instance** as the **simulationTarget**, and set **silent** to **true**. See [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Running a Simulation Configuration' space=''] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Simulation Configuration and UI modeling' space=''] for more information.

143637308

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Running a Simulation Configuration' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a model for Activity simulation' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Simulation Configuration and UI modeling' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2978dbf0-a19a-4100-9f4c-1fd625785c46"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">143637310</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a5339d0d-54f6-4c90-a2c9-66c3c558d6b7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">143637312</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c0d81100-9c3e-4890-8d05-f2acadcfa12d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">143637311</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can add some breakpoints to the model created when <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ac:link> before executing it. This section demonstrates how to suspend the model simulation at some specific points with breakpoints. You can use either the diagram or browser context menu to add a breakpoint to an element. </p><p>The following example shows you how to add breakpoints to pins <strong>a</strong> and <strong>b</strong> of the action <strong>print</strong>. Once the model simulation has reached these pins, the simulation will be suspended. </p><p><br /></p><p>To add a breakpoint to an element and simulate the model</p><hr /><ol><li><p>Right-click an element and select <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Add</strong><strong>Breakpoint(s)</strong>. The breakpoints will be shown in the <strong>Breakpoints</strong> pane of the Simulation window.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Adding Breakpoints to Pin a of print Action" ac:alt="Adding Breakpoints to Pin a of print Action"><ri:attachment ri:filename="AddBreakPointAtPinA.png" /></ac:image></h6><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Breakpoints Pane in the Simulation Window" ac:alt="The Breakpoints Pane in the Simulation Window"><ri:attachment ri:filename="AddBreakpointsToAandB.png" /></ac:image></h6><h6>The Breakpoints pane in the Simulation window.</h6><h6><ac:image ac:title="Opening the Simulation Window from the Main Menu" ac:alt="Opening the Simulation Window from the Main Menu"><ri:attachment ri:filename="WindowSimulationContextMenu.png" /></ac:image></h6></li></ol><h6 style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Clicking Window &gt; Simulation on the main menu to open the Simulation window.</h6><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">2. Right-click <strong>instance</strong> in the containment browser and select <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Run</strong> to simulate the model from <strong>instance</strong>, which is the InstanceSpecification of the <strong>SumPrinter</strong> Classifier.</p><h6 style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Executing the InstanceSpecification of the SumPrinter Classifier" ac:alt="Executing the InstanceSpecification of the SumPrinter Classifier"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ExecuteInstanceSpecification.png" /></ac:image></h6><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">3. A new simulation session will be created and displayed in the <strong>Sessions</strong> pane of the Simulation window. The symbol of the elements with breakpoints attached will be highlighted in yellow by default. Click the <strong>Start</strong> button on the <strong>Simulation</strong> window toolbar to animate the simulation on the <strong>Print-Sum</strong> Activity diagram.</p><h6 style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="A Simulation Session in the Sessions Pane of the Simulation Window" ac:alt="A Simulation Session in the Sessions Pane of the Simulation Window"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Figure_0079.png" /></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">4.  The simulation will be suspended when pin <strong>a</strong> or <strong>b</strong> of the <strong>print</strong> action is activated. You can hover your mouse pointer over the active element to see its runtime value.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Simulation Is Suspended When Pin a Is Activated" ac:alt="The Simulation Is Suspended When Pin a Is Activated"><ri:attachment ri:filename="SuspendExecutionAtA.png" /></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">5. Click the <strong>Resume</strong> button on the <strong>Simulation</strong> window toolbar to continue the simulation.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Resume Button in the Simulation Window" ac:alt="The Resume Button in the Simulation Window"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ResumeToolbarButton.png" /></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">6. The simulation will be suspended again when pin <strong>b</strong> is activated. Click the <strong>Resume</strong> button to continue the simulation. In the <strong>Console</strong> pane of the <strong>Simulation</strong> window, you can see the printed value of 10, which is the summation between 2 and 8. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Console Pane of the Simulation Window Showing the Printed Result of the Summation" ac:alt="The Console Pane of the Simulation Window Showing the Printed Result of the Summation"><ri:attachment ri:filename="110195_PrintSum.png" /></ac:image></h6><p> </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="655cdf81-caa8-4949-af0d-1e20a9ec966e"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you do not want to display animation (silent simulation), you can create <strong>Simulation </strong><strong>Configuration</strong> to customize the simulation, select <strong>instance</strong> as the <strong>simulationTarget</strong>, and set <strong>silent</strong> to <strong>true</strong>. See <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Running a Simulation Configuration" /></ac:link> and <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Simulation Configuration and UI modeling" /></ac:link> for more information.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="491b3a64-616a-4592-8c9d-fce1054abbc5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">143637308</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Running a Simulation Configuration" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Simulation Configuration and UI modeling" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570629 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00602: Executing an interaction model

- page_id: `249570629`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570629/Executing+an+interaction+model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-10-02T07:16:05.458+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Interaction simulation
- labels: ['interaction-model-execution']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This section illustrates the steps to simulate the interaction model mentioned in section [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a model for Activity simulation' space='']. 
 
To simulate the interaction model in section [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a model for Activity simulation' space='']

1. Right-click the package **Model** in the containment browser and select **Create Element** > **Package**. Name the package, for example, InstancePackage. The following figure shows the Containment tree containing the objects within its hierarchy. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Right-click the **InstancePackage** and select **Create** **Element** > **Instance** **Specification**. Name the instance specification, for example, Sys. The following figure shows the creation of an InstancePackage element from its shortcut menu.. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Right-click the instance specification Sys and select Specification to open the specification dialog.
4. Click the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] browse button in the **Classifier** row to open the **Select Elements** dialog (see the first figure below) and select class **System** as the classifier of the instance specification **Sys** (see the second figure below). [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The following figure shows the elements that can be selected in the **Select Elements** dialog. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Repeat steps 2 and 3 above to create the other instance specifications named **aa1** and **bb1** of the classifiers **A** and **B** respectively. The following figure shows the Containment tree depicting objects within its hierarchy. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
6. Right-click the instance specification Sys and select Simulation > Run (see the first figure below). The Simulation window will appear. [ATTACHMENT filename='InteractionSimulation29.png']
7. Click the **Start** button in the **Simulation** window. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Simulation **Console** shows the results of a simulation and the constraints which have passed or failed. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>This section illustrates the steps to simulate the interaction model mentioned in section <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Creating a Model for Activity Simulation]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To simulate the interaction model in section <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating a model for Activity simulation" /></ac:link> </p><hr /><ol><li><p>Right-click the package <strong>Model</strong> in the containment browser and select <strong>Create Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Package</strong>. Name the package, for example, InstancePackage. The following figure shows the Containment tree containing the objects within its hierarchy.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Creating Package Named InstancePackage" ac:alt="Creating Package Named InstancePackage"><ri:attachment ri:filename="InteractionSimulation24.png" /></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Right-click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">InstancePackage</strong> and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Create</strong> <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Element</strong> &gt; <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Instance</strong> <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Specification</strong>. Name the instance specification, for example, Sys. The following figure shows the creation of an InstancePackage element from its shortcut menu..</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Creating Instance Specification Named Sys" ac:alt="Creating Instance Specification Named Sys"><ri:attachment ri:filename="InteractionSimulation25.png" /></ac:image></h6></li><li>Right-click the instance specification <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Sys</strong> and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Specification</strong> to open the specification dialog.</li><li><p>Click the <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="2015-12-02 15_31_56-Specification of State __.png" /></ac:image> browse button in the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Classifier</strong> row to open the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Select Elements</strong> dialog (see the first figure below) and select class <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">System</strong> as the classifier of the instance specification <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Sys</strong> (see the second figure below). </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Specification Of Instance" ac:alt="Specification Of Instance"><ri:attachment ri:filename="spec-of-instance-specification.png" /></ac:image></h6><p>The following figure shows the elements that can be selected in the <strong>Select Elements</strong> dialog.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Selecting System Class as the Classifier of the Instance Specification Sys in the Select Elements Dialog" ac:alt="Selecting System Class as the Classifier of the Instance Specification Sys in the Select Elements Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="select-elements-dialog.png" /></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Repeat steps 2 and 3 above to create the other instance specifications named <strong>aa1</strong> and <strong>bb1</strong> of the classifiers <strong>A</strong> and <strong>B</strong> respectively. The following figure shows the Containment tree depicting objects within its hierarchy.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Creating Instance Specifications aa1 and bb1 of the Classifiers A and B Respectively" ac:alt="Creating Instance Specifications aa1 and bb1 of the Classifiers A and B Respectively"><ri:attachment ri:filename="InteractionSimulation28.png" /></ac:image></h6></li><li>Right-click the instance specification <strong>Sys</strong> and select <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Run</strong> (see the first figure below). The <strong>Simulation</strong> window will appear.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Executing the Instance Specification Sys Simulation through the Context Menu" ac:alt="Executing the Instance Specification Sys Simulation through the Context Menu"><ri:attachment ri:filename="InteractionSimulation29.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li><p>Click the <strong>Start</strong> button in the <strong>Simulation</strong> window.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Running the Simulation of the Instance Specification Sys in the Simulation Window" ac:alt="Running the Simulation of the Instance Specification Sys in the Simulation Window"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ExecuteInteraction_1.png" /></ac:image></h6><p>The Simulation <strong>Console</strong> shows the results of a simulation and the constraints which have passed or failed.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="ExecuteInteraction_2.png" /></ac:image></p></li></ol><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249571409 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00603: Executing the StopWatch class

- page_id: `249571409`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249571409/Executing+the+StopWatch+class
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-10-09T03:29:08.443+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Tutorial > Stopwatch model sample > Executing the stopwatch model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The StopWatch Class is now ready to be executed. You can execute the StopWatch Class either from the containment browser or from the diagram.

To execute the StopWatch Class

1. Either (i) select the StopWatch class in the containment tree and click the Run button on the Simulation Control main toolbar or (ii) right-click the StopWatch class and select Simulation > Run . [ATTACHMENT filename='shortcutmenu_run.png']
2. The Simulation window will open. It contains four tabs: (i) Sessions , (ii) Console , (iii) Variables , and (iv) Breakpoints tabs. At this point, a simulation session to execute the StopWatch Class will be created. You can see this session in the Sessions tab of the Simulation window. [ATTACHMENT filename='simulation_window.png']
3. Click the **Start** button [ATTACHMENT filename='start_button.png'] on the Simulation window toolbar.

Once you have clicked the **Run** **Execution** button, the Object of the **StopWatch** Class will be created and shown in the **Variables** tab. The Classifier Behavior of the **StopWatch** class and the **StopWatch** State Machine will be executed respectively. A new session to execute the **StopWatch** State Machine will be created under the simulation session of the **StopWatch** class, and the **StopWatch** State Machine diagram will be open and ready for the simulation.

The **StopWatch** State Machine execution will start from the initial stage and automatically move to the **ready** state because the transition which connects between the initial stage and **ready** state does not have a defined trigger. When the **ready** State is entered, the **reset** Activity, which is assigned as the entry to the **ready** State, will be invoked. This Activity also calls the **resetTime** activity of the StopWatch Class for setting the time value to be zero. At this point, a new simulation session will be created to execute the **resetTime** Activity, and the **resetTime** Activity diagram will be open and animated.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 
 The execution of the **resetTime** Activity will be terminated once it has reached the final stage. When the simulation stops, the session executing the **resetTime** Activity will be closed, the StopWatch State Machine diagram will be reactivated, and the simulation will wait for an Event signal at the ready State. You can browse the **StopWatch** object in the **Variables** tab to see the time value.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 
 Now we want to trigger the StopWatch object from the ready State to the running State. To do this, we need to send a start signal to the StopWatch object. 
 
To send an event (start) signal to the object of the **StopWatch**Class

1. Select the StopWatch node in the Variables tab. The signals that can be sent to the StopWatch will be listed in the Trigger drop-down menu.
2. Select the start signal from the Triggers drop-down menu (see the following figure).

#### NOTE: Note

Note

You can click the arrow button on the left or right-hand side of the **Simulation** window toolbar to scroll the toolbar to the left or to the right.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 Once the start Signal has been sent, the **StopWatch** will go to the **running** State. At the entry to the **running** State, the **increase** Activity will be defined; therefore, it will be invoked. The **increase** Activity will then call the **increaseTime** Activity of the **StopWatch** Class to increment the time value by one second. A new simulation session will be created to execute the **increaseTime** Activity, and the **increaseTime** Activity diagram will be open and animated. 
 [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 
 The **increaseTime** Activity will be completely executed when its Final stage has been reached. Once the execution is complete, the simulation session of the **increaseTime** Activity will be closed and the **StopWatch** State Machine will be activated again. Now you can see in the **Variables** tab that the time value of the StopWatch object has been incremented by one.

The StopWatch object will still be at the **running** State and the time Event will be triggered at this State every second. Therefore, a new simulation session will be created to execute the **increaseTime** activity every second, and the time value will increment by one every time the increaseTime Activity is executed.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 
 While the StopWatch object is at the **running** State, we can send a **split** signal to trigger the object to the **paused** State or send a **stop** signal to trigger the object to the **stopped** State. 
 
 To send a split signal to the StopWatch object

1. Select the StopWatch object in the Variables tab.
2. Select the split signal from the Triggers drop-down menu on the Simulation window toolbar. The state of the StopWatch object will be changed to the paused State.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 You can change the paused StopWatch object to the **running** State by sending an **unsplit** signal to the object. 
 
To change the StopWatch object from a paused state to a running State

1. Select the StopWatch object in the Variables tab.
2. Select the unsplit signal from the Triggers drop-down menu on the Simulation window toolbar. The state of the StopWatch object will be changed to the running State, and the time value will continuously increase. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav6240bec3246339c7c6e2a46d39e55614.png']

To send a stop Signal to the StopWatch object

1. Select the StopWatch object in the Variables tab.
2. Select the stop signal from the Triggers drop-down menu on the Simulation window toolbar. The State of the StopWatch object will be changed to the stopped State. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavaf4fd7ef425f080b2957e3bda2118678.png']

At the **stopped** State, if the **reset** signal is sent to the StopWatch object, the object will go to the **ready** State, and the time value will be reset to zero at the entry to the **ready** state by calling the **resetTime** Activity. If the **stop** signal is sent to the object again, the object will go to the final stage, and then the simulation will stop and all of the simulation sessions will be closed.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The StopWatch Class is now ready to be executed. You can execute the StopWatch Class either from the containment browser or from the diagram. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br /></p><p>To execute the StopWatch Class</p><hr /><ol><li>Either (i) select the <strong>StopWatch</strong> class in the containment tree and click the <strong>Run</strong> button on the <strong>Simulation </strong><strong>Control</strong> main toolbar or (ii) right-click the <strong>StopWatch</strong> class and select <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Run</strong>. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Shortcut Menu Run" ac:alt="Shortcut Menu Run"><ri:attachment ri:filename="shortcutmenu_run.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> window will open. It contains four tabs: (i) <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Sessions</strong>, (ii) <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Console</strong>, (iii) <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Variables</strong>, and (iv) <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Breakpoints</strong> tabs. At this point, a simulation session to execute the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> Class will be created. You can see this session in the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Sessions</strong> tab of the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> window.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Simulation Console" ac:alt="Simulation Console"><ri:attachment ri:filename="simulation_window.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click the <span style="line-height: 20.0px;"><strong>Start</strong></span> button <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="start_button.png" /></ac:image> on the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> window toolbar. </li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Once you have clicked the <strong>Run</strong> <strong>Execution</strong> button, the Object of the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class will be created and shown in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab. The Classifier Behavior of the <strong>StopWatch</strong> class and the <strong>StopWatch</strong> State Machine will be executed respectively. A new session to execute the <strong>StopWatch</strong> State Machine will be created under the simulation session of the <strong>StopWatch</strong> class, and the <strong>StopWatch</strong> State Machine diagram will be open and ready for the simulation.</p><p>The <strong>StopWatch</strong> State Machine execution will start from the initial stage and automatically move to the <strong>ready</strong> state because the transition which connects between the initial stage and <strong>ready</strong> state does not have a defined trigger. When the <strong>ready</strong> State is entered, the <strong>reset</strong> Activity, which is assigned as the entry to the <strong>ready</strong> State, will be invoked. This Activity also calls the <strong>resetTime</strong> activity of the StopWatch Class for setting the time value to be zero. At this point, a new simulation session will be created to execute the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity, and the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity diagram will be open and animated.</p><p><br /> <ac:image ac:title="Executing the ResetTime Activity" ac:alt="Executing the ResetTime Activity"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav0d2d0cab3d8ad736e9f36d62ab703bb2.png" /></ac:image><br /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> The execution of the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity will be terminated once it has reached the final stage. When the simulation stops, the session executing the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity will be closed, the StopWatch State Machine diagram will be reactivated, and the simulation will wait for an Event signal at the ready State. You can browse the <strong>StopWatch</strong> object in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab to see the time value.</p><p><br /><ac:image ac:title="(Upper) The Reset Activity Invoked at the Entry of the Ready State and (Lower) Browsing the StopWatch Object in the Variables Tab for the Time Value" ac:alt="(Upper) The Reset Activity Invoked at the Entry of the Ready State and (Lower) Browsing the StopWatch Object in the Variables Tab for the Time Value"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav710b7b9c304fbabadfdd01d17a76b53d.png" /></ac:image><br /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Now we want to trigger the StopWatch object from the ready State to the running State. To do this, we need to send a start signal to the StopWatch object. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To send an event (start) signal to the object of the <strong>StopWatch </strong>Class</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the <strong>StopWatch</strong> node in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab. The signals that can be sent to the <strong>StopWatch</strong> will be listed in the <strong>Trigger</strong> drop-down menu.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">start</strong> signal from the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Triggers</strong> drop-down menu (see the following figure). </li></ol><p> </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="356a0572-f189-4c33-9671-47aa551d6212"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can click the arrow button on the left or right-hand side of the <strong>Simulation</strong> window toolbar to scroll the toolbar to the left or to the right.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /> <ac:image ac:title="Selecting the Start Signal from the Triggers Drop-down Menu" ac:alt="Selecting the Start Signal from the Triggers Drop-down Menu"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav26b7e0c79977913f0326c7502218e610.png" /></ac:image><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Once the start Signal has been sent, the <strong>StopWatch</strong> will go to the <strong>running</strong> State. At the entry to the <strong>running</strong> State, the <strong>increase</strong> Activity will be defined; therefore, it will be invoked. The <strong>increase</strong> Activity will then call the <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity of the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class to increment the time value by one second. A new simulation session will be created to execute the <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity, and the <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity diagram will be open and animated.<br /> <ac:image ac:title="The Execution of the IncreaseTime Activity (Upper) and the Time Value of the StopWatch Object Incrementing by One (Lower) " ac:alt="The Execution of the IncreaseTime Activity (Upper) and the Time Value of the StopWatch Object Incrementing by One (Lower) "><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav01fbe03f0d8da98602e00cbb751b5fb1.png" /></ac:image><br /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> The <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity will be completely executed when its Final stage has been reached. Once the execution is complete, the simulation session of the <strong>increaseTime</strong> Activity will be closed and the <strong>StopWatch</strong> State Machine will be activated again. Now you can see in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab that the time value of the StopWatch object has been incremented by one.</p><p>The StopWatch object will still be at the <strong>running</strong> State and the time Event will be triggered at this State every second. Therefore, a new simulation session will be created to execute the <strong>increaseTime</strong> activity every second, and the time value will increment by one every time the increaseTime Activity is executed.</p><p><br /> <ac:image ac:title="Activating the Transition to Self Every Second" ac:alt="Activating the Transition to Self Every Second"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav4170bb121ff21494de21372aff8918de.png" /></ac:image><br />  <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> While the StopWatch object is at the <strong>running</strong> State, we can send a <strong>split</strong> signal to trigger the object to the <strong>paused</strong> State or send a <strong>stop</strong> signal to trigger the object to the <strong>stopped</strong> State. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> To send a split signal to the StopWatch object</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the StopWatch object in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">split</strong> signal from the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Triggers</strong> drop-down menu on the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> window toolbar. The state of the StopWatch object will be changed to the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">paused</strong> State. </li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:title="Changing the State of the StopWatch Object to the Paused State" ac:alt="Changing the State of the StopWatch Object to the Paused State"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavaf5f9748fcb0d4a3e1491cfa121f2468.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /> You can change the paused StopWatch object to the <strong>running</strong> State by sending an <strong>unsplit</strong> signal to the object. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To change the StopWatch object from a paused state to a running State</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the StopWatch object in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">unsplit</strong> signal from the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Triggers</strong> drop-down menu on the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> window toolbar. The state of the StopWatch object will be changed to the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">running</strong> State, and the time value will continuously increase. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Changing the State of the StopWatch Object from the Paused State to the Running State with Unsplit Signal" ac:alt="Changing the State of the StopWatch Object from the Paused State to the Running State with Unsplit Signal"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav6240bec3246339c7c6e2a46d39e55614.png" /></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To send a stop Signal to the StopWatch object</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the StopWatch object in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stop</strong> signal from the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Triggers</strong> drop-down menu on the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> window toolbar. The State of the StopWatch object will be changed to the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stopped</strong> State.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Changing the State of the StopWatch Object to the Stopped State by Sending the Stop Signal" ac:alt="Changing the State of the StopWatch Object to the Stopped State by Sending the Stop Signal"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavaf4fd7ef425f080b2957e3bda2118678.png" /></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> At the <strong>stopped</strong> State, if the <strong>reset</strong> signal is sent to the StopWatch object, the object will go to the <strong>ready</strong> State, and the time value will be reset to zero at the entry to the <strong>ready</strong> state by calling the <strong>resetTime</strong> Activity. If the <strong>stop</strong> signal is sent to the object again, the object will go to the final stage, and then the simulation will stop and all of the simulation sessions will be closed. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249571424 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00604: Executing the StopWatch instance specification

- page_id: `249571424`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249571424/Executing+the+StopWatch+instance+specification
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:24.763+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Tutorial > Stopwatch model sample > Executing the stopwatch model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In the previous section, we have shown you how to execute the StopWatch model from the StopWatch Class. You can also use Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit to execute the model from an InstanceSpecification. With this feature, you can specify values to the slots of an InstanceSpecification. These values will be used as the input values for the execution such as using a ReadStructuralFeatureAction to read the values.

In this tutorial, our StopWatch contains only one value property, which is time. The time value will always reset to zero whenever the system enters the ready State. If we create an InstanceSpecification of StopWatch and use it for model execution, the result will be the same as the model execution of the StopWatch Class.

To create an InstanceSpecification of StopWatch

1. Open a System Class diagram.
2. Click Instance on the Class Diagram toolbar and click the System Class diagram. A new InstanceSpecification will be created and the Select Classifier dialog will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='select-classifier-a.png']
3. Select the StopWatch Class and click OK .
4. On the Class diagram, name the created InstanceSpecification "stopwatch" . Now we will need to create values and assign them to the slots.

To create values and assign them to the slots

1. Right-click the symbol of the stopwatch InstanceSpecification and select Specification to open the Specification window of the stopwatch InstanceSpecification.
2. Select the Slots node on the left-hand side of the dialog.
3. Select the Structural Feature of the slot to which the values will be applied. In this case, select time:Integer[1] . [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav9a7cf25ce112886b657a3d1ed123cab8.png']
4. Click the Create Value button. A new integer value will be created for the slot. If an Integer value is selected, a zero will be created by default. You can modify this value by typing a new value into the Value box. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav26add523b109fefb885b3d0398fc018f.png']
5. Click Close to close the Specification window.

To start executing the stopwatch InstanceSpecification, right-click it either on the **System** Class diagram or in the containment browser and click **Execute**. A simulation session will be created in the **Sessions** tab of the Simulation window to execute this instance. The steps to run the execution are the same as those to execute the **StopWatch** Class.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>In the previous section, we have shown you how to execute the StopWatch model from the StopWatch Class. You can also use Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit to execute the model from an InstanceSpecification. With this feature, you can specify values to the slots of an InstanceSpecification. These values will be used as the input values for the execution such as using a ReadStructuralFeatureAction to read the values.</p><p>In this tutorial, our StopWatch contains only one value property, which is time. The time value will always reset to zero whenever the system enters the ready State. If we create an InstanceSpecification of StopWatch and use it for model execution, the result will be the same as the model execution of the StopWatch Class. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br /></p><p>To create an InstanceSpecification of StopWatch</p><hr /><ol><li>Open a <strong>System</strong> Class diagram.</li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Instance</strong> on the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Class Diagram</strong> toolbar and click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">System</strong> Class diagram. A new InstanceSpecification will be created and the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Select </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Classifier</strong> dialog will open.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Select Classifier Dialog" ac:alt="The Select Classifier Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="select-classifier-a.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> Class and click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">OK</strong>.</li><li>On the Class diagram, name the created InstanceSpecification <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;stopwatch&quot;</em>. Now we will need to create values and assign them to the slots.</li></ol><p> </p><p>To create values and assign them to the slots</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the symbol of the stopwatch InstanceSpecification and select <strong>Specification</strong> to open the Specification window of the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stopwatch</strong> InstanceSpecification.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Slots</strong> node on the left-hand side of the dialog.</li><li>Select the Structural Feature of the slot to which the values will be applied. In this case, select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">time:Integer[1]</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Specification Dialog of the StopWatch InstanceSpecification" ac:alt="The Specification Dialog of the StopWatch InstanceSpecification"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav9a7cf25ce112886b657a3d1ed123cab8.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Create </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Value</strong> button. A new integer value will be created for the slot. If an Integer value is selected, a zero will be created by default. You can modify this value by typing a new value into the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Value</strong> box.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Setting a Slot Value in the Specification Dialog" ac:alt="Setting a Slot Value in the Specification Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav26add523b109fefb885b3d0398fc018f.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Close</strong> to close the Specification window. </li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> To start executing the stopwatch InstanceSpecification, right-click it either on the <strong>System</strong> Class diagram or in the containment browser and click <strong>Execute</strong>. A simulation session will be created in the <strong>Sessions</strong> tab of the Simulation window to execute this instance. The steps to run the execution are the same as those to execute the <strong>StopWatch</strong> Class. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="The Simulation Window during the Execution of StopWatch Model from InstanceSpecification" ac:alt="The Simulation Window during the Execution of StopWatch Model from InstanceSpecification"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav1ed8cb9f4b7f147c9cceb298df636342.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></p><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249571430 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00606: Executing the StopWatch using Simulation Configuration

- page_id: `249571430`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249571430/Executing+the+StopWatch+using+Simulation+Configuration
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:24.943+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Tutorial > Stopwatch model sample > Executing the stopwatch model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to customize model execution by using an Simulation Configuration. The Simulation Configuration is a stereotype that can be applied to a Class Element. This stereotype contains tag definitions for model execution customization such as enable or disable animation and UI mockups. To be able to use the Simulation Configuration, we need to add the *SimulationProfile.mdzip* file to our project. 
 
 To add the *SimulationProfile.mdzip* file to a project

1. Click File > Use Project on the main menu. The Use Project wizard dialog opens.
2. Select the From predefined location option.
3. Select <install.root>\profiles in Project modules paths , and then select SimulationProfile.mdzip . [ATTACHMENT filename='Use Project Dialog.png']
4. Click Next to go to the second step, Module Settings . [ATTACHMENT filename='use-project-dialog-2.png']
5. Use the default settings and click Finish . The simulation profile will be added to the project. You can see it in the containment browser. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddavd4057523d3d2f3e88e8ca3eb06273df0.png']

An Simulation Configuration is a Class Element, which is applied with the «SimulationConfig» stereotype (see the following figure). This stereotype contains tag definitions to customize model execution (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SimulationConfig stereotype' space='']).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Next, we will create the Simulation Configuration to execute our StopWatch model without using animation. 
 
 To execute our StopWatch model without using animation

1. Open a System Class diagram.
2. Create a new Class Element by clicking the Class button on the Class diagram toolbar.
3. Click the diagram to create the Class Element.
4. Name the created Class Element "SilentStopwatch" .
5. Right-click the SilentStopwatch Class and select Stereotype to apply the «SimulationConfig» stereotype to the created Class. The Stereotype context menu group will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='Applying SimulationConfig Stereotype.png']
6. Select SimulationConfig from the list of available stereotypes for the Class Element.
7. Click Apply . The SilentStopwatch Class will be with the applied SimulationConfig stereotype. [ATTACHMENT filename='Applying SimulationConfig Stereotype Completed.png']

Next, we will create a SimulationLog to record the runtime events occurrence and assign this SimulationLog to the SimulationConfig.

To record all runtime events occurrence and assign a SimulationLog to the SimulationConfig

1. Create a new Class Element by clicking the Class button on the Class diagram toolbar.
2. Click the diagram to create the Class Element.
3. Name the created Class "SimulationLogs" .
4. Right-click the SimulationLogs Class and select Stereotype to apply the «SimulationLog» stereotype to the created Class. The Stereotype context menu will open. [ATTACHMENT filename='Applying SimulationLog Stereotype.png']
5. Select SimulationLog and click Apply . The SimulationLogs Class will be with the applied «SimulationLog» stereotype. [ATTACHMENT filename='SimulationLog Completed.png']

Now we need to specify the tagged values of the «SimulationConfig» stereotype that is applied to the SilentStopwatch Class. 
 
To specify the tagged values of the «SimulationConfig» stereotype

1. Open the Specification dialog of the SilentStopwatch Simulation Configuration by right-clicking the SilentStopwatch symbol and select Specification .
2. Select the Tags node on the left-hand side of the dialog.
3. Select SimulationProfile from the Profile drop-down menu. [ATTACHMENT filename='Specification Dialog Selecting SimulationProfile.png']
4. Specify the tagged value of the execution target to the StopWatch Class by selecting executionTarget and click Create Value . The Select Elements dialog will open for you to select the Element that will be the execution target. [ATTACHMENT filename='Select Elements Dialog for executionTarget.png']
5. Select the StopWatch Class (you can also select the stopwatch InstanceSpecification, which has been created in the previous section) as the executionTarget .
6. Click OK to close the Select Elements dialog.
7. Follow the same steps used to set the tagged value of executionTarget to the StopWatch Class and specify the tagged values of the following tag definitions i. log to SimulationLogs . ii. resultLocation to the Stopwatch folder. iii. silent to true .
8. Click Close to close the Specification dialog. [ATTACHMENT filename='Complete SimulationConfig.png']

To execute the Simulation Configuration

1. Right-click the SilentStopwatch symbol on the diagram and select Simulation > Execute .
2. Click the Run Simulation button on the Simulation window toolbar to start the simulation. The StopWatch Class will then be executed without animation. You will need to wait until the runtime object of StopWatch is at the ready State. You can see the State of the runtime object at the Variables tab. The State of the object will be displayed in square brackets.
3. Select the StopWatch node in the Variables tab.
4. Select the start signal from the Triggers drop-down menu on the Simulation window toolbar.

Now, you can see that the value of **time:Integer**, which is displayed in the **Variables** tab, will increase by one every second. 
 
To stop the execution

1. Select the StopWatch node in the Variables tab.
2. Select the stop signal from the Triggers drop-down menu. The StopWatch object will go to the stopped State.
3. Select the stop signal again to move the object to the final stage to close the simulation session. The latest value of time:Integer will be saved into the slot value of the stopwatch InstanceSpecification.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to customize model execution by using an Simulation Configuration. The Simulation Configuration is a stereotype that can be applied to a Class Element. This stereotype contains tag definitions for model execution customization such as enable or disable animation and UI mockups. To be able to use the Simulation Configuration, we need to add the <em>SimulationProfile.mdzip</em> file to our project. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> To add the <em>SimulationProfile.mdzip</em> file to a project</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>File</strong> &gt; <strong>Use</strong> <strong>Project</strong> on the main menu. The <strong>Use</strong> <strong>Project</strong> wizard dialog opens.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">From predefined </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">location</strong> option.</li><li>Select <em>&lt;install.root&gt;\profiles</em> in <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Project </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">modules </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">paths</strong>, and then select <em>SimulationProfile.mdzip</em>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Use Project Dialog.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Next</strong> to go to the second step, <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Module </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Settings</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Module Settings Pane in the Use Module Dialog" ac:alt="The Module Settings Pane in the Use Module Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="use-project-dialog-2.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Use the default settings and click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Finish</strong>. The simulation profile will be added to the project. You can see it in the containment browser. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="The Simulation Profile in the Containment Browser" ac:alt="The Simulation Profile in the Containment Browser"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavd4057523d3d2f3e88e8ca3eb06273df0.png" /></ac:image></li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> An Simulation Configuration is a Class Element, which is applied with the «SimulationConfig» stereotype (see the following figure). The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Stereotype</strong> context menu group will open.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Applying SimulationConfig Stereotype.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">SimulationConfig</strong> from the list of available stereotypes for the Class Element.</li><li>Click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Apply</strong>. The SilentStopwatch Class will be with the applied SimulationConfig stereotype.<br /><br />  <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Applying SimulationConfig Stereotype Completed.png" /></ac:image></li></ol><p><br /></p><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Next, we will create a SimulationLog to record the runtime events occurrence and assign this SimulationLog to the SimulationConfig. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br /></p><p>To record all runtime events occurrence and assign a SimulationLog to the SimulationConfig</p><hr /><ol><li>Create a new Class Element by clicking the <strong>Class</strong> button on the Class diagram toolbar.</li><li>Click the diagram to create the Class Element.</li><li>Name the created Class <em style="line-height: 1.42857;">&quot;SimulationLogs&quot;</em>.</li><li>Right-click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">SimulationLogs</strong> Class and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Stereotype</strong> to apply the «SimulationLog» stereotype to the created Class. The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Stereotype</strong> context menu will open.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Applying SimulationLog Stereotype.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">SimulationLog</strong> and click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Apply</strong>. The <strong>SimulationLogs</strong> Class will be with the applied «SimulationLog» stereotype.<br /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="SimulationLog Completed.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li></ol><p>Now we need to specify the tagged values of the «SimulationConfig» stereotype that is applied to the SilentStopwatch Class. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To specify the tagged values of the «SimulationConfig» stereotype</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the <strong>Specification</strong> dialog of the <strong>SilentStopwatch</strong> Simulation Configuration by right-clicking the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">SilentStopwatch</strong> symbol and select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Specification</strong>.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Tags</strong> node on the left-hand side of the dialog.</li><li>Select <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">SimulationProfile</strong> from the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Profile</strong> drop-down menu. <br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Specification Dialog Selecting SimulationProfile.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Specify the tagged value of the execution target to the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> Class by selecting <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">executionTarget</strong> and click <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Create</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Value</strong>.
````
