The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> Class will then be executed without animation. You will need to wait until the runtime object of <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> is at the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">ready</strong> State. You can see the State of the runtime object at the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Variables</strong> tab. The State of the object will be displayed in square brackets.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> node in the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Variables</strong> tab.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">start</strong> signal from the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Triggers</strong> drop-down menu on the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Simulation</strong> window toolbar. </li></ol><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Now, you can see that the value of <strong>time:Integer</strong>, which is displayed in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab, will increase by one every second. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To stop the execution</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the <strong>StopWatch</strong> node in the <strong>Variables</strong> tab.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stop</strong> signal from the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Triggers</strong> drop-down menu. The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">StopWatch</strong> object will go to the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stopped</strong> State.</li><li>Select the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stop</strong> signal again to move the object to the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">final</strong> stage to close the simulation session. The latest value of <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">time:Integer</strong> will be saved into the slot value of the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">stopwatch</strong> InstanceSpecification.</li></ol><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570499 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00607: Execution of incomplete or dummy models

- page_id: `249570499`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570499/Execution+of+incomplete+or+dummy+models
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:12.910+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Activity simulation
- labels: ['dummy-models', 'incomplete-model-execution']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Information

376786264

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Information

376786266

376786262

**On this page**

22

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Information

376786265

Previous versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit did not support execution of incomplete or dummy models. From the 18.4 version and above, it will be possible to execute incomplete or dummy models.

Previously, the execution would halt. Now, if any input pin does not have an accompanying value, the Behavior of the Action will not be to halt execution, instead a null value token will be passed to that pin, or when simulating a **CallOperationAction** which does not have a target pin, a runtime object, which is the context of the Activity will be selected as the target. Null Value tokens do not trigger flow animation on triggered behaviors if the input pin multiplicity's lower bound is 0.

The information on this page describes the Behavior of an Action when any of the input or output pins, or any properties of the Action, are not specified or not present.

#### INFO: Supported Actions

Supported Actions

The Actions which are currently supported are as follows

- AddStructuralFeatureValueAction
- ReadStructuralFeatureValueAction
- CallBehaviorAction
- CallOperationAction
- SendSignalAction
- ValueSpecificationAction
- StartClassifierBehaviorAction and StateObjectBehaviorAction
- OpaqueAction

#### AddStructuralFeatureValueAction

An add structural feature value Action is a write structural feature Action for adding values to a structural feature. When any input pin is unspecified or empty, or when a **Structural Feature** is unspecified or does not exist, this Action's Behaviorwill have the rules as follows

- I f Object input pin is not specified, the runtime context of the Activity execution will be used as the target.
- If Value input pin is not specified, this Action will be skipped.
- If Structural Feature is not specified or it does not exist in its own context, then this Action will be skipped.

If an **Object** input pin is empty or unspecified, the runtime context of the Activity execution will be used to add the value to the **Structural Feature** of the context.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A demo showing the situation if an object pin is unspecified.

#### ReadStructuralFeatureValueAction

A read structural feature Action is a structural feature Action that retrieves the values of a structural feature. When any of the input or output pins are unspecified or not present, this Action's Behaviorwill have the rules as follows

- If the Object pin is not specified, the runtime context of the Activity execution will be used as the target.
- If Structural Feature is not specified or if the Structural Feature has no values, then a n ull value token will be sent.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A demo showing the situation if an object pin is not specified.

#### CallBehaviorAction

Call Behavior Action is a call Action that invokes a Behavior directly rather than invoking an operation that invokes the Behavior. When any of the **Argument** pins are either unspecified or not present, this Action's Behaviorwill have the rules as follows

- If any of the Argument input pins are unspecified, this Action will send a null value token for that parameter.
- If none of the Argument pins are unspecified, this Action will send null value tokens for all the Arguments .
- If the Result output pin is specified and this Action does not return any results, it will return a null value token instead.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A demo showing the situation if an argument pin is unspecified (A null value token is passed to the pin.).

#### CallOperationAction

A call operation Action is an Action that transmits an operation call request to the target object, where it may cause the invocation of associated Behavior. When any input or output pin is unspecified or not present, this Action's Behaviorwill have the rules as follows

- This Action will send a null value token for each unspecified Argument input pin.
- In case no Argument pin is specified, this Action will send null value tokens for all the parameters.
- If the **Result** output pin is specified and this Action does not return any results, it will return a **null** value token instead.
- If the **Target** input pin is not specified, the runtime context of the Activity execution will be used as the target. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] A demo showing the situation if an argument input pin is unspecified (A null value token will be passed.).

#### SendSignalAction

Send signal Action is an invocation Action that creates a signal from its inputs, and transmits it to the specified target object, where it may cause the firing of a State Machine transition or the execution of an Activity. When either **Argument** pin or **Target** input pin is unspecified or empty, this Action's Behavior will have the rules as follows

- If there are any incomplete Argument pins, this Action will send null value tokens for those parameters.
- If Argument pin is not present, this Action will send null value tokens for all parameters.
- If Target input pin is not present, the runtime context of the Activity execution will be used as the target.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A demo showing the situation when there are incomplete argument pins.

#### ValueSpecificationAction

A value specification Action is an Action that evaluates a value specification. When the value of a **ValueSpecification** Action is not specified, this Action's Behavior will apply the rule as follows

- If the value of a ValueSpecification Action is not specified, a **null** value token will be passed.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A demo showing the situation when the value of a ValueSpecification Action is unspecified (A null value token is passed.).

#### StartClassifierBehaviorAction and StateObjectBehaviorAction

A start Classifier Behavior Action is an Action that starts the Classifier Behavior of the input. If the **Object** input pin is not present or empty, this Action's Behavior will apply the rule as follows

- If the Object input pin is not present or empty, the StartClassifierBehaviorAction Action will use the runtime context of the Activity execution as the target.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A demo showing the situation when the object pin is either not present or empty.

#### OpaqueAction

Opaque Actions are a type of Action that can be used to represent implementation information. They can also be used as placeholder Actions until you determine the specific type of Action to use. If the opaque Action does not return a value to the **OutputValue** pin, this Action's Behavior will apply the rule as follows

- If the opaque Action does not return any value to the OutputValue pin, a null value token will be passed to the pin at runtime.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### If an opaque Action does not return any value to the OutputValue pin, a null token will be passed to the pin.

#### INFO: Information

Information

A **"null value token"** is an **ObjectToken** with has no values defined.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5e8fe2e6-3547-4435-8ac7-06a67d51bef6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">376786264</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6da8c9f3-723f-4700-b00a-441d2a9480ac"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">376786266</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ea691c5f-b691-4c2d-811b-6ddf7a53906c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">376786262</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7ceba2bf-9727-4011-bab3-bf9986f9d9c7"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">2</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="minLevel">2</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8e7d3e92-fa06-4441-b7e0-7a4fb2fc3682"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">376786265</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Previous versions of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit did not support execution of incomplete or dummy models. When the value of a <strong>ValueSpecification</strong> Action is not specified, <span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">this Action's Behavior will apply the rule as follows</span></span><br /></span></p><ul><li><span style="line-height: 1.42857;">If the value of a </span><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">ValueSpecification</strong><span style="line-height: 1.42857;"> Action is not specified, a <strong>null</strong> value token will be passed.<br /></span></li></ul><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="6.ValueSpecificationAction.PNG" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">A demo showing the situation when the value of a ValueSpecification Action is unspecified (A null value token is passed.).</h6><h2>StartClassifierBehaviorAction and StateObjectBehaviorAction </h2><p>A start Classifier Behavior Action is an Action that starts the Classifier Behavior of the input. If the <strong>Object</strong> input pin is not present or empty, <span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">this Action's Behavior will apply the rule as follows</span></span></p><ul><li>If the <strong>Object</strong> input pin is not present or empty, the <strong>StartClassifierBehaviorAction</strong> Action will use the runtime context of the Activity execution as the target.</li></ul><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="7.StartClassifierBehaviorAction.PNG" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"> A demo showing the situation when the object pin is either not present or empty.</h6><h2>OpaqueAction</h2><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Opaque Actions are a type of Action that can be used to represent implementation information. They can also be used as placeholder Actions until you determine the specific type of Action to use. If the opaque Action does not return a value to the <strong>OutputValue</strong> pin, <span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">this Action's Behavior will apply the rule as follows</span></span><br /></span></p><ul><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> If the opaque Action does not return any value to the </span><strong>OutputValue</strong> pin, a <strong>null</strong> value token will be passed to the pin at runtime.</li></ul><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="8.OpaqueAction.PNG" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">If an opaque Action does not return any value to the OutputValue pin, a null token will be passed to the pin.</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="307ac12b-77b0-4277-87ed-2492ff681cf7"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Information</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>A <strong>&quot;null value token&quot;</strong> is an <strong>ObjectToken</strong> with has no values defined.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249569754 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00608: Exporting plots data

- page_id: `249569754`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249569754/Exporting+plots+data
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-02-13T05:01:36.843+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > Time Series Chart
- labels: ['plot-data', 'exporting-plot-data']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1415232085

1415232088

1415232086

A Time series chart provides two options for you to export your plot data in which time values (the values that appear on the horizontal axis) and the corresponding recorded values (the values that appear on the vertical axis) are plotted in a Time series chart. You can export the plot values to either a comma-separated value file (CSV file) or the slot values of an instance model.

1415232084

**Related pages**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7757e2c1-82c4-4192-8071-52c0a7bba4c0"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1415232085</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9e35daef-221d-491b-9354-392e9172974b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1415232088</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7dba503c-636f-4036-9b3d-3b93e9ba67f8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1415232086</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>A Time series chart provides two options for you to export your plot data in which time values (the values that appear on the horizontal axis) and the corresponding recorded values (the values that appear on the vertical axis) are plotted in a Time series chart. You can export the plot values to either a comma-separated value file (CSV file) or the slot values of an instance model.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d3f58372-71f4-439c-b558-4028a99cdab5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1415232084</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="c68ec771-4d65-4891-a7e5-50e55a78cc79" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249569760 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00609: Exporting plots data to a CSV file

- page_id: `249569760`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249569760/Exporting+plots+data+to+a+CSV+file
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-09-27T03:29:20.225+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > Time Series Chart > Exporting plots data
- labels: ['plot-data-to-csv-file', 'exporting-plot-data-to-csv']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1415448634

1415448636

1415448635

The values plotted in a Time series chart are exportable to a CSV file.

To export plots data of a Time series chart to a CSV file

1. Click the **Export** **Data** toolbar button on the Time series chart's plot panel. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Enter a filename and select a location to save the file.
3. Click the Save button. The following figure shows plotted values in the Time series chart

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Plotted values in the Time series chart.

The following figure shows the exported plot values in a CSV file

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Exported plot values in a CSV file.

If you exported the plotted values in a Time series chart to a CSV file, the exported plots data in the CSV file would look like those shown in the first figure. The axes labels would appear in the first paragraph of the file as the column header.

1415448633

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Exporting plots data' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Exporting plots data to an instance model' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d76f801e-a062-4122-8f45-a48a9aa636e1"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1415448634</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="373791af-4512-4b15-a1f7-2611570bd4b3"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1415448636</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fec85301-bb35-4173-9111-375c1c9ab0be"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1415448635</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The values plotted in a Time series chart are exportable to a CSV file. </p><p>To export plots data of a Time series chart to a CSV file</p><hr /><ol><li><p>Click the <strong>Export</strong> <strong>Data</strong> toolbar button on the Time series chart's plot panel. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Export Data Toolbar Button on a Time Series Chart" ac:alt="The Export Data Toolbar Button on a Time Series Chart"><ri:attachment ri:filename="time-series-chart-button-panel.png" /></ac:image></h6></li><li>Enter a filename and select a location to save the file.</li><li>Click the <strong>Save</strong> button.<br /><br />The following figure shows plotted values in the Time series chart</li></ol><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Plotted Values in a Time Series Chart" ac:alt="Plotted Values in a Time Series Chart"><ri:attachment ri:filename="plotted_timeserieschart.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Exporting plots data" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;">Plotted values in the Time series chart.</h6><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">The following figure shows the exported plot values in a CSV file</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:border="true" ac:title="Exported Plot Values in CSV File" ac:alt="Exported Plot Values in CSV File" ac:height="382" ac:width="133"><ri:attachment ri:filename="dataplots_inCSV.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Exporting plots data" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Exported plot values in a CSV file.</h6><p>If you exported the plotted values in a Time series chart to a CSV file, the exported plots data in the CSV file would look like those shown in the first figure. The axes labels would appear in the first paragraph of the file as the column header. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ac9a157e-473f-4eb6-bf54-eea97c5f84f2"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1415448633</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Exporting plots data" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Exporting plots data to an instance model" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249569762 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00610: Exporting plots data to an instance model

- page_id: `249569762`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249569762/Exporting+plots+data+to+an+instance+model
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:07.106+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > Time Series Chart > Exporting plots data
- labels: ['exporting-plot-data-to-instance-model', 'plot-data-to-instance-model', 'auto-save-plot-values']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1415823846

1415823848

1415823847

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit also allows you to export plots data in a Time series chart to an Instance model. The model for exported plots data in the simulation profile comprises of DataSet and Data Classes. Whenever you export plots data to an instance model, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will create an InstanceSpecification of a DataSet Class. The slot of a data property will contain a list of InstanceSpecifications of the Data Class. The first instance on the list is an InstanceSpecification containing the time values while the second one is an InstanceSpecification containing the values that appear on the vertical axis of the first plot. If a Time series chart has more than one plot, the third InstanceSpecification on the list will be an InstanceSpecification containing the values of the second plot and so on.

To export plots data to an instance model

1. Click the **Export** **to** **Instance** toolbar button of the Time series chart's panel whose values you want to export. The **Element Selection** dialog will open. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Select a package that will own the instance model you want to create.
3. Click the OK button. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will create the instance model containing the plots data in a Time Series Chart as shown in the following figure

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The instance model containing the plots data in a Time series chart.

To auto-save plot values into model on every simulation run

1. Do one of the following

- Double-click the Time series chart.
- Right-click the Time series chart and select Specification .

The Specification window will open as follows

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

2. Click the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button beside the **Plot Result Location** property to edit its value. The**Plot Result Location** property value takes the name of the**Instance** to save the values, as in the following figure

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

3. Start the simulation. When the simulation terminates, the plot values will be written to the **Instance specification** selected in the previous step, as in the following figure

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

1415823845

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Exporting plots data' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Exporting plots data to a CSV file' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570268 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00611: Exporting runtime objects to InstanceSpecifications

- page_id: `249570268`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570268/Exporting+runtime+objects+to+InstanceSpecifications
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:10.335+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation debugging > Runtime Value Monitoring
- labels: ['exporting-runtime-values', 'exporting-runtime-objects']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

45332699

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

45332701

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

45332700

You can export the values of a runtime object through the **Variables** pane to the following Specifications

- a new InstanceSpecification.
- an existing InstanceSpecification.
- the InstanceSpecification that created the runtime object. Once exported, the values of a runtime object will be set to the slots of the InstanceSpecification.

The type of InstanceSpecification you want to export the values of a runtime object to, depends on the element you used to create the runtime object. If the element is a Classifier, you can export the values of the runtime object to either of the following

- A new InstanceSpecification.
- An existing InstanceSpecification.

If the element is an InstanceSpecification, you can export the values of the runtime object to either of the following

- A new InstanceSpecification.
- The same InstanceSpecification that you used to create that runtime object.

To export a runtime value to a new InstanceSpecification

1. Do either of the following

- Click a runtime object whose value you want to export in the Name column and click the Export to New Instance icon on the Variables pane toolbar.
- Right-click the row and select Export Value To > New Instance

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

2. The **Select Owner** dialog will open. Select the owner of the new InstanceSpecification (the system folder).

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

3. Click **OK**.

To export a runtime value to an existing InstanceSpecification

1. Do either of the following

- Click a runtime object whose value you want to export in the Name column and click the Export to Instance icon on the Variables pane toolbar.
- Right-click the row and select Export Value To > Instance .

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### NOTE: Note

Note

To export the value of a runtime object using this method, the runtime object must be created from a Classifier.

2. The **Select Instance** dialog will open. Select an InstanceSpecification that will be used to save the runtime object (You can select only the InstanceSpecification whose classifier is the same as that of the runtime object). 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

3. Click **OK**.

To export a runtime value to the InstanceSpecification that has created the runtime object

1. Do either of the following

- Click a runtime object whose value you want to export in the Name column and click the Export to Instance icon on the Variables pane toolbar.
- Right-click the row and select Export Value To > Instance .

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

2. Once Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit exported and saved the values of the runtime object to the Instance Specification that created it, you can see the hyperlink to that particular Instance Specification in the **Simulation** **Console** pane.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

3. Click the hyperlink, and you will see the exact Instance Specification in the Containment tree.

This method allows you to export the value of the runtime object to the slots of the Instance Specification that has created the object. You can see the notification of each successful export and a location hyperlink, if any, in the **Simulation** **Console** pane.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

To export the value of a runtime object using this method, the runtime object must be created from an Instance Specification.

45332698

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Instance Specification' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Simulation console' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Variables pane' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570911 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00612: Extraction of Constraints from Text Based Requirements

- page_id: `249570911`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570911/Extraction+of+Constraints+from+Text+Based+Requirements
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:20.122+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation of SysML models > Requirements traceability from the Variables pane
- labels: ['extracting-constraints-from-text-based-requirements']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

As of version 18.4, another new addition to Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit's already impressive repertoire, is the ability to automatically extract constraint equations from the text of a requirement. 
For example, if a requirement text states that a moving car must have speed more than 0, then the constraint equation “speed > 0” is automatically extracted from the requirement text and will be evaluated upon running of a simulation. 
While typing the requirement text, a shortcut menu appears as soon as you type some keywords which are available in the Glossary, see the example as follows

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Constraints within text-based requirements.

For the constraint to execute properly, the requirement should be linked to a property, such as in the above example, a satisfy relation is used to link between the property and the requirement.

When the simulation is run, the constraint is evaluated and color-coded according to the result of the simulation, red if the constraint fails and green if it passes, as shown in figure below. Additionally, a mouse over the variable will display a tooltip.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Running a simulation evaluates the constraint within the requirement and color-codes it.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>As of version 18.4, another new addition to Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit's already impressive repertoire, is the ability to automatically extract constraint equations from the text of a requirement.<br />For example, if a requirement text states that a moving car must have speed more than 0, then the constraint equation “speed &gt; 0” is automatically extracted from the requirement text and will be evaluated upon running of a simulation.<br />While typing the requirement text, a shortcut menu appears as soon as you type some keywords which are available in the Glossary, see the example as follows</p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Constraints within text based  requirements" ac:alt="Constraints within text based  requirements"><ri:attachment ri:filename="108924_1.jpg" /></ac:image><br />Constraints within text-based requirements.</h6><p>For the constraint to execute properly, the requirement should be linked to a property, such as in the above example, a satisfy relation is used to link between the property and the requirement.</p><p>When the simulation is run, the constraint is evaluated and color-coded according to the result of the simulation, red if the constraint fails and green if it passes, as shown in figure below. Additionally, a mouse over the variable will display a tooltip. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Running a simulation evaluates the constraint within the requirement and color-codes it" ac:alt="Running a simulation evaluates the constraint within the requirement and color-codes it"><ri:attachment ri:filename="108925_b.jpg" /></ac:image><br />Running a simulation evaluates the constraint within the requirement and color-codes it.</h6><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570885 space=MMA version=4 -->
## PAGE 00613: Flow Port

- page_id: `249570885`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570885/Flow+Port
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-09-15T18:04:05.937+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation of SysML models > Supported SysML elements > Port
- labels: ['flow-port']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Flow Port' space='MED'] is a Port that specifies the input and output items that can flow between a Block and its environment. Flow updating depends on directions (**in**, **out**, and **inout** directions as shown in the figure below). If there is the **in** or **out** direction, the value cannot be updated in the reverse direction. In general, Flow Ports are used for asynchronous, broadcast, and send-and-forget interactions. Animation of the value flow is animated through parts, Ports, and Connectors. Both atomic Flow Ports (typed by Signal or ValueType) and non-atomic Flow Ports (typed by FlowSpecification) are supported. If the Flow Port is connected to multiple external and/or internal Connectors, the items are propagated (broadcast) to the other ends of all Connectors that have matching properties.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Flow Ports with animation of value propagation in the Internal Block diagram.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Port' space='']
- Full Port
- Proxy Port

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>A <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Flow Port" /></ac:link> is a Port that specifies the input and output items that can flow between a Block and its environment. Flow updating depends on directions (<strong>in</strong>, <strong>out</strong>, and <strong>inout</strong> directions as shown in the figure below). If there is the <strong>in</strong> or <strong>out</strong> direction, the value cannot be updated in the reverse direction. In general, Flow Ports are used for asynchronous, broadcast, and send-and-forget interactions. Animation of the value flow is animated through parts, Ports, and Connectors. Both atomic Flow Ports (typed by Signal or ValueType) and non-atomic Flow Ports (typed by FlowSpecification) are supported. If the Flow Port is connected to multiple external and/or internal Connectors, the items are propagated (broadcast) to the other ends of all Connectors that have matching properties.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Flow Port.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Flow Ports with animation of value propagation in the Internal Block diagram.</h6></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul class="childpages-macro"><li data-uuid="777a6dbe-f419-4206-a68a-738fda0258aa"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Port" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="dad47d16-ced3-4879-836f-9798d529fcdf"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/Full+Port">Full Port</a></li><li data-uuid="913b1f47-48e1-48a8-90ac-a41ba533df3e"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/Proxy+Port">Proxy Port</a></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570880 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00614: Flow property

- page_id: `249570880`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570880/Flow+property
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-10-06T04:50:38.276+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation of SysML models > Supported SysML elements
- labels: ['flow-property']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A flow property is a property to which the stereotype «FlowProperty» is applied. A flow property has a flow direction. A flow due to flow properties will incur flow properties matching. Matching flow properties must have the same direction and type. If the direction and type of multiple flow properties at either end of a connector match, a flow will occur between the flow properties with the same name. When the flow occurs between matching flow properties, the value of the flow property with a direction 'out' or 'inout' will be delivered to the flow property with the direction 'in' or 'inout'.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A flow property is a property to which the stereotype «FlowProperty» is applied. A flow property has a flow direction. A flow due to flow properties will incur flow properties matching. Matching flow properties must have the same direction and type. If the direction and type of multiple flow properties at either end of a connector match, a flow will occur between the flow properties with the same name. When the flow occurs between matching flow properties, the value of the flow property with a direction 'out' or 'inout' will be delivered to the flow property with the direction 'in' or 'inout'. </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570853 space=MMA version=3 -->
## PAGE 00615: FMI 2.0 co-simulation

- page_id: `249570853`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570853/FMI+2.0+co-simulation
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:17.088+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation of SysML models
- labels: ['fmi-co-simulation']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports Functional Mockup Interface (FMI) Version 1.0 and 2.0. FMI 2.0 co-simulation applies when several FMIs are connected in Internal Block diagrams (IBD), an integration of separate independent black box simulation models along with the master algorithm.

FMI is a standard that supports model exchange and co-simulation of models. A component that implements FMI is called FMU (Functional Mockup Unit). The Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit is also capable of reading FMU files. An FMU is a zipped file containing an XML description file and implementation in binary form that executes the equation representing the component’s Behavior.

#### WARNING: Important

Important

Before simulating FMU files, you must make sure the following conditions are met:

- The FMU file can be attached to the project, placed in the same directory as the project or the path to the file can be specified as url tag on the FMU block. The path is set automatically when importing an FMU file. [ATTACHMENT filename='File path .png']
- The FMU files must be OS-platform supported, e.g., Win64, Win32, linux64, linux32, etc. (Cross-platform execution of FMU files is not supported.). You can see all supported platforms in the Binaries folders of the FMU files. If the platform you are using is not in the Binaries folder, it will not be supported.
- An FMU Block requires a SimulationConfig with the Internal simulation clock to provide the reference time in the simulation (shown in Step 3 below). Otherwise, a warning message will be shown in the Console pane. Please see also the WaterTankFMI build-in sample of Simulation.

To co-simulate FMU Blocks

1. Drag FMU files into a Block Definition diagram of the SysML project. The FMU files will be presented as «block» and «fmu» stereotypes along with ports. You may create associations (aggregations) with the main system block to make the FMU files become part properties.
2. If there is only one FMU file to simulate, proceed to Step 3. Otherwise, you will have to create an Internal Block diagram for the main system block to automatically include part properties in the diagram. Select the Flow Port context menu to show flow ports of those properties and create connections as needed.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

3. Create a Simulation Configuration diagram with a [SimulationConfig](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/SimulationConfig+stereotype) to specify *startTime, stepSize*, and *endTime* (optional) in the properties. You can add a [Time series chart](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/Time+series+chart) and specify *represents* and *value* of the FMU represented as user interfaces. You can also include [CSVExport](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/CSV+export) to store the results.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

4. Run the Simulation Configuration and see the results in the Time series chart and **Variables** pane, as well as the resulting Excel file from CSVExport.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="Textbody">Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports Functional Mockup Interface (FMI) Version 1.0 and 2.0. FMI 2.0 co-simulation applies when several FMIs are connected in Internal Block diagrams (IBD), an integration of separate independent black box simulation models along with the master algorithm.</p><p class="Textbody">FMI is a standard that supports model exchange and co-simulation of models. A component that implements FMI is called FMU (Functional Mockup Unit). The Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit is also capable of reading FMU files. An FMU is a zipped file containing an XML description file and implementation in binary form that executes the equation representing the component’s Behavior.</p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a71e4f85-9e60-4ce5-9c23-3378be2dc56a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Important</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="Textbody">Before simulating FMU files, you must make sure the following conditions are met:</p><ul><li>The FMU file can be attached to the project, placed in the same directory as the project or the path to the file can be specified as url tag on the FMU block. The path is set automatically when importing an FMU file.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="File path .png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>The FMU files must be OS-platform supported, e.g., Win64, Win32, linux64, linux32, etc. (Cross-platform execution of FMU files is not supported.). You can see all supported platforms in the <strong>Binaries</strong> folders of the FMU files. If the platform you are using is not in the <strong>Binaries</strong> folder, it will not be supported.</li><li>An FMU Block requires a <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/SimulationConfig+stereotype">SimulationConfig</a> with the <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/Internal+simulation+clock">Internal simulation clock</a> to provide the reference time in the simulation (shown in Step 3 below). Otherwise, a warning message will be shown in the <strong>Console</strong> pane. Please see also the <strong>WaterTankFMI</strong> build-in sample of Simulation.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To co-simulate FMU Blocks</p><hr /><ol><li>Drag FMU files into a Block Definition diagram of the SysML project. The FMU files will be presented as «block» and «fmu» stereotypes along with ports. You may create associations (aggregations) with the main system block to make the FMU files become part properties.</li><li>If there is only one FMU file to simulate, proceed to Step 3. Otherwise, you will have to create an Internal Block diagram for the main system block to automatically include part properties in the diagram. Select the <strong>Flow Port</strong> context menu to show flow ports of those properties and create connections as needed.</li></ol><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="7113-1 Creating  a SysML Internal Block Diagram.PNG" /></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">3. Create a Simulation Configuration diagram with a <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/SimulationConfig+stereotype">SimulationConfig</a> to specify <em>startTime, stepSize</em>, and <em>endTime</em> (optional) in the properties. You can add a <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/Time+series+chart">Time series chart</a> and specify <em>represents</em> and <em>value</em> of the FMU represented as user interfaces. You can also include <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/CSV+export">CSVExport</a> to store the results.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="7113-2 Creating a Simulation Configuration Diagram.PNG" /></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">4. Run the Simulation Configuration and see the results in the Time series chart and <strong>Variables</strong> pane, as well as the resulting Excel file from CSVExport.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="7113-3 Running the Simulation Configuration.PNG" /></ac:image></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249571291 space=MMA version=4 -->
## PAGE 00616: Frequently asked questions

- page_id: `249571291`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249571291/Frequently+asked+questions
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-09-15T14:47:44.009+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide
- labels: ['frequently-asked-questions']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Activity simulation

**Question 1**:

Why isn't an operation outside a context object executed, e.g., an operation referring to an Activity outside a Block?

**Answer**: According to the fUML specification, an operation will be dispatched to the target object only, which is the context of the execution. You need to move the Activity into the Block and use it as a CallBehaviorAction with the Behavior setting.

**Question 2**:

Can I simulate dummy tokens of CallBehaviorActions that do not have a Behavior?

**Answer**: Yes, you can set the **Auto-Create fUML Object of Output Pin** option in the **Simulation [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Project options' space='']**dialog to **true**, which is the default value in Version 19.0 SP2 and later. Please see also****[Dummy tokens of Actions through Output Pins](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/Dummy+tokens+of+Actions+through+Output+Pins).

##### Parametric evaluation

**Question 1**:

Why can't I use MATLAB as an external evaluator even after [integration with MATLAB](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/Integration+with+MATLAB) of the modeling tool?

**Answer**: You must use the 64-bit version of MATLAB (Version 2012a or later) to align it with the 64-bit version of the modeling tool you are working with, e.g., MagicDraw or Cameo Systems Modeler. You must also ensure that **Path** of **System variables** in the **Environment Variables** dialog is set to the correct MATLAB path.

**Question 2**:

Can I use MATLAB 2019b as an external evaluator?

**Answer**: Yes, you can use MATLAB 2019b as an external evaluator. MATLAB R2019b has a new option, **Single simulation output**, set true by default in Simulink, which doesn’t affect the simulation. However, there are a few limitations as follows:

- If Simulation cannot load the Simulink, e.g., an invalid URL/ filename, both warning and script exception messages will be printed in the Console panel. In this case, the MATLAB engine cannot be used, so you will need to manually restart MATLAB.
- If there is no input for the In Port, Simulation will skip the evaluation, and a warning message will be printed in Console panel.

##### State Machine simulation

**Question**:

Can I change State activation semantics from **before entry** (official UML semantics) to **after entry**?

**Answer**: Yes, you can set the **State** **Activation** **Semantics** option in the **Simulation [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Project options' space='']**dialog to **After entry**. The default value is **Before entry** in Version 19.0 and later. Please see also [State activation semantics.](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/State+activation+semantics)

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity simulation' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Call Behavior Action' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Behavior' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Integration with MATLAB' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Parametric evaluator' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State activation semantics' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dummy tokens of Actions through Output Pins' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

You need to move the Activity into the Block and use it as a CallBehaviorAction with the Behavior setting.</p><p><br /></p><p><strong>Question 2</strong>:</p><p>Can I simulate dummy tokens of CallBehaviorActions that do not have a Behavior?</p><p><strong>Answer</strong>: Yes, you can set the <strong>Auto-Create fUML Object of Output Pin</strong> option in the <strong>Simulation <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Project options" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Project Options]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> </strong>dialog to <strong>true</strong>, which is the default value in Version 19.0 SP2 and later. You must also ensure that <strong>Path</strong> of <strong>System variables</strong> in the <strong>Environment Variables</strong> dialog is set to the correct MATLAB path<span class="external-link">.</span></p><p><span class="external-link"> </span></p><p><strong>Question 2</strong>:</p><p>Can I use MATLAB 2019b as an external evaluator?</p><p><strong>Answer</strong>: Yes, you can use MATLAB 2019b as an external evaluator. MATLAB R2019b has a new option, <strong>Single simulation output</strong>, set true by default in Simulink, which doesn’t affect the simulation. However, there are a few limitations as follows:</p><ul><li data-uuid="6af859a3-d62d-40f8-924d-95f3529991c7">If Simulation cannot load the Simulink, e.g., an invalid URL/ filename, both warning and script exception messages will be printed in the <strong>Console</strong> panel. In this case, the MATLAB engine cannot be used, so you will need to manually restart MATLAB.</li><li data-uuid="8841c0ef-1aa7-4d9e-b7ad-5f707b2a62b7">If there is no input for the In Port, Simulation will skip the evaluation, and a warning message will be printed in <strong>Console</strong> panel.</li></ul><p><span class="external-link"> </span></p><hr /><h3>State Machine simulation</h3><p><strong>Question</strong>:</p><p>Can I change State activation semantics from <strong>before entry</strong> (official UML semantics) to <strong>after entry</strong>?</p><p><strong>Answer</strong>: Yes, you can set the <strong>State</strong> <strong>Activation</strong> <strong>Semantics</strong> option in the <strong>Simulation <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Project options" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Project Options]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> </strong>dialog to <strong>After entry</strong>. The default value is <strong>Before entry</strong> in Version 19.0 and later. Please see also <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/State+activation+semantics">State activation semantics.</a></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="25fff097-6aac-4cac-81e6-6a1077274948"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Activity" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="7bcd7a3c-e69d-4443-a5c3-8fd9b18be390"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Activity simulation" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="448b3165-b423-433c-86a9-d4622e9339d3"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Block" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="5a845abc-3b70-4219-bf45-e393f2fc6e48"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Call Behavior Action" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="c836ef37-5889-4be2-9593-d9f668d0226d"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Behavior" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="21176699-648d-4f7a-b8ef-0ed296d59a8f"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Integration with MATLAB" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="1124f0fa-e821-40f5-af40-6ad8993f6dbd"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Parametric evaluator" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="9c025d4d-8b67-4ebb-b260-54552e2a452c"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="State activation semantics" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="e556de24-8c16-4dba-a0b8-9ff9c631185a"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Dummy tokens of Actions through Output Pins" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

## PAGE 00617: Full Port

- page_id: `249570887`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570887/Full+Port
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:18.486+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation of SysML models > Supported SysML elements > Port
- labels: ['full-port']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

944179700

944179711

944179710

A Full port is a port stereotyped with «FullPort». It specifies a separate Element of an owning block. Initializing the owning block will cause Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit to initialize a port object from a Classifier that types the full port.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
An Object containing full Ports.

944179699

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Port' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Flow Port' space='']
- Proxy Port

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570771 space=MMA version=4 -->
## PAGE 00618: Functions

- page_id: `249570771`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570771/Functions
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2026-01-20T07:04:13.246+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator > Built-in Math
- labels: ['functions']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### NOTE: Note

Note

- x and y represent real values or variables.

- c and d represent complex values or variables.

- m and n represent integer values or variables.

- U represents a matrix of values.

- A matrix can be passed to a function that operates Element-wise on matrices, as its argument in the example as follows

X = [1, -2, 3; -4, 5, -6; 7, -8, 9];

Y = abs(X) 
result: 
Y = [1 2 3; 4 5 6; 7 8 9]

- det(U) - To return a determinant of a given matrix. U is a square matrix.

| Function name | Syntax | Function |
| --- | --- | --- |
| abs | abs(x)abs(c) | To return an absolute value of x or a complex modulus of c. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| acos | acos(x)acos(c) | To return an arc cosine of an angle in the range of 0.0 through pi. All angles are measured in radians.This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| acosd | acosd(x)acosd(c) | To return an inverse cosine of a given value expressed in degrees. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| acosh | acosh(x)acosh(c) | To return an inverse hyperbolic cosine of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| acot | acot(x)acot(c) | To return an inverse cotangent of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| acotd | acotd(x)acotd(c) | To return an inverse cotangent of a given value expressed in degrees. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| acoth | acoth(x)acoth(c) | To return an inverse hyperbolic cotangent of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| acsc | acsc(x)acsc(c) | To return an inverse cosecant of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| acscd | acscd(x)acscd(c) | To return an inverse cosecant of a given value expressed in degrees. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| acsch | acsch(x)acsch(c) | To return an inverse hyperbolic cosecant of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| asec | asec(x)asec(c) | To return an inverse secant of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| asecd | asecd(x)asecd(c) | To return an inverse secant of a given value expressed in degrees.This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| asech | asech(x)asech(c) | To return an inverse hyperbolic secant of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| asin | asin(x)asin(c) | To return an arc sine of an angle in the range of -pi/2 through pi/2. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| asind | asind(x)asind(c) | To return an inverse sine of a given value expressed in degrees. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| asinh | asinh(x)asinh(c) | To return an inverse hyperbolic sine of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| atan | atan(x)atan(c) | To return an arc tangent of an angle in the range of -pi/2 through pi/2. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| atan2 | atan2(x,y)atan2(U,V) | To return an arc tangent of an angle in the range of -pi through pi. atan2(U, V) returns a matrix of the same size as the U and V matrices containing the Element-by-Element, inverse tangent of the real parts of U and V. |
| atand | atand(x)atand(c) | To return an inverse tangent of a given value, expressed in degrees. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| atanh | atanh(x)atanh(c) | To return an inverse hyperbolic tangent of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| ceil | ceil(x) | To return a smallest (closest to negative infinity) value that is not less than the value of x and is equal to a mathematical integer. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| col | col(U, m) | To return a specific column values of a given matrix. U is a square matrix, m is a column index (starts at 0). |
| conj | conj(c) | To return a conjugated value of c. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| cos | cos(x)cos(c) | To return a trigonometric cosine of an angle. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| cosd | cosd(x)cosd(c) | To return a cosine of a given value expressed in degrees. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| cosh | cosh(x)cosh(c) | To return a hyperbolic cosine of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| cot | cot(x)cot(c) | To return a cotangent of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| cotd | cotd(x)cotd(c) | To return a cotangent of a given value expressed in degrees. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| coth | coth(x)coth(c) | To return a hyperbolic cotangent of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| count | count(U) | To return a number of Elements of a given matrix. |
| csc | csc(x)csc(c) | To return a cosecant of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| cscd | cscd(x)cscd(c) | To return a cosecant of a given value expressed in degree. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| csch | csch(x)csch(c) | To return a hyperbolic cosecant of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| det | det(U) | To return a determinant of a given matrix. U is a square matrix. |
| diag | diag(U)diag(U,m) | To return a diagonal matrix and diagonals of the matrix. If U is a row matrix or a column matrix of n Elements, this function will return a square matrix of order n+abs(m), with the Elements of U on the kth diagonal. k = 0 represents the main diagonal. k > 0 is above the main diagonal. k < 0 is below the main diagonal. If U is a square matrix, this function will return a column matrix formed by the Elements of the kth diagonal of U. |
| exp | exp(x)exp(c) | To return a Euler's number e raised to the power of a or c. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| eye | eye(m) | To return an identity matrix of dimension m x m. |
| factorial | factorial(m) | To return a factorial of m value. |
| floor | floor(x)floor(c) | To return a largest (closest to positive infinity) value that is not greater than the value of x and is equal to a mathematical integer. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| IEEEremainder | IEEEremainder(x,y) | To compute the remainder operation in two arguments as prescribed by the IEEE 754 standard. |
| if | if(b,x,y) | To return the value of x if b is true. Otherwise, y is returned. Where b is a boolean value. |
| imag | imag(c) | To return a real value of an imaginary part of a given complex number. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| invert | invert(U) | To return an inverse or pseudo inverse of a given matrix. If the given matrix is a square matrix, the inverse of a U matrix will be returned using the LU factorization. If the given matrix is not a square matrix, a pseudo inverse matrix will be returned using the QR factorization. |
| linsolve | linsolve(U,V) | X = linsolve(U,V) solves the linear system U*X = V using the LU factorization with partial pivoting when U is a square matrix. |
| In | ln(x)ln(c) | To return a natural logarithm (base e) of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| log | log(x, y)log(c) | To return the logarithm (base x) of a given value y. If only one parameter is provided, the function operates with the base of e (natural logarithm). This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| log10 | log10(x)log10(c) | To return a logarithm base 10 of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| log2 | log2(x)log2(c) | To return a logarithm base 2 of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| max | max(x,y,...)max(c,d,...)max(U)max(U,V) | To return a greater of the given values. max(U) returns the largest Element of a given matrix. max(U, V) returns a matrix the same size as U and V with the largest Elements taken from U or V. The dimensions of U and V must be the same. |
| mean | mean(U) | To return a mean or average value of a given matrix. U is a row or a column matrix: mean(U) returns the mean value of all Elements in the given matrix.U is a 2-D matrix: mean(U) returns a row matrix that contains the mean value of each column of the given matrix. |
| median | median(U) | To return a median value of a given matrix. U is a row or column matrix: median(U) returns the median value of all Elements in the given matrix. U is a 2-D matrix: median(U) returns a row matrix that contains the median value of each column of the given matrix. |
| min | min(x,y,...)min(c,d,...)min(U)min(U,V) | To return a smaller of the given values. min(U) returns the smallest Element of a given matrix. min(U, V) returns a matrix the same size as U and V with the smallest Elements taken from U or V. The dimensions of U and V must be the same. |
| mod | mod(x, y) | To return floored division modulus of number x with divisor y. The returned number is always the same sign as a divisor. |
| num2str | num2str(x)num2str(c) | To return a string specifying a given number x. |
| ones | ones(m,n) | To return an m x n matrix of all 1s. |
| pow | pow(x, y)pow(U, c)pow(c, d)pow(U, x)pow(x, U) | To return a value of the first argument raised to the power of the second argument. This function operates Element-wise on a given matrix U. |
| prod | prod(U, dim) | To return the product of elements along dimension dim. If dim is omitted, the first non-singleton dimension is used by default. |
| random | random() | To return a real value with a positive sign, greater than or equal to 0.0 but less than 1.0. |
| real | real(c) | To return a real value of the real part of a given complex number. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| rint | rint(x) | To return a value that is closest in value to an argument and is equal to a mathematical integer. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| round | round(x) | To return a closest value to an argument and is equal to a mathematical integer. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| row | row(U, m) | To return a specific row values of a given matrix. U is a square matrix, m is a row index (starts at 0). |
| sec | sec(x)sec(c) | To return a secant of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| secd | secd(x)secd(c) | To return a secant of a given value expressed in degree. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| sech | sech(x)sech(c) | To return a hyperbolic secant of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| sin | sin(x)sin(c) | To return a trigonometric sine of an angle. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| sind | sind(x)sind(c) | To return a sine of a given value, expressed in degree This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| sinh | sinh(x)sinh(c) | To return a hyperbolic sine of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| size | size(U)size(U, m) | To return a size of a given matrix. If only the matrix is passed to the function as an argument, the returned value is a 1x2 matrix. The first Element is the number of rows and the second Element is the number of columns.If the second parameter (m) is specified, this function will return the size of an mth dimension of a given matrix as a scalar value. The second argument can be 1 or 2 (1 for the row size and 2 for the column size). For example:U = [1, 2, 3; 4, 5, 6];size(U) is [2, 3]size(U, 1) is 2size(U, 2) is 3 |
| sort | sort(U)sort(U, ‘descend’) | To sort the Elements of a given matrix in an ascending or descending order. If the second argument is specified with ‘ascend’ or ‘descend’, the Elements will be in an ascending or descending order respectively. If this function is called without a second argument, the Elements will be sorted in an ascending order.U is a row or column matrix: sort(U) and sort(U, ascend) sort all Elements in the given matrix. U is a 2-D matrix: sort(U) and sort(U, ascend) sort Elements in each column of the given matrix. |
| ssqrt | sqrt(x)sqrt(c) | To return a correctly rounded positive square root of a double value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| std | std(U)std(U, flag) | To return a standard deviation of a given matrix. The ’flag’ argument can be 0 or 1. It specifies the method for calculating the standard deviation. If the flag = 0, the standard deviation is normalized by N-1. If the flag = 1, the standard deviation is normalized by N where N is the number of data. The value of the flag will be zero by default. U is a row or column matrix: std(U) and std(U, flag) returns the standard deviation of all Elements in the given matrix. U is 2-D matrix: std(U) and std(U,flag) returns a row matrix that contains the standard deviation of each column of the given matrix. |
| str2num | str2num(s) | To return a number specified by a given string s. |
| sum | sum(U) | To return a summation of all Elements in a U matrix. |
| tan | tan(x)tan(c) | To return a trigonometric tangent of an angle. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| tand | tand(x)tand(c) | To return a tangent of a given value expressed in degree.This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| tanh | tanh(x)tanh(c) | To return a hyperbolic tangent of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| toDegrees | toDegrees(x)toDegrees(c) | To convert an angle measured in radians to an approximately equivalent angle measured in degrees. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| toRadians |  | To convert an angle measured in degrees to an approximately equivalent angle measured in radians. This function operates Element-wise on matrices. |
| transpose | transpose(U) | To return a transposition of a given matrix |
| variance | variance(U) | To return a variance of a given matrix. U is a row or column matrix:variance(U) returns the variance of all Elements in the given matrix. U is a 2-D matrix: variance(U) returns a row matrix that contains the variance of each column of the given matrix. |
| zeros | zeros(m, n) | To return an m x n matrix of all 0s. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE
