This function operates Element-wise on matrices.</p></td></tr><tr><td>sech</td><td><p>sech(x)<br />sech(c)</p></td><td><p>To return a hyperbolic secant of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices.</p></td></tr><tr><td>sin</td><td><p>sin(x)<br />sin(c)</p></td><td><p>To return a trigonometric sine of an angle. This function operates Element-wise on matrices.</p></td></tr><tr><td>sind</td><td><p>sind(x)</p><p>sind(c)</p></td><td><p>To return a sine of a given value, expressed in degree This function operates Element-wise on matrices.</p></td></tr><tr><td>sinh</td><td><p>sinh(x)<br />sinh(c)</p></td><td><p>To return a hyperbolic sine of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices.</p></td></tr><tr><td>size</td><td><p>size(U)<br />size(U, m)</p></td><td><p>To return a size of a given matrix. If only the matrix is passed to the function as an argument, the returned value is a 1x2 matrix. The first Element <br />is the number of rows and the second Element is the number of columns.If the second parameter (m) is specified, this function will return the size of an mth dimension of a given matrix as a scalar value. The second argument can be 1 or 2 (1 for the row size and 2 for the column size). For example:<br />U = [1, 2, 3; 4, 5, 6];<br />size(U) is [2, 3]<br />size(U, 1) is 2<br />size(U, 2) is 3</p></td></tr><tr><td>sort</td><td><p>sort(U)<br />sort(U, ‘descend’)</p></td><td><p>To sort the Elements of a given matrix in an ascending or descending order. If the second argument is specified with ‘ascend’ or ‘descend’, the Elements will be in an ascending or descending order respectively. If this function is called without a second argument, the Elements will be sorted in an ascending order.<br />U is a row or column matrix: <br />sort(U) and sort(U, ascend) sort all Elements in the given matrix. <br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">U is a 2-D matrix: sort(U) and sort(U, ascend) sort Elements in each column of the given matrix.</span></p></td></tr><tr><td>ssqrt</td><td><p>sqrt(x)<br />sqrt(c)</p></td><td><p>To return a correctly rounded positive square root of a double value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices.</p></td></tr><tr><td>std</td><td><p>std(U)<br />std(U, flag)</p></td><td><p>To return a standard deviation of a given matrix. The ’flag’ argument can be 0 or 1. It specifies the method for calculating the standard deviation. <br />If the flag = 0, the standard deviation is normalized by N-1. If the flag = 1, the standard deviation is normalized by N where N is the number of data. <br />The value of the flag will be zero by default. U is a row or column matrix: <br />std(U) and std(U, flag) returns the standard deviation of all Elements in the given matrix. U is 2-D matrix: std(U) and std(U,flag) returns a row matrix that contains the standard deviation of each column of the given matrix.</p></td></tr><tr><td>str2num</td><td>str2num(s)</td><td>To return a number specified by a given string s.</td></tr><tr><td>sum</td><td>sum(U)</td><td>To return a summation of all Elements in a U matrix.</td></tr><tr><td>tan</td><td><p>tan(x)<br />tan(c)</p></td><td><p>To return a trigonometric tangent of an angle. This function operates Element-wise on matrices.</p></td></tr><tr><td>tand</td><td><p>tand(x)<br />tand(c)</p></td><td><p>To return a tangent of a given value expressed in degree.This function operates Element-wise on matrices.</p></td></tr><tr><td>tanh</td><td><p>tanh(x)<br />tanh(c)</p></td><td><p>To return a hyperbolic tangent of a given value. This function operates Element-wise on matrices.</p></td></tr><tr><td>toDegrees</td><td><p>toDegrees(x)<br />toDegrees(c)</p></td><td><p>To convert an angle measured in radians to an approximately equivalent angle measured in degrees. This function operates Element-wise on matrices.</p></td></tr><tr><td>toRadians</td><td><br /></td><td><p>To convert an angle measured in degrees to an approximately equivalent angle measured in radians. This function operates Element-wise on matrices.</p></td></tr><tr><td>transpose</td><td>transpose(U)</td><td>To return a transposition of a given matrix</td></tr><tr><td>variance</td><td>variance(U)</td><td><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">To return a variance of a given matrix. U is a row or column matrix:</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><br />variance(U) returns the variance of all Elements in the given matrix. U is a 2-D matrix: variance(U) returns a row matrix that contains the variance of each column of the given matrix.<br /></span></td></tr><tr><td>zeros</td><td>zeros(m, n)</td><td>To return an m x n matrix of all 0s.</td></tr></tbody></table><p><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=268173461 space=MMA version=8 -->
## PAGE 00619: Generate Reusable UI

- page_id: `268173461`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/268173461/Generate+Reusable+UI
- version_number: 8
- version_date: `2025-11-10T07:29:48.237+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > Reusable UI Mockup
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

TheGenerate Reusable UIfeature provides an automated mechanism to create a reusable, parameterized User Interface (UI) from an existing UI Frame element. This UI acts as an independent UI prototype that can be integrated into system simulations and composite structure diagrams. The **Generate Reusable UI**command automatically generates a reusable UI from a selected Frame element.

This action is only available for symbols and elements with the **<<Frame>>** stereotype applied.

To generate reusable UI

- Right-click any element with the <<Frame>> stereotype and select Simulation>Generate Reusable UI. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

When the**Generate Reusable UI**action is selected, the following steps are performed automatically

1. The selected Frame element is set as a UI.
2. For each UI component (Label, TextField, CheckBox, Slider) within the selected Frame, a Port is created as an inner element of the Frame.
3. Each of the UI components is assigned a Port.

Once the Reusable UI is generated, you can drag and drop it into an**IBD diagram**. This automatically displays the property typed by the Reusable UI, along with its ports. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Generated Reusable UI dragged and dropped into an**IBD diagram**.

To connect UI components with system attributes, create a**connector**between the UI port and the system attribute. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Connectors created between the UI port and the system attribute.

When a Reusable UI is used during simulation, the corresponding UI dialog is automatically displayed.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p style="text-align: left;">The<span> </span>Generate Reusable UI<span> </span>feature provides an automated mechanism to create a reusable, parameterized User Interface (UI) from an existing UI Frame element. This UI acts as an independent UI prototype that can be integrated into system simulations and composite structure diagrams. The <strong>Generate Reusable UI </strong>command automatically generates a reusable UI from a selected Frame element.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="37c1eca8-c855-4d95-8df2-da2c2d8a73a9"><ac:rich-text-body><p>This action is only available for symbols and elements with the <strong>&lt;&lt;Frame&gt;&gt;</strong> stereotype applied.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p style="text-align: left;">To generate reusable UI</p><hr /><ul><li style="text-align: left;" data-uuid="d7c64ab1-c76c-46dd-ba41-ddcd228ad0ee">Right-click any element with the <strong>&lt;&lt;Frame&gt;&gt;</strong> stereotype and select <strong>Simulation&gt;Generate Reusable UI.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Generate Reusable UI.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></strong></li></ul><p style="text-align: left;">When the<span> </span><strong>Generate Reusable UI</strong><span> </span>action is selected, the following steps are performed automatically</p><hr /><ol style="text-align: left;"><li data-uuid="fd2536aa-e540-44aa-afe1-b2123456418c">The selected Frame element is set as a UI.</li><li data-uuid="5cb37dbe-4e1a-43c5-9ea8-c5092886abb4">For each UI component (Label, TextField, CheckBox, Slider) within the selected Frame, a<span> </span><strong>Port</strong><span> </span>is created as an inner element of the Frame.</li><li data-uuid="b7ea7193-b8fc-4de3-8d73-825b5466a1f8">Each of the UI components is assigned a Port.<br /><br /></li></ol><p style="text-align: left;">Once the Reusable UI is generated, you can drag and drop it into an<span> </span><strong>IBD diagram</strong>. This automatically displays the property typed by the Reusable UI, along with its ports. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Drag n Drop to IBD.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Generated Reusable UI dragged and dropped into an<span> </span><strong>IBD diagram</strong>.</h6><p style="text-align: left;">To connect UI components with system attributes, create a<span> </span><strong>connector</strong><span> </span>between the UI port and the system attribute.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Connectors added.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Connectors created between the UI port and the system attribute.</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2b6664cc-13fd-4a6c-b9a9-da375b950631"><ac:rich-text-body><p>When a Reusable UI is used during simulation, the corresponding UI dialog is automatically displayed.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249569979 space=MMA version=3 -->
## PAGE 00620: Generating an HTML table from a UI table, Time series chart, and CSV export configuration

- page_id: `249569979`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249569979/Generating+an+HTML+table+from+a+UI+table+Time+series+chart+and+CSV+export+configuration
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-09-12T15:08:42.528+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > HTML UI
- labels: ['generating-html-table', 'html-table']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

33

##### Generating an HTML table from a UI table

This feature of the Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit enables you to export MagicDraw HTML tables in UI modeling diagrams to HTML5, allowing them to be viewed in a embeddedweb browser. The sample model used on this page, *SpacecraftMassRollup.mdzip*, demonstrates how to create a User Interface table in the User Interface Modeling diagram, generate HTML, run the simulation, and view the UI table in HTML5 in your embedded browser.

To generate an HTML table from a UI table component

1. Open the <modeling_tool_install_dir>\samples\simulation\SpacecraftMassRollup.mdzip file.
2. Right-click the Model element in the containment tree and select Expert in the Contextual Menu.
3. Select **Create Diagram**>**Other Diagrams** >**User Interface Modeling Diagram**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Drag a UI table component onto the newly created diagram. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. From the Containment tree, drag the**spacecraft**Block present in the **Structure**Packageto the table and using a long drop select**Create UI Prototype.**The table headers will change from**Column 1**and**Column 2**to**Name**and**Value**. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] TipIf you drag the **spacecraft** Block to the table quickly, the column name will change automatically, without having to select **Create UI Prototype**.
6. Open the Specification dialog of the UI Table and verify that the Classifier is assigned to the **Represents** property. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] beside the **Value** property and select the values you want to monitor from the tree view in the **Select Nested Properties** dialog and Click **OK.**
8. The selected values will appear in the **Value** property in the UI table Specification window. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
9. Specify the created Table UI as UI of the **Simulation Configuration.**
10. Set the **Show UI AsHTML**property to *true*.
11. Run thespacecraft mass analysis with Table Simulation Configuration. The HTML table will be opened in an embedded browser.

You can edit the**Documentation/Comments**tag from the UI table's Specification window and run the simulation again, it will show the table's descriptions. To learn more refer to the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Comment' space='MT']page.

##### Generating an HTML table from a Time series chart and CSV export configuration

An HTML table can also be generated from a Time series chart and CSV export configuration.

To generate an HTML table from either a Time series chart or a CSV export configuration

1. Right-click a Time Series Chart or a CSV Export configuration and choose **Simulation**> **Generate HTML Table**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. This will generate an HTML file, which can be viewed either online or offline. A message stating that the HTML table is generated will be displayed in the Simulation Console pane.

#### Sample model

The model used in the figures on this page is the *SpacecraftMassRollup.mdzip*****sample model that comes with your modeling tool.

To open the sample

- Find it in the modeling tool <modeling_tool_install_dir>\samples\simulation\SpacecraftMassRollup.mdzip.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249571253 space=MMA version=3 -->
## PAGE 00621: Generating FMU - Technology preview

- page_id: `249571253`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249571253/Generating+FMU+-+Technology+preview
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-12-03T09:49:44.048+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Server-side simulation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can generate an FMU (Functional Mock-up Unit) for external co-simulation of dynamic models that enables the exchange of simulation results between different simulation tools. The generated FMU exposes the defined input and output parameters of a model, as specified in a Simulation Configuration. The generated FMU runs the server-side simulation of a particular Simulation Configuration using given inputs and returns the outputs after each time step. FMUs can be generated for two platforms: Linux 64-bit and Windows 64-bit.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

- You must have the same Java HOME version as the modeling tool’s Java version. To check the Java HOME version, run java --version in the command prompt. To check the modeling tool’s Java version, go to Help > About > Environment tab.
- You must have a simulation server certificate to co-simulate the generated FMU.
- You must use the generated FMU with tools that use the same JAVA version as the modeling tool; otherwise, you will not be able to run the generated FMU.
- You must add the JAR files to the plugin library to generate FMU. To learn more, refer to .

FMU generation uses selected Simulation Configuration properties, and can contain the following defined data:

- Start Time, End Time, and Step Size property values.
- Input Parameters property value: the default values from the defined input parameters will be included in the generated FMU.
- Output Parameters property value: the default values from the defined output parameters will be included in the generated FMU.

To generate an FMU

1. Open a Teamwork Cloud project.
2. Right-click a Simulation Configuration and select Simulation > Generate FMU. [ATTACHMENT filename='Generate_FMU_Command.png']
3. In the Generate FMU dialog specify the following:
  - In the File Directory box, specify the location where the generated file should be stored.
  - Select the Win64 or Lin64 radio button, depending on the platform on which you will run the generated FMU.
  - Optionally, select the **Include login credentials**check box and specify a server-side simulation server address and login credentials or, token to be stored in the generated FMU file. You can leave the **Include login credentials** check box empty and provide the login credentials or token later as FMU parameters while importing an FMU file. In this case, the credentials will not be stored in the generated FMU file.You can also generate a personal accesstoken instead of a username and password. To learn more about generating a token, refer to[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Generating a personal access token' space='MCS']. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Click Generate .

In the case of an FMU file generation error, see the detailed error message in the log file.

##### Adding JAR files to the library

An external library is required to generate the FMU files. You must add the *JAR*files to the plugin library folder.

To add the *JAR*files to the plugin library

1. Download the [ATTACHMENT filename='fmu-builder-2.6.1.jar'] and the [ATTACHMENT filename='fmu-framework-2.6.1.jar'] files.
2. Copy the downloaded JAR files to the <modeling tool installation directory>\plugins\com.nomagic.magicdraw.simulation\lib path .
3. Restart the modeling tool after copying the *JAR* files.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can generate an FMU (Functional Mock-up Unit) for external co-simulation of dynamic models that enables the exchange of simulation results between different simulation tools. The generated FMU exposes the defined input and output parameters of a model, as specified in a Simulation Configuration. The generated FMU runs the server-side simulation of a particular Simulation Configuration using given inputs and returns the outputs after each time step. FMUs can be generated for two platforms: Linux 64-bit and Windows 64-bit.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e966c364-7df9-46fa-9614-72a8d454d5a7"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Prerequisites</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li data-uuid="5c8c3d19-8d58-4368-b295-0b153206862f">You must have the same Java HOME version as the modeling tool’s Java version. To check the Java HOME version, run <code>java --version</code> in the command prompt. To check the modeling tool’s Java version, go to <strong>Help &gt; About &gt; Environment</strong> tab.</li><li data-uuid="bed41d3a-02d5-4a5b-8458-ce6b94b58d01">You must have a simulation server certificate to co-simulate the generated FMU.</li><li data-uuid="11a3c5ca-fbfe-4387-8f96-9e6dddefe141">You must use the generated FMU with tools that use the same JAVA version as the modeling tool; otherwise, you will not be able to run the generated FMU.</li><li data-uuid="463fb1ca-f54a-478f-973f-87a5db2f0039">You must add the <em>JAR</em> files to the plugin library to generate FMU. To learn more, refer to <ac:link ac:anchor="Adding JAR file to the library" />.</li></ul><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>FMU generation uses selected Simulation Configuration properties, and can contain the following defined data:</p><ul><li data-uuid="0414bdb5-7c2f-409e-8fb5-830d835c775a"><strong>Start Time, End Time, </strong>and <strong>Step Size </strong>property values.</li><li data-uuid="1ca2cede-950a-43d2-8a20-2dfe21adbf45"><strong>Input Parameters </strong>property value: the default values from the defined input parameters will be included in the generated FMU.</li><li data-uuid="63578dbe-44ec-476e-bada-7b64c82e9e29"><strong>Output Parameters </strong>property value: the default values from the defined output parameters will be included in the generated FMU.</li></ul><p><br /></p><p>To generate an FMU</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="a08b1b2c-c51e-4221-85e2-3da599165676"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Open a Teamwork Cloud project.</span></li><li data-uuid="0d2f12cc-a3b9-4dd3-b1eb-386139553997"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Right-click a Simulation Configuration and select </span><strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Generate FMU.</strong><br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Generate_FMU_Command.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li data-uuid="8b45fc66-d536-4db8-9922-eedcf12ead1f">In the <strong>Generate FMU</strong> dialog specify the following:<ul><li>In the <strong>File Directory</strong> box, specify the location where the generated file should be stored.</li><li>Select the <strong>Win64 </strong>or <strong>Lin64 </strong>radio button, depending on the platform on which you will run the generated FMU. </li><li><p>Optionally, select the <strong>Include login credentials </strong>check box and specify a server-side simulation server address and login credentials or, token to be stored in the generated FMU file.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="580df867-91be-4b31-a58e-13229a5d3e43"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>You can leave the <strong>Include login credentials</strong> check box empty <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">and provide the login credentials or token later as FMU parameters while importing an FMU file. In this case, the credentials will not be stored in the generated FMU file.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">You can also generate a personal <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">access<span> </span></span>token instead of a username and password. To learn more about generating a token, refer to </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCS" ri:content-title="Generating a personal access token" /></ac:link>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Generate FMU dialog.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></p></li></ul></li><li data-uuid="7371a98d-81a5-4150-833f-a4e57642f49f">Click <strong>Generate</strong>.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9aaf3281-df90-4fb0-987d-07749a422358"><ac:rich-text-body><p>In the case of an FMU file generation error, see the detailed error message in the log file.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3 class="auto-cursor-target">Adding JAR files to the library</h3><p>An external library is required to generate the FMU files. You must add the <em>JAR </em>files to the plugin library folder.</p><p>To add the <em>JAR </em>files to the plugin library</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="5cdb06a8-b64b-4a2b-8c82-5d1815164e8c">Download the <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="fmu-builder-2.6.1.jar" /></ac:link><em> </em>and the <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="fmu-framework-2.6.1.jar" /></ac:link> files.</li><li data-uuid="c19e1461-a0c5-4d97-9ede-3d05e3446b98">Copy the downloaded <em>JAR </em>files to the <em>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\plugins\com.nomagic.magicdraw.simulation\lib </em>path<em>.</em></li><li data-uuid="cccda86e-471c-424e-95ef-6a849fb1765c"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Restart the modeling tool after copying the <em>JAR</em> files.</span></li></ol>
````

## PAGE 00624: Getting simulation time

- page_id: `249571077`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249571077/Getting+simulation+time
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-04-07T11:40:17.582+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Action languages > Action scripts APIs > Manipulating runtime objects using scripts
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

4

##### Accessing the current simulation time

Simulation time is a timestamp that is retrieved from a simulation clock. You can use ALH API to get the simulation time of execution.

```text

```

The following code fragment shows how to get the simulation time of execution through ALH API.

```text

```

Supported time units are nanosecond(ns), microsecond(µs), millisecond(ms), second(s), minute(min), hour(h), day(d), week(w), month(mo), year(y).

##### Accessing a simulation time unit

The simulation time unit is defined in the tag "timeUnit" of an SimulationConfig stereotype. If you do not define the value of this tag, the default unit of time is the millisecond. You can use ALH API to get a simulation time unit of execution.

The following code fragment shows how to get the*TimeUnit*tag of a**SimulationConfig**through ALH API.

```text

```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2bc6051c-b04b-489a-94c8-3444d1b2297c"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Accessing the current simulation time</h3><p>Simulation time is a timestamp that is retrieved from a simulation clock. You can use ALH API to get the simulation time of execution.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="92a783e2-38fa-4949-8ef3-4a871a07be7d"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.getCurrentTime()
ALH.getCurrentTime("timeUnit")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The following code fragment shows how to get the simulation time of execution through ALH API.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0491714d-caca-4c58-be6e-b5045e9f243b"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.getCurrentTime();
ALH.getCurrentTime("ms");					//You can use either the name or abbreviation of the time unit.]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e19fb120-adc9-4f1c-8fb4-c4dfacded5b1"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Supported time units are nanosecond(ns), microsecond(<span style="color: rgb(0,29,53);">µs</span>), millisecond(ms), second(s), minute(min), hour(h), day(d), week(w), month(mo), year(y).</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3 class="auto-cursor-target">Accessing a simulation time unit</h3><p>The simulation time unit is defined in the tag &quot;timeUnit&quot; of an SimulationConfig stereotype. If you do not define the value of this tag, the default unit of time is the millisecond. You can use ALH API to get a simulation time unit of execution.</p><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">The following code fragment shows how to get the </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">TimeUnit</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> tag of a </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">SimulationConfig</strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> through ALH API.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="23f48c62-df1d-424e-ab6f-affcdbd87006"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.getTimeUnit();          // millisecond]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249569547 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00625: Getting started

- page_id: `249569547`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249569547/Getting+started
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:04.792+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide
- labels: ['getting-started']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1326481356

1326481358

1326481357

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit is a MagicDraw plugin, which provides a unique set of tools supporting the standardized construction, verification, and simulation of computational complete models based on a foundational subset of the UML. It also enables Activity, State Machine and parametric simulation (execution), animation, and debugging in UML and SysML models, including profiles, samples, and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Key features' space=''].

No Magic is the first in the industry to provide customers with an easy-to-use, standard-based executable UML solution that integrates the semantics of different UML behaviors. The purpose of this Getting Started section is to provide you with an overview of the plugin and a brief explanation of how exactly this plugin works.

1326481355

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Behavior' space='']

It also enables Activity, State Machine and parametric simulation (execution), animation, and debugging in UML and SysML models, including profiles, samples, and <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Key features" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[key features]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p>No Magic is the first in the industry to provide customers with an easy-to-use, standard-based executable UML solution that integrates the semantics of different UML behaviors. The purpose of this Getting Started section is to provide you with an overview of the plugin and a brief explanation of how exactly this plugin works.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="54ab5e8e-2365-4308-8388-8bc7c8407f0e"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1326481355</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="5ba7d632-fdd9-44aa-9ff8-f3b99fe190e5" /></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Behavior" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 00626: Getting the caller of a script

- page_id: `249571078`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249571078/Getting+the+caller+of+a+script
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-04-02T14:16:56.033+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Action languages > Action scripts APIs > Manipulating runtime objects using scripts
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Once a script has been evaluated, you can use the following ALH APIto trace from where the script was called and access the Caller.

```text

```

It will return the Caller element of the script. For example, if a Behavior that runs a script is called from a callBehavior, you will get a callBehavior action as the Caller.

The following code fragment shows how to trace from where the script was called, e.g., from *State1*, through ALH API.

```text

```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Once a script has been evaluated, you can use the following <span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">ALH API </span>to trace from where the script was called and access the Caller.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dfd0ff97-c8ef-4795-a391-39f1283d8240"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.getCaller()]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>It will return the Caller element of the script. For example, if a Behavior that runs a script is called from a callBehavior, you will get a callBehavior action as the Caller.</p><p>The following code fragment shows how to trace from where the script was called, e.g., from <em>State1</em>, through ALH API.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ade77bb0-678c-4329-8167-6ca76c6cb6cc"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.getCaller().getName();		// Trace from State1.]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249569833 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00628: Histogram

- page_id: `249569833`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249569833/Histogram
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:07.507+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling
- labels: ['histogram']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Tips

1399917931

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Tips

1399917934

1399917930

**On this page**

32

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Tips

1399917932

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit introduces built-in support for Monte Carlo analysis, allowing you to manage uncertainties and estimate how random variation of sensitive parameters affects the overall performance of the system being modeled. A Histogram shows the density of the underlying distribution of data and estimates the probability density function of underlying variables. The total area of the Histogram used for probability density is always normalized to 1. A Histogram also allows you to dynamically view updates on those statistical values during the simulation. In addition, you can customize itd properties, including title, plot color, and labels. For more information about using the Histogram in your model, see the *<install_root>\samples\simulation\HingeMonteCarloAnalysis.mdzip* sample as a demonstration.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A Histogram with dynamic statistical results (N, Mean, SD, and OutOfSpec).

#### Properties of the Histogram

The Specification dialog of the Histogram contains the properties you can select and modify to change the display of the Histogram, as shown below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The properties of the Histogram in its Specification dialog.

The following table describes the function of each property in the Specification dialog of the Histogram

| Property | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Dynamic | Set to true to open the Histogram during multiple executions to show dynamic results. Otherwise, the Histogram is opened when all executions are completed.NoteWhen the Dynamic property is set to true, the animation of the Histogram with dynamic statistical results (N, Mean, SD, and OutOfSpec) is available. |
| Grid X | Set to true to show a vertical grid line. |
| Grid Y | Set to true to show a horizontal grid line. |
| Keep Open After Termination | Set to true to keep the Histogram open after the termination of the simulation. |
| Plot Color | Specify a plot color. |
| Record Plot Data As | Specify the selected format of the file to be saved if the Result File property is specified. |
| Result File | Specify the name of the file to be saved with the results in the file format specified in the Record Plot Data As property. Otherwise, the results are saved in a model element. |
| Title | Specify the title of the Histogram. |
| X Label | Specify the label of the x-axis of the Histogram. |
| Y Label | Specify the label of the y-axis of the Histogram. |
| Number Of Bins | Specify the number of data bins in which the results are distributed. Otherwise, the number of bins is dynamic, depending on the number of samples. |

#### NOTE: Note

Note

The Histogram can be used only as a local user interface, and the **Number Of Runs**property of the SImulation Configuration must be set to more than 1.

#### Using the Histogram

To use the Histogram

1. Create a Histogram element to represent a Classifier, e.g., Hinge Analysis .
2. In the Specification window of the Histogram, specify the Value property and the properties whose values will be monitored in the Histogram. These properties must be the members of the Classifier represented by the Histogram, e.g., hinge.clearance .
3. Specify other Histogram properties if necessary (see the property table).
4. Drag the Histogram to a Simulation Configuration.
5. Make sure that the value of the UI property of the Simulation Configuration is set to the created Histogram.
6. Run the Simulation Configuration.

#### TIP: Tips

Tips

You can export the Histogram data as a CSV file by clicking the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] icon at the top-left of the Histogram pane. The Histogram is then exported as a CSV file, as shown below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Histogram is exported as a CSV file.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="084d5af1-fc21-438d-bebe-f54aa7d38679"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1399917931</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="79c734fc-0775-4fdd-b5c0-7a1ab771a4fa"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1399917934</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="01314ade-99e3-4d7d-bb81-70122b01627c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1399917930</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6b4ac3c2-e0d1-461b-8e51-c38b9b5bf5ad"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">3</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="minLevel">2</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7dd7c85a-a4e3-429e-aeaa-9322507933a1"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1399917932</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit introduces built-in support for Monte Carlo analysis, allowing you to manage uncertainties and estimate how random variation of sensitive parameters affects the overall performance of the system being modeled. A Histogram shows the density of the underlying distribution of data and estimates the probability density function of underlying variables. The total area of the Histogram used for probability density is always normalized to 1. A Histogram also allows you to dynamically view updates on those statistical values during the simulation. In addition, you can customize itd properties, including title, plot color, and labels. For more information about using the Histogram in your model, see the <em>&lt;install_root&gt;\samples\simulation\HingeMonteCarloAnalysis.mdzip</em> sample as a demonstration.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Histogram - Monte Carlo.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">A Histogram with dynamic statistical results (N, Mean, SD, and OutOfSpec).</h6><h2>Properties of the Histogram</h2><p>The Specification dialog of the Histogram contains the properties you can select and modify to change the display of the Histogram, as shown below.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Histogram.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The properties of the Histogram in its Specification dialog.</h6><p>The following table describes the function of each property in the Specification dialog of the Histogram</p><table class="relative-table wrapped" style="width: 99.9444%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 19.9637%;" /><col style="width: 80.0678%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Property</th><th>Description</th></tr><tr><td><strong>Dynamic</strong></td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p>Set to <em>true</em> to open the Histogram during multiple executions to show dynamic results. Otherwise, the Histogram is opened when all executions are completed.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e8747645-0661-4d08-9189-a3674621603d"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>When the <strong>Dynamic</strong> property is set to <strong>true</strong>, the animation of the Histogram with dynamic statistical results (<strong>N</strong>, <strong>Mean</strong>, <strong>SD</strong>, and <strong>OutOfSpec</strong>) is available.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></div></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Grid X</strong></td><td>Set to <em>true</em> to show a vertical grid line.</td></tr><tr><td><strong>Grid Y</strong></td><td>Set to <em>true</em> to show a horizontal grid line.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><div class="content-wrapper"><p><strong>Keep Open After Termination</strong></p></div></td><td colspan="1">Set to <em>true</em> to keep the Histogram open after the termination of the simulation.</td></tr><tr><td><strong>Plot Color</strong></td><td>Specify a plot color.</td></tr><tr><td><strong>Record Plot Data As</strong></td><td>Specify the selected format of the file to be saved if the <strong>Result File</strong> property is specified.</td></tr><tr><td><strong>Result File</strong></td><td>Specify the name of the file to be saved with the results in the file format specified in the <strong>Record Plot Data As</strong> property. Otherwise, the results are saved in a model element.</td></tr><tr><td><strong>Title</strong></td><td>Specify the title of the Histogram.</td></tr><tr><td><strong>X Label</strong></td><td>Specify the label of the x-axis of the Histogram.</td></tr><tr><td><strong>Y Label</strong></td><td>Specify the label of the y-axis of the Histogram.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><strong>Number Of Bins</strong></td><td colspan="1"><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">Specify the number of data bins in which the results are distributed. Otherwise, the number of bins is dynamic, depending on the number of samples.</span></td></tr></tbody></table><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2b3b9143-fb7d-45ee-af8a-b385aedbe550"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The Histogram can be used only as a local user interface, and the <strong>Number Of Runs </strong>property of the SImulation Configuration must be set to more than 1.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><h2>Using the Histogram</h2><p>To use the Histogram</p><hr /><ol><li>Create a Histogram element to represent a Classifier, e.g., <em>Hinge Analysis</em>.</li><li>In the Specification window of the Histogram, specify the <strong>Value</strong> property and the properties whose values will be monitored in the Histogram. These properties must be the members of the Classifier represented by the Histogram, e.g., <em>hinge.clearance</em>.</li><li>Specify other Histogram properties if necessary (see the property table).</li><li>Drag the Histogram to a Simulation Configuration.</li><li>Make sure that the value of the <strong>UI </strong>property of the Simulation Configuration is set to the created Histogram.</li><li><p>Run the Simulation Configuration.</p></li></ol><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a7a698f7-e5e9-4f58-90db-b0414efb81fe"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Tips</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can export the Histogram data as a CSV file by clicking the <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Export CSV Button.png" /></ac:image> icon at the top-left of the Histogram pane. The Histogram is then exported as a CSV file, as shown below.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Histogram CSV Exported File.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Histogram is exported as a CSV file.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249569893 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00629: HTML UI

- page_id: `249569893`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249569893/HTML+UI
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:07.979+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit enables you to export, open, and use HTML UI mockups and control model simulations within an embedded browser. To automatically generate the HTML files, you must set the **Show UI As HTML** property to *true*before running a **SimulationConfig.**While the simulation is running, hyperlinks to the generated HTML files are saved in the local project folder and will appear in the **Simulation Console** tab. The corresponding HTML UI is also opened in the embedded browser. You can customize these HTML files at any time and regenerate them using the **Generate HTML** button on the toolbar of the UI modeling diagram.

To learn more about HTML UI, refer to the following pages:

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p style="text-align: left;" data-start="83" data-end="226">Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit enables you to export, open, and use HTML UI mockups and control model simulations within an embedded browser. To automatically generate the HTML files, you must set the <strong>Show UI As HTML</strong> property to <em>true </em>before running a <strong>SimulationConfig. </strong>While the simulation is running, hyperlinks to the generated HTML files are saved in the local project folder and will appear in the <strong data-start="497" data-end="519">Simulation Console</strong> tab. The corresponding HTML UI is also opened in the embedded browser. You can customize these HTML files at any time and regenerate them using the <strong data-start="668" data-end="685">Generate HTML</strong> button on the toolbar of the UI modeling diagram.</p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="c60bc3ef-4c83-4b50-861b-62984c68033f">To</ac:inline-comment-marker> learn more about HTML UI, refer to the following pages:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="ae60b508-c793-42b3-85be-9b01b871dd5a" /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249569714 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00630: ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage

- page_id: `249569714`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249569714/ImageSwitcher+and+ActiveImage
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-06-08T10:01:57.040+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling
- labels: ['image-switcher', 'active-image']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1354113671

1354113673

1354113672

ImageSwitcher is a predefined subtype of UI config. It is a simple, yet flexible and powerful animation tool. You can use ImageSwitcher to represent the state or the enumeration value of a runtime object. To easily create an «ImageSwitcher» element, specify a represented Classifier, and create as many attributes and different States as you wish to see them animate. Each attribute is called an «ActiveImage» and has the following properties

- Image An image that will be used in animation either from browsing the file or dragging the image directly from a web browser.
- activeElement An element that will use an image once it is activated. An active element can be set to represent an active State, Action, or an Enumeration Literal.
- onClick A signal that will be triggered once an image is clicked.

1354113670

**Related pages**

It is a simple, yet flexible and powerful animation tool. You can use ImageSwitcher to represent the state or the enumeration value of a runtime object. To easily create an «ImageSwitcher» element, specify a represented Classifier, and create as many attributes and different States as you wish to see them animate. Each attribute is called an «ActiveImage» and has the following properties</p><ul><li><strong>Image<br /></strong>An image that will be used in animation either from browsing the file or dragging the image directly from a web browser.</li><li><strong>activeElement</strong><br />An element that will use an image once it is activated. An active element can be set to represent an active State, Action, or an Enumeration Literal.</li><li><strong>onClick</strong><br />A signal that will be triggered once an image is clicked. </li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f2580dc0-cb1d-4853-b4e4-cb6bb58a9eef"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1354113670</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="8b5270fc-58f5-4f0a-a297-4171960c19f7" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249571018 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00631: Importing external libraries

- page_id: `249571018`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249571018/Importing+external+libraries
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-09-12T15:08:38.466+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Action languages
- labels: ['importing-external-libraries', 'external-libraries']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

When you use a scripting language in your model, you may need to call some external libraries to do some specific tasks. Therefore, you must add external libraries to your project before executing it.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

Only *.jar files are supported as external libraries.

To add an external library to a project

1. On the main menu, click Options and select Project . The Project Options dialog opens.
2. On the left pane, click General > Simulation>External Libraries.
3. Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to open the Select Files and/or Directories dialog. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. In the Select Files and/or Directories dialog, click **Add. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']**
5. In the Open dialog, select the required file and click **Open**. The path filename will be added to theSelect Filesand/or Directoriesdialog. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] Once you click **Open**, a Use Path Variables dialog may appear. The dialog suggests an alternative file path name. Clear the checkbox if you wish not to see the dialog in the future. To learn more, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating path variables' space='MT'].
6. Confirm the path file name in the Select Files and/or Directories dialog and click **OK**. Once you click **OK**, a Use Path Variables dialog may appear. The dialog suggests an alternative file path name. Clear the checkbox if you wish not to see the dialog in the future. To learn more, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating path variables' space='MT'].
7. Confirm the path file name in the Project Options dialog and click **OK**.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>When you use a scripting language in your model, you may need to call some external libraries to do some specific tasks. Therefore, you must add external libraries to your project before executing it.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="802c94a2-8f19-4800-b067-a1955d7a5dce"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Only *.jar files are supported as external libraries.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To add an external library to a project <span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);"> </span></p><hr /><ol><li>On the main menu, click<span> </span><strong>Options</strong><span> </span>and select<span> </span><strong>Project</strong>.
````
