<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=290029610 space=MMA version=8 -->
## PAGE 00633: Instance selection for Behavioral Diagram

- page_id: `290029610`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/290029610/Instance+selection+for+Behavioral+Diagram
- version_number: 8
- version_date: `2026-02-11T16:28:44.395+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Animation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can filter animation for one specific object in a Behavioral Diagram, such as a State Machine, Activity, or Sequence Diagram.If several instances of the same behavior exist, the Behavioral Diagram automatically shows animation for only one instance. By default, the diagram shows animation for the first instance that annotated the diagram. This prevents the diagram from merging events from multiple concurrent instances and improves clarity during simulation analysis. You can switch the displayed instance from the contextual menu.

- The list shows only the objects that currently annotate the diagram or have annotated it previously. You cannot pre-select an object that has not started this behavior.
- If multiple sessions of the same behavior run under the same object, the diagram shows their animation together.
- The **Instance** command appears only when multiple objects are annotated in the currently opened diagram.

To select an instance

1. Open the State Machine, Activity, or Sequence diagram.
2. Right-click anywhere in the diagram, select **Instances,** and choose the required object instance from the list. You can select an instance in both the open diagram and the diagram preview. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">You can filter animation for one specific object in a Behavioral Diagram, such as a State Machine, Activity, or Sequence Diagram. </span>If several instances of the same behavior exist, the Behavioral Diagram automatically shows animation for only one instance. By default, the diagram shows animation for the first instance that annotated the diagram. This prevents the diagram from merging events from multiple concurrent instances and improves clarity during simulation analysis. You can switch the displayed instance from the contextual menu.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="48b8f64c-32c4-49b1-bf64-2e29f636d245"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li data-uuid="cba3d2bd-f8ce-401d-8d69-de11a995f09f"><p>The list shows only the objects that currently annotate the diagram or have annotated it previously. You cannot pre-select an object that has not started this behavior.</p></li><li data-uuid="ea610ec4-9d88-4b83-8d6f-bd5ad68eeac6"><p>If multiple sessions of the same behavior run under the same object, the diagram shows their animation together.</p></li><li data-uuid="b1343b9e-b822-4f15-af45-f644c4078228"><p>The <strong>Instance</strong> command appears only when multiple objects are annotated in the currently opened diagram.</p></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To select an instance</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="2bdfea53-090f-43b9-a9a6-ca867a3850b2"><p>Open the State Machine, Activity, or Sequence diagram.</p></li><li data-uuid="26956a03-ec5c-4c13-a8e8-e6546ff1b97b"><p>Right-click anywhere in the diagram, select <strong>Instances,</strong> and choose the required object instance from the list.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="67d0401f-dca1-4ab3-9730-d022861247c6"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can select an instance in both the open diagram and the diagram preview.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Instance selection.png" /></ac:image></p></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249569608 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00634: Instance Specification

- page_id: `249569608`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249569608/Instance+Specification
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:05.574+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Model simulation > Elements simulation
- labels: ['instance-specification-simulation', 'simulating-instance-specifications']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can also run an InstanceSpecification. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will initialize an object from the Classifier of the Instance Specification. The structure values of the object will be loaded from the slot of the Instance Specification rather than from the default value of the properties of the Classifier.

To run an Instance Specification

- Right-click an Instance Specification and select either Simulation > Run or Simulation > Run with Context if the classifier of the Instance Specification is a Behavior. You can also specify an Instance Specification as a target of Simulation Configuration.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can run an Instance Specification whose Classifier is a Behavior. In such case, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will initialize the object from the Behavior and load the slot values to the object. Then it will simulate the object in the same way as it simulates the object initialized from a Behavior.

If the Behavior, the Classifier of the Instance Specification, has a SysML Adjunct property whose primary is a parameter of the Behavior (See [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Adjunct property' space='']), Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will set the value of the parameter with the value of the Adjunct property. Therefore, you can use a slot value of the Adjunct property to set the initial value of the parameter.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Simulation of an Instance Specification whose Classifier is a Behavior (with Adjunct property).

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can also run an <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="9abeb3db-6a23-4c65-bb81-0e5a9fa54af1">InstanceSpecification</ac:inline-comment-marker>. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will initialize an object from the Classifier of the Instance Specification. The structure values of the object will be loaded from the slot of the Instance Specification rather than from the default value of the properties of the Classifier. </p><p>To run an Instance Specification</p><hr /><ul><li>Right-click an Instance Specification and select either <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Run</strong> or <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Run with</strong> <strong>Context</strong> if the classifier of the Instance Specification is a Behavior. You can also specify an Instance Specification as a target of Simulation Configuration. </li></ul><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Figure_0012.png" /></ac:image></p><p>You can run an Instance Specification whose Classifier is a Behavior. In such case, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will initialize the object from the Behavior and load the slot values to the object. Then it will simulate the object in the same way as it simulates the object initialized from a Behavior. </p><p>If the Behavior, the Classifier of the Instance Specification, has a SysML Adjunct property whose primary is a parameter of the Behavior (See <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Adjunct property" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Adjunct Property]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>), Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will set the value of the parameter with the value of the Adjunct property. Therefore, you can use a slot value of the Adjunct property to set the initial value of the parameter. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:alt="Simulation of an Instance Specification whose Classifier is a Behavior (with Adjunct Property)" ac:title="Simulation of an Instance Specification whose Classifier is a Behavior (with Adjunct Property)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="110037_2016-06-24+14_32_10-Photos (1).png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Simulation of an Instance Specification whose Classifier is a Behavior (with Adjunct property).</h6><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249571547 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00635: Integrating widgets for simulation

- page_id: `249571547`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249571547/Integrating+widgets+for+simulation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-02T12:01:37.882+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > Tutorial
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

After you find any interesting widgets, please follow the steps below to integrate them for simulation. A free widget, named jQuery Knob, will be used in this demonstration. This widget can be found and downloaded via this [link](https://github.com/aterrien/jQuery-Knob).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The jQuery Knob widget.

To integrate widgets for simulation

1. See what information is needed to create the widget, e.g., the jQuery Knob widget needs an input element, scripts, and jQuery Knob library, as displayed below. textCode example from https://github.com/aterrien/jQuery-Knob$(function() {
 $(".dial").knob();
 });]]>
2. Create a **widget.HTML** file with the content below. textwidget.HTML@title@
 

 
 
]]> Information**class="widget"** informs the simulation that this file is widget.**paths="@paths@"** is used for generating HTML code from the widget, and the simulation will use the value of the paths attribute to register for value change. Note**@title@** can be optionally replaced with any title. It is a predefined variable in simulation which will be replaced by the property name whose type is of this widget.
3. Create a new **js** folder. Copy the jQuery Knob library to the created **js** folder. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] NoteDifferent widgets require different numbers of jQuery library files, including CSS files. Please ensure that you copy and include all those files.
4. Import required jQuery libraries and CSS files, if any, to the HTML head element, and add the widget code to the body element, as shown below. textwidget.HTML@title@

 
 

 
 
 
 $(function(){
 $(".dial").knob();
 });
 
]]>
5. Because this widget requires the jQuery library, it needs to also import the jQuery library before the widget import statement. Alternatively, the **@scripts@** predefined variable can be used so that simulation will generate jQuery files and replace this variable with jQuery import statements. In addition, the **@simulation_js@** predefined variable must be added as the last import statement in the head element because simulation will replace this variable with the core simulation javascript import statement. See the code below for the detail in this step. textwidget.HTML@title@

 @scripts@
 
 @simulation_js@
 

 
 

 $(function(){
 $(".dial").knob();
 });
 
]]>
6. Add the following attributes to the widget element, where the value of the **paths** attribute is the name of a property/Port that will be used as input/output in simulation. text Apply the added attributes to the widget element as shown below. textwidget.HTML@title@

 @scripts@
 
 @simulation_js@
 

 
 

 $(function(){
 $(".dial").knob();
 });
 
]]>
7. If the widget can be used as an output to display a value from simulation, you can override the **customSetHTMLValue** method and set the widget value in the overridden method, e.g., the jQuery Knob widget sets the value by using the following code. textCode example from https://github.com/aterrien/jQuery-Knob$('.dial')
 .val(27)
 .trigger('change');
]]> It can then be integrated for simulation as follows. textwidget.HTML@title@

 @scripts@
 
 @simulation_js@
 

 
 

 $(function(){
 $(".dial").knob();
 });

 /**
 * This method will be called when a value of the widget element is changed.
 * @param item is an element with matching attribute paths in the body and the element itself.
 * @param data represents a new value.
 * @param formattedValue indicates a new value formatted by the simulation.
 */

 function customSetHTMLValue(item, data, formattedValue) {
 if ($(item).is($(".dial"))) {
 $(item).val(data).trigger('change');
 }
 }
 
]]> NoteThe line of code shown below is needed to prevent the **customSetHTMLValue** method from being called when there is a value change from any other widgets with the same property paths. The following line of code checks if the updating widget (the **item** parameter), is the same element as this Knob widget.text
8. If the widget can be used as an input to change simulation value, you can call the **doSetWidgetValue** method when the widget changes the value, e.g., the jQuery Knob widget has a hook to tell when the value is changed, as demonstrated below. textCode example from https://github.com/aterrien/jQuery-Knob$(".dial").knob({
 'change' : function (v) { console.log(v); }
 });]]> This is integrated for simulation as following. textwidget.HTML@title@

 @scripts@
 
 @simulation_js@
 

 
 

 $(function() {
 $(".dial").knob({
 'release' : function(value) {
 //call the doSetWidgetValue method to set a simulation value.
 doSetWidgetValue($(".dial"), value);
 }
 });
 });

 /**
 * This method will be called when a value of the widget element is changed.
 * @param item is an element with matching attribute paths in the body and the element itself.
 * @param data represents a new value.
 * @param formattedValue indicates a new value formatted by the simulation.
 */

 function customSetHTMLValue(item, data, formattedValue) {
 if ($(item).is($(".dial"))) {
 $(item).val(data).trigger('change');
 }
 }
 
]]>
9. If the widget has options, these options can be modified to customize in the widget Class in MagicDraw, e.g., the jQuery Knob widget has the **min** and **max** options in the code below. textCode example from https://github.com/aterrien/jQuery-Knob When integrated for simulation, the value for each option can be defined by using the **$widget_property$** format, where **widget_property** is the name of a property owned by the widget Class in MagicDraw as shown below. textwidget.HTML@title@

 @scripts@
 
 @simulation_js@
 

 
 

 $(function(){
 $(".dial").knob({
 'min' : $min$,
 'max' : $max$,
 'release' : function(value) {
 //call the doSetWidgetValue method to set simulation value.
 doSetWidgetValue($(".dial"), value);
 }
 });
 });

 /**
 * This method will be called when a value of the widget element is changed.
 * @param item is an element with matching attribute paths in the body and the element itself.
 * @param data represents a new value.
 * @param formattedValue indicates a new value formatted by the simulation.
 */

 function customSetHTMLValue(item, data, formattedValue) {
 if ($(item).is($(".dial"))) {
 $(item).val(data).trigger('change');
 }
 }
 
]]>
10. Archive the **widget.HTML** file and the **js** folder into a zip file. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] NoteThe **widget.HTML** file and all related resources must be archived into a zip file and the **widget.HTML** file must be the root directory of the zip file.
11. Open MagicDraw and create a new Simulation project. 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] InformationYou can also create other kinds of project, but you need to use the Simulation profile in your created project before proceeding to the next steps.
12. Create a Class element in the Containment browser. [ATTACHMENT filename='05_create_class.png']
13. Apply the created Class with the « Widget » stereotype. [ATTACHMENT filename='06_apply_widget_ster.png']
14. Drag the zipped widget file from File Explorer to the widget Class and select Create Attached File . [ATTACHMENT filename='07_create_attached_file.png']
15. Create a Port element with any corresponding widget type. The name of the Port must match the value of the paths attribute of the widget element in the widget.HTML file. [ATTACHMENT filename='08_create_port_value.png']
16. Create a property for each option with any corresponding type. Also, the default value must be set for each property to prevent javascript syntax errors when running simulation. [ATTACHMENT filename='09_create_widget_properties.png']
17. Capture the screen of jQuery Knob on the jQuery Knob Web site and save it as an image to be used as the icon of the widget Class.
18. Open the Specification dialog of the widget Class. Find and select the value field of the **Image** property to set an icon for the widget Class. [ATTACHMENT filename='10_set_widget_icon.png']
19. Click the … button to open the browse dialog and select the captured image file. [ATTACHMENT filename='11_select_icon.png']
20. Create the SysML Block diagram and a property with Integer as type, and specify 10 as the default value, as illustrated below. [ATTACHMENT filename='12_create_block.png']
21. Create the SysML Internal Block diagram under the System Block. [ATTACHMENT filename='13_create_ibd.png']
22. Drag the Knob widget in the Containment browser to the IBD to create a widget symbol. [ATTACHMENT filename='14_dnd_widget.png']
23. Specify a name for the widget property and create a binding Connector between the **value** Port of the widget and the **value** property. [ATTACHMENT filename='15_create_binding.png']
24. Open the Simulation Config diagram. Drag the System Block in the Containment browser to the Simulation Config element to set as the execution target. [ATTACHMENT filename='16_set_execution_target.png']
25. Right-click the Simulation Config element. Set the Auto Start option as **true** and the Silent option as **false**. [ATTACHMENT filename='17_set_config_options.png']
26. Run the Simulation Config element and open the System IBD to see the result of widget integration. [ATTACHMENT filename='18_simulation_result.png']

###### Predefined variables

The table below explains how predefined variables will be replaced when widget files are generated.

| Variable name | Resolved text after generating widgets |
| --- | --- |
| @title@ | A name of the Part property representing the widget. |
| @scripts@ | Predefined javascript import statements:<script type="text/javascript" src="js/jquery-1.12.4.min.js"></script><script type="text/javascript" src="js/jquery-ui-1.12.0.min.js"></script> |
| @simulation_js@ | Core simulation javascript import statement:<script type="text/javascript" src="js/Simulation.js"></script> |
| @paths@ | Auto-generated paths to the widget. |
| $widget_property$ | widget_property as the name of a property, owned by the widget Class in MagicDraw. This variable will be replaced by the default value of the property or the slot value of the widget instance. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>After you find any interesting widgets, please follow the steps below to integrate them for simulation. A free widget, named jQuery Knob, will be used in this demonstration. This widget can be found and downloaded via this <a href="https://github.com/aterrien/jQuery-Knob">link</a>.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="01_jquery_knob.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The jQuery Knob widget.</h6><p>To integrate widgets for simulation</p><hr /><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">See what information is needed to create the widget, e.g., the jQuery Knob widget needs an input element, scripts, and jQuery Knob library, as displayed below.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9a3b2a62-b410-4084-b160-e7c7e16cc8ac"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Code example from https://github.com/aterrien/jQuery-Knob</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[<input type="text" value="75" class="dial">

<script>
	$(function() {
		$(".dial").knob();
    });
</script>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Create a <strong>widget.HTML</strong> file with the content below.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f193b5fa-45aa-4a62-a8d9-a8bb5ac0f54d"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">widget.HTML</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[<!doctype html>
<html>
	<head>
		<title>@title@</title>
	</head>

	<body class="widget" paths="@paths@">
	</body>
</html>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="28fc9a1f-4411-4c0b-9edb-e996efca3726"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Information</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><strong>class=&quot;widget&quot;</strong> informs the simulation that this file is widget.</li><li><strong>paths=&quot;@paths@&quot;</strong> is used for generating HTML code from the widget, and the simulation will use the value of the paths attribute to register for value change.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="98d47a26-44af-4231-965a-93d0fa2ee8f9"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>@title@</strong> can be optionally replaced with any title. It is a predefined variable in simulation which will be replaced by the property name whose type is of this widget.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Create a new <strong>js</strong> folder. Copy the jQuery Knob library to the created  <strong>js</strong> folder.</p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="02_folder_structure.png" /></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b72bef0e-2cc7-408e-be4e-b1adaf6152c6"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Different widgets require different numbers of jQuery library files, including CSS files. Please ensure that you copy and include all those files.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Import required jQuery libraries and CSS files, if any, to the HTML head element, and add the widget code to the body element, as shown below.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="062b3d4d-9136-4075-8dd5-dc5b4afbca36"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">widget.HTML</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[<!doctype html>
<html>
	<head>
		<title>@title@</title>

		<script type="text/javascript" src="js/jquery.knob.js"></script>
	</head>

	<body class="widget" paths="@paths@">
		<input type="text" class="dial">
		
		<script>
			$(function(){
				$(".dial").knob();
			});
		</script>
	</body>
</html>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Because this widget requires the jQuery library, it needs to also import the jQuery library before the widget import statement. Alternatively, the <strong>@scripts@</strong> predefined variable can be used so that simulation will generate jQuery files and replace this variable with jQuery import statements. In addition, the <strong>@simulation_js@</strong> predefined variable must be added as the last import statement in the head element because simulation will replace this variable with the core simulation javascript import statement. See the code below for the detail in this step.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ed0be334-8170-4b21-beb6-b3245871dcff"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">widget.HTML</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[<!doctype html>
<html>
	<head>
		<title>@title@</title>

		@scripts@
		<script type="text/javascript" src="js/jquery.knob.js"></script>
		@simulation_js@
	</head>

	<body class="widget" paths="@paths@">
		<input type="text" class="dial">

		<script>
			$(function(){
				$(".dial").knob();
			});
		</script>
	</body>
</html>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Add the following attributes to the widget element, where the value of the <strong>paths</strong> attribute is the name of a property/Port that will be used as input/output in simulation.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="40bcf4b9-3c95-40e7-aebb-e1b6c19b7921"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[runtime="true" pathType="widget" paths="value"]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">Apply the added attributes to the widget element as shown below.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2a310a0d-012d-4d6b-ad74-75b01e409c34"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">widget.HTML</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[<!doctype html>
<html>
	<head>
		<title>@title@</title>

		@scripts@
		<script type="text/javascript" src="js/jquery.knob.js"></script>
		@simulation_js@
	</head>

	<body class="widget" paths="@paths@">
		<input type="text" class="dial" runtime="true" pathType="widget" paths="value">

		<script>
			$(function(){
				$(".dial").knob();
			});
		</script>
	</body>
</html>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">If the widget can be used as an output to display a value from simulation, you can override the <strong>customSetHTMLValue</strong> method and set the widget value in the overridden method, e.g., the jQuery Knob widget sets the value by using the following code.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9ee6cba4-5cdf-4b5d-8df6-b1ef3c73234b"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Code example from https://github.com/aterrien/jQuery-Knob</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[<script>
    $('.dial')
        .val(27)
        .trigger('change');
</script>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">It can then be integrated for simulation as follows.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c3027e72-cd27-4022-82ee-336a5a4c0d77"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">widget.HTML</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[<!doctype html>
<html>
	<head>
		<title>@title@</title>

		@scripts@
		<script type="text/javascript" src="js/jquery.knob.js"></script>
		@simulation_js@
	</head>

	<body class="widget" paths="@paths@">
		<input type="text" class="dial" runtime="true" pathType="widget" paths="value">

		<script>
			$(function(){
				$(".dial").knob();
			});

			/**
			* This method will be called when a value of the widget element is changed.
			* @param item is an element with matching attribute paths in the body and the element itself.
			* @param data represents a new value.
			* @param formattedValue indicates a new value formatted by the simulation.
			*/

			function customSetHTMLValue(item, data, formattedValue) {
				if ($(item).is($(".dial"))) {
					$(item).val(data).trigger('change');
				}
			}
		</script>
	</body>
</html>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="744b85e0-f321-4542-9be6-a4faa7551782"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The line of code shown below is needed to prevent the <strong>customSetHTMLValue</strong> method from being called when there is a value change from any other widgets with the same property paths. The following line of code checks if the updating widget (the <strong>item</strong> parameter), is the same element as this Knob widget.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a6ce59cc-4582-4531-9ed9-90c7c5cd98cd"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[if ($(item).is($(".dial"))) {]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">If the widget can be used as an input to change simulation value, you can call the <strong>doSetWidgetValue</strong> method when the widget changes the value, e.g., the jQuery Knob widget has a hook to tell when the value is changed, as demonstrated below.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1a9dc050-2a42-4869-a9b1-23cf16d99b95"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Code example from https://github.com/aterrien/jQuery-Knob</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[<input type="text" value="75" class="dial">
 
<script>
    $(".dial").knob({
        'change' : function (v) { console.log(v); }
    });
</script>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">This is integrated for simulation as following.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ad71d91e-4d67-41f9-8104-b783124abcc1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">widget.HTML</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[<!doctype html>
<html>
	<head>
		<title>@title@</title>

		@scripts@
		<script type="text/javascript" src="js/jquery.knob.js"></script>
		@simulation_js@
	</head>

	<body class="widget" paths="@paths@">
		<input type="text" class="dial" runtime="true" pathType="widget" paths="value">

		<script>
			$(function() {
				$(".dial").knob({
					'release' : function(value) {
						//call the doSetWidgetValue method to set a simulation value.
						doSetWidgetValue($(".dial"), value);
					}
				});
			});

			/**
			* This method will be called when a value of the widget element is changed.
			* @param item is an element with matching attribute paths in the body and the element itself.
			* @param data represents a new value.
			* @param formattedValue indicates a new value formatted by the simulation.
			*/

			function customSetHTMLValue(item, data, formattedValue) {
				if ($(item).is($(".dial"))) {
					$(item).val(data).trigger('change');
				}
			}
		</script>
	</body>
</html>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">If the widget has options, these options can be modified to customize in the widget Class in MagicDraw, e.g., the jQuery Knob widget has the <strong>min</strong> and <strong>max</strong> options in the code below.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="61ca7376-95a7-44cb-a69a-2d7cb989c8f3"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Code example from https://github.com/aterrien/jQuery-Knob</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$(".dial").knob({
    'min':-50,
    'max':50
});]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">When integrated for simulation, the value for each option can be defined by using the <strong>$widget_property$</strong> format, where <strong>widget_property</strong> is the name of a property owned by the widget Class in MagicDraw as shown below.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6e5e0452-3dae-4f28-acb7-41224e1c62f3"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">widget.HTML</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[<!doctype html>
<html>
	<head>
		<title>@title@</title>

		@scripts@
		<script type="text/javascript" src="js/jquery.knob.js"></script>
		@simulation_js@
	</head>

	<body class="widget" paths="@paths@">
		<input type="text" class="dial" runtime="true" pathType="widget" paths="value">

		<script>
			$(function(){
				$(".dial").knob({
					'min' : $min$,
					'max' : $max$,
					'release' : function(value) {
						//call the doSetWidgetValue method to set simulation value.
						doSetWidgetValue($(".dial"), value);
					}
				});
			});

			/**
			* This method will be called when a value of the widget element is changed.
			* @param item is an element with matching attribute paths in the body and the element itself.
			* @param data represents a new value.
			* @param formattedValue indicates a new value formatted by the simulation.
			*/

			function customSetHTMLValue(item, data, formattedValue) {
				if ($(item).is($(".dial"))) {
					$(item).val(data).trigger('change');
				}
			}
		</script>
	</body>
</html>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Archive the <strong>widget.HTML</strong> file and the <strong>js</strong> folder into a zip file.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="03_create_zip_file.png" /></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c61dfac7-8e76-478f-9b3d-ff8cb9b44369"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The <strong>widget.HTML</strong> file and all related resources must be archived into a zip file and the <strong>widget.HTML</strong> file must be the root directory of the zip file.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Open MagicDraw and create a new Simulation project.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="04_create_simulation_project.png" /></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7680a479-1851-47f2-9cf3-5d6a04e0268a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Information</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can also create other kinds of project, but you need to use the Simulation profile in your created project before proceeding to the next steps.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li>Create a Class element in the Containment browser.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="05_create_class.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Apply the created Class with the «<strong>Widget</strong>» stereotype.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="06_apply_widget_ster.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Drag the zipped widget file from File Explorer to the widget Class and select <strong>Create Attached File</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="07_create_attached_file.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Create a Port element with any corresponding widget type. The name of the Port must  match the value of the <strong>paths</strong> attribute of the widget element in the <strong>widget.HTML</strong> file.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="08_create_port_value.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Create a property for each option with any corresponding type. Also, the default value must be set for each property to prevent javascript syntax errors when running simulation.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="09_create_widget_properties.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Capture the screen of jQuery Knob on the jQuery Knob Web site and save it as an image to be used as the icon of the widget Class.</li><li><p>Open the Specification dialog of the widget Class. Find and select the value field of the <strong>Image</strong> property to set an icon for the widget Class.</p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="10_set_widget_icon.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click the <strong>…</strong> button to open the browse dialog and select the captured image file.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="11_select_icon.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Create the SysML Block diagram and a property with Integer as type, and specify 10 as the default value, as illustrated below.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="12_create_block.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Create the SysML Internal Block diagram under the <strong>System</strong> Block.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="13_create_ibd.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Drag the <strong>Knob</strong> widget in the Containment browser to the IBD to create a widget symbol.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="14_dnd_widget.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li><p>Specify a name for the widget property and create a binding Connector between the <strong>value</strong> Port of the widget and the <strong>value</strong> property.</p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="15_create_binding.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Open the Simulation Config diagram. Drag the <strong>System</strong> Block in the Containment browser to the <strong>Simulation Config</strong> element to set as the execution target.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="16_set_execution_target.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="b76bd5a0-96c4-43d8-b8ec-b460d79fbce4">Right-click the </ac:inline-comment-marker><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="b76bd5a0-96c4-43d8-b8ec-b460d79fbce4">Simulation Config</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="b76bd5a0-96c4-43d8-b8ec-b460d79fbce4"> element. Set the </ac:inline-comment-marker><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="b76bd5a0-96c4-43d8-b8ec-b460d79fbce4">Auto Start</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="b76bd5a0-96c4-43d8-b8ec-b460d79fbce4"> option as <strong>true</strong> and the </ac:inline-comment-marker><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="b76bd5a0-96c4-43d8-b8ec-b460d79fbce4">Silent</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="b76bd5a0-96c4-43d8-b8ec-b460d79fbce4"> option as <strong>false</strong>.</ac:inline-comment-marker><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="17_set_config_options.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Run the <strong>Simulation Config</strong> element and open the <strong>System</strong> IBD to see the result of widget integration.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="18_simulation_result.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li></ol><h4>Predefined variables</h4><p>The table below explains how predefined variables will be replaced when widget files are generated.</p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Variable name</strong></p></td><td><p><strong>Resolved text after generating widgets</strong></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@title@</p></td><td><p>A name of the Part property representing the widget.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@scripts@</p></td><td><p>Predefined javascript import statements:</p><p><strong>&lt;script type=&quot;text/javascript&quot; src=&quot;js/jquery-1.12.4.min.js&quot;&gt;&lt;/script&gt;</strong></p><p><strong>&lt;script type=&quot;text/javascript&quot; src=&quot;js/jquery-ui-1.12.0.min.js&quot;&gt;&lt;/script&gt;</strong></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@simulation_js@</p></td><td><p>Core simulation javascript import statement:</p><p><strong>&lt;script type=&quot;text/javascript&quot; src=&quot;js/Simulation.js&quot;&gt;&lt;/script&gt;</strong></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@paths@</p></td><td><p>Auto-generated paths to the widget.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>$widget_property$</p></td><td><p><strong>widget_property</strong> as the name of a property, owned by the widget Class in MagicDraw. This variable will be replaced by the default value of the property or the slot value of the widget instance.</p></td></tr></tbody></table><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570820 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00636: Integration with Dymola

- page_id: `249570820`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570820/Integration+with+Dymola
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2021-10-26T11:27:56.661+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator > Integration with external Evaluators
- labels: ['dymola-integration']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

976296314

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

976296325

976296313

**On this page**

33

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

976296315

##### Dymola overview

[Dymola](https://www.3ds.com/products-services/catia/products/dymola/), an abbreviation for Dynamic Modeling Laboratory, is a complete tool for Modelica modeling and simulation of integrated complex systems used with automotive, aerospace, robotics, process and other applications. You can download and install Dymola from [https://www.3ds.com/products-services/catia/products/dymola/](https://www.3ds.com/products-services/catia/products/dymola/) and set up your modeling tool to allow integration between Simulation (2021x and later) and Dymola , which can be specified as a language of Opaque expressions.

##### Integrating Simulation with Dymola

After successful integration, Dymola will be listed as available for language selection, e.g., through the language option in the **Simulation Console** pane. Simulation will then be able to process Modelica commands from the **Simulation Console** pane as shown in the image below.

To integrate Simulation with Dymola

1. On the modeling tool main menu, select Tools > Integrations . The Integrations dialog opens. [ATTACHMENT filename='Integrations Dialog Dymola.png']
2. Select Dymola (the Not Integrated status by default) and click Integrate/Remove Integration .
3. The Dymola directory selection dialog opens. Click Browse to select the Dymola home directory and click OK . [ATTACHMENT filename='Dymola Directory Selection Dialog.png']
4. The Dymola integration dialog will be shown. Click OK . The Dymola integration status will change to Integrated . [ATTACHMENT filename='Info Dialog.png']
5. Restart the modeling tool to complete the integration.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

- Dymola integration does not support Linux or Mac operating systems (only Windows).
- For Teamwork Cloud projects, the model file must be attached only to the project so that the working directory of Dymola will be automatically set to the temp folder of the modeling tool, e.g., C:\Users\ABC1\AppData\Local\.magicdraw \2021x\tmp\2020-11-23-13-32-34\attachedFiles .

##### Using Simulation with Dymola through the scripting language

After installing Dymola, you can use Simulation with Dymola through the scripting language to call a Modelica model via two built-in Dymola commands: *simulateExtendedModel* and *simulateMultiExtendedModel*.

To use Simulation with Dymola through the scripting language (Steps 4 and 5 are optional)

1. Drag a Modelica model file, e.g., BouncingBall.mo , into the modeling tool to attach a Modelica model to the project.
2. In the Console pane, select the language list box and choose Dymola . The input prompt will be changed to dymola>> .
3. Enter the command below and press **ENTER**: text The **Starting Math Engine** dialog appears shortly, and results will be shown in the **Console** pane.
4. Create an Opaque Behavior to call the model using the following tags: **
  - language : Dymola
  - name (optional): e.g., simulateExtendedModel(“BouncingBall”)
  - body : Use the command from Step 3.
5. Run the Opaque Behavior. The results will be displayed in the **Console** pane as shown below. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Results from the simulateExtendedModel of Dymola called from an Opaque Behavior.

##### Using Simulation with Dymola through an Activity diagram

Alternatively, you can use Simulation with Dymola through an Activity diagram with parameters to call a Modelica model through Dymola built-in commands.

To use simulation with Dymola through an Activity diagram

1. Create an Opaque Behavior to call the model with the following tags: [ATTACHMENT filename='Edit Body and Language.PNG']
  - language : Dymola
  - name (optional): e.g., simulateExtendedModel
  - body : (isSuccess,results)=simulateExtendedModel(problem,stopTime=stpTime, initialNames=iniNames,initialValues=iniValues,finalNames=fnlNames);
  - parameters : create parameters according to the command with valid Types and Multiplicities, e.g., in modelName : String; in stpTime : Real; in fnlNames : String [1..*]; in iniNames : String [1..*]; in iniValues : Real [1..*]; out isSuccess : Boolean; out results : Real [*] .
2. Create a Call Behavior Action in an Activity diagram of a Block and create valid in/out Parameters through in/out Pins according to the parameters of the Opaque Behavior. You can also create a Block, e.g., Modelica , with value properties to keep values and set an Activity as the Classifier Behavior of the Block as shown below. [ATTACHMENT filename='CallBehaviorAction in the Activity Diagram.PNG']
3. Create an Instance Specification to specify parameter values, e.g., CoupledClutches . Run the simulation through the instance to get results from Dymola back to Simulation as shown below. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

You can download and install Dymola from <a href="https://www.3ds.com/products-services/catia/products/dymola/">https://www.3ds.com/products-services/catia/products/dymola/</a> and set up your modeling tool to allow integration between Simulation (2021x and later) and Dymola , which can be specified as a language of Opaque expressions.</p><h3>Integrating Simulation with Dymola</h3><p>After successful integration, Dymola will be listed as available for language selection, e.g., through the language option in the <strong>Simulation Console</strong> pane. Simulation will then be able to process Modelica commands from the <strong>Simulation Console</strong> pane as shown in the image below.</p><p><br /></p><p>To integrate Simulation with Dymola</p><hr /><ol><li>On the modeling tool main menu, select <strong>Tools</strong> &gt; <strong>Integrations</strong>. The <strong>Integrations</strong> dialog opens.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Integrations Dialog Dymola.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Select <strong>Dymola</strong><span> (the </span><strong>Not Integrated</strong><span> status by default) and click </span><strong>Integrate/Remove Integration</strong><span>.</span></li><li>The <strong>Dymola directory selection</strong> dialog opens. Click <strong>Browse</strong> to select the Dymola home directory and click <strong>OK</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Dymola Directory Selection Dialog.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>The <strong>Dymola integration</strong><span> dialog will be shown. Click </span><strong>OK</strong><span>. The Dymola integration status will change to </span><strong>Integrated</strong><span>.<br /><br /></span><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Info Dialog.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Restart the modeling tool to complete the integration.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b5a31409-af2a-4295-b80e-a7dc3e4687b0"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Dymola integration does not support Linux or Mac operating systems (only Windows).</li><li>For Teamwork Cloud projects, the model file must be attached only to the project so that the working directory of Dymola will be automatically set to the temp folder of the modeling tool, e.g., <em>C:\Users\ABC1\AppData\Local\.magicdraw \2021x\tmp\2020-11-23-13-32-34\attachedFiles</em>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p><br /></p><h3>Using Simulation with Dymola through the scripting language</h3><p>After installing Dymola, you can use Simulation with Dymola through the scripting language to call a Modelica model via two built-in Dymola commands: <em>simulateExtendedModel</em> and <em>simulateMultiExtendedModel</em>.</p><p><br /></p><p>To use Simulation with Dymola through the scripting language (Steps 4 and 5 are optional)</p><hr /><ol><li>Drag a Modelica model file, e.g., <em>BouncingBall.mo</em>, into the modeling tool to attach a Modelica model to the project.</li><li>In the <strong>Console</strong> pane, select the language list box and choose <strong>Dymola</strong>. The input prompt will be changed to <em>dymola&gt;&gt;</em>.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Enter the command below and press <strong>ENTER</strong>:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="622bdf5e-c3f5-434f-ad20-c15f2788a5eb"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[simulateExtendedModel(“BouncingBall”, stopTime=3, initialNames={“e”, “h0”}, initialValues={.8, 1}, finalNames={“h”});]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">The <strong>Starting Math Engine</strong> dialog appears shortly, and results will be shown in the <strong>Console</strong> pane.</p></li><li>Create an Opaque Behavior to call the model using the following tags:<em><em><br /></em></em><ul><li><em>language</em>: Dymola</li><li><em>name </em>(optional): e.g., <em>simulateExtendedModel(“BouncingBall”)</em><em><br class="_mce_tagged_br" /></em></li><li><em>body</em>: Use the command from Step 3.</li></ul></li><li><p>Run the Opaque Behavior. The results will be displayed in the <strong>Console</strong> pane as shown below.<br /><br /></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Results from the simulateExtendedModel of Dymola Called from an Opaque Behavior.PNG" /></ac:image></p><h6>Results from the simulateExtendedModel of Dymola called from an Opaque Behavior.</h6></li></ol><h3>Using Simulation with Dymola through an Activity diagram</h3><p>Alternatively, you can use Simulation with Dymola through an Activity diagram with parameters to call a Modelica model through Dymola built-in commands.</p><p><br /></p><p>To use simulation with Dymola through an Activity diagram</p><hr /><ol><li>Create an Opaque Behavior to call the model with the following tags:<br /><ul><li><em>language</em>: Dymola</li><li><em>name</em> (optional): e.g., <em>simulateExtendedModel</em></li><li><em>body</em>: <em>(isSuccess,results)=simulateExtendedModel(problem,stopTime=stpTime, initialNames=iniNames,initialValues=iniValues,finalNames=fnlNames);</em></li><li><em>parameters</em>: create parameters according to the command with valid Types and Multiplicities, e.g., <em>in modelName : String; in stpTime : Real; in fnlNames : String [1..*]; in iniNames : String [1..*]; in iniValues : Real [1..*]; out isSuccess : Boolean; out results : Real [*]</em>.<br /><br /></li></ul><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Edit Body and Language.PNG" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Create a Call Behavior Action in an Activity diagram of a Block and create valid in/out Parameters through in/out Pins<em> </em>according to the parameters of the Opaque Behavior. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570772 space=MMA version=5 -->
## PAGE 00637: Integration with external Evaluators

- page_id: `249570772`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570772/Integration+with+external+Evaluators
- version_number: 5
- version_date: `2026-04-13T12:37:31.028+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator
- labels: ['external-evaluators']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to use an external Evaluator to evaluate an opaque expression in your model. Therefore, you can use any language supported by the external Evaluator in the body of the opaque expression.

- Integration with MATLAB ( R2016b or later )
- Integration with Mathematica ( 9 or later )
- Integration with Dymola ( 2021x or later )

For successful integration, you must use the 64-bit version of the external evaluator, e.g. MATLAB, to align it with the 64-bit version of modeling tools, e.g., MagicDraw or Cameo Systems Modeler.

You must specify the name of the language in the opaque expression. If you do not specify the language of the opaque expression, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will use the **Default Language** option specified in the **Simulation** **Framework** group in the **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Project options' space='']** dialog as follows.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Default Language property in the Project Options dialog.

If the language of expressions of constraints of a SysML Constraint Block is not specified, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will use the Evaluator, specified in the **Default Parametric Evaluator** option in the **Parametric Evaluator** group in the **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Project options' space='']** dialog, to solve the expressions as follows.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Default Parametric Evaluator property in the Project Options dialog.

**Related pages**

- Specifying the language for the expression
- Value binding
- Evaluating expressions
- Dynamic constraint
- Manual value updates using the Parametric Evaluator
- Communicating with evaluators through simulation console
- Built-in Math
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Trade study analysis' space='']
- Sample project

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570811 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00638: Integration with Mathematica

- page_id: `249570811`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570811/Integration+with+Mathematica
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:16.736+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator > Integration with external Evaluators
- labels: ['mathematica-integration']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports Mathematica®, a Mathematical computation engine, to analyze and solve Mathematica®l expressions. Once you have installed Mathematica® , you can specify it as the language of opaque expressions.

First, you must install Mathematica® on your local machine, and then set up your system to allow Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit to use the installed Mathematica®.

To use Mathematica® on a 64-bit version of Microsoft Windows and Linux

1. On the MagicDraw main menu, select Tools > Integrations . The Integrations dialog opens. [ATTACHMENT filename='Integrations Dialog (Windows).PNG']
2. From the list, select Mathematica and click Integrate/Remove Integration . The Mathematica directory selection dialog opens. [ATTACHMENT filename='Mathematica Dialog.PNG']
3. Browse for the home directory of Mathematica®.
4. Click OK and restart MagicDraw.

To use Mathematica® on Mac OSX

1. From the main menu, click Tools > Integrations . The Integrations dialog opens. [ATTACHMENT filename='Integrations Dialog (Mac).PNG']
2. From the list, select Mathematica and click Integrate/Remove Integration . The Mathematica directory selection dialog opens. [ATTACHMENT filename='Integrate Remove Integration Dialog (Mac).png']
3. Specify the directory where you have installed Mathematica® and click OK .
4. Restart the modeling tool.

#### INFO: Information

Information

(Only for integration with Mathematica **Version 12.3**or later) After completing the steps above using the **Integration** dialog, you must change **JAVA_HOME** to **JDK 17** by following the steps below:

1. Go to [https://adoptium.net/temurin/releases/](https://adoptium.net/temurin/releases/) and download JDK 17 (17.0.8 recommended) for your operating system.
2. Open the < install_root>/bin/<modeling_tool_name>.properties file (e.g., magicdraw.properties ) with a text editor.
3. Change **JAVA_HOME** to the **JDK 17** directory and save the file, for example: text
4. Restart the modeling tool.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports Mathematica®, a Mathematical computation engine, to analyze and solve Mathematica®l expressions. Once you have installed Mathematica® , you can specify it as the language of opaque expressions. </p><p>First, you must install Mathematica® on your local machine, and then set up your system to allow Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit to use the installed Mathematica®.</p><p><br /></p><p>To use Mathematica® on a 64-bit version of Microsoft Windows and Linux </p><hr /><ol><li>On the MagicDraw main menu, select <strong>Tools </strong>&gt;<strong> Integrations</strong>. The <strong>Integrations</strong> dialog opens.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Integrations Dialog (Windows).PNG" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>From the list, select <strong>Mathematica</strong> and click <strong>Integrate/Remove Integration</strong>. The <strong>Mathematica</strong> <strong>directory selection</strong> dialog opens.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Mathematica Dialog.PNG" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Browse for the home directory of Mathematica®.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong> and restart MagicDraw.<br /><br /></li></ol><p>To use Mathematica® on Mac OSX<span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);"> </span></p><hr /><ol><li>From the main menu, click <strong>Tools</strong> &gt; <strong>Integrations</strong>. The <strong>Integrations</strong> dialog opens.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Integrations Dialog (Mac).PNG" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>From the list, select <strong>Mathematica</strong> and click <strong>Integrate/Remove Integration</strong>. The <strong>Mathematica directory selection</strong> dialog opens.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:width="726"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Integrate Remove Integration Dialog (Mac).png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Specify the directory where you have installed Mathematica® and click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li>Restart the modeling tool.</li></ol><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b5d7d930-91eb-4265-aedc-3d65a0ec16f4"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Information</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>(Only for integration with Mathematica <strong>Version 12.3 </strong>or later) After completing the steps above using the <strong>Integration</strong> dialog, you must change <strong>JAVA_HOME</strong> to <strong>JDK 17</strong> by following the steps below:</p><ol><li>Go to <em><a href="https://adoptium.net/temurin/releases/">https://adoptium.net/temurin/releases/</a></em> and download JDK 17 (17.0.8 recommended) for your operating system.</li><li>Open the &lt;<em><span class="error">install_root&gt;</span>/bin/&lt;modeling_tool_name<span class="error">&gt;</span>.properties</em> file (e.g., <em>magicdraw.properties</em>) with a text editor.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Change <strong>JAVA_HOME</strong> to the <strong>JDK 17</strong> directory and save the file, for example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f706e332-153e-42d7-a1c3-b25b207b893b"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[JAVA_HOME=C\:\\Program Files\\Eclipse Adoptium\\jdk-17.0.8]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Restart the modeling tool.</p></li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570779 space=MMA version=7 -->
## PAGE 00639: Integration with MATLAB

- page_id: `249570779`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570779/Integration+with+MATLAB
- version_number: 7
- version_date: `2026-01-23T17:59:42.536+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator > Integration with external Evaluators
- labels: ['matlab-integration']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can use MATLAB Version **R2016b**or later to evaluate expressions written in MATLAB syntax in the Simulation Toolkit. You must install MATLAB and set up your modeling tools to call and use it.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

- For successful integration, you must use the 64-bit version of MATLAB to align it with the 64-bit version of modeling tools, e.g., MagicDraw or Cameo Systems Modeler.
- To enable the Integrations menu, you must change the perspective to Full Featured , System Engineer , or Software Architect (from the main menu, select Options > Perspectives > Perspectives , select Full Featured , System Engineer , or Software Architect , and click Apply ).

To integrate a modeling tool with MATLAB

1. From the main menu, click Tools > Integrations . The Integrations dialog opens. [ATTACHMENT filename='integrations_dialog.png']
2. Select MATLAB and click Integrate/Remove Integration . The MATLAB directory selection dialog opens.
3. Specify the MATLAB home directory , e.g., C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2024b . [ATTACHMENT filename='directory_selection_dialog.png'] The JRE home directory is selected automatically.
4. Click **OK** and restart your system or the modeling tool.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Integration with external Evaluators' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Connecting to a running MATLAB session' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Simulink co-simulation' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570558 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00641: Interaction simulation

- page_id: `249570558`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570558/Interaction+simulation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:13.254+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide
- labels: ['interaction-simulation']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit comes with an interaction engine that can simulate an interaction element based on the UML semantics. An interaction is a unit of Behavior that focuses on information exchange among internal properties of a Class. You can use it to describe the main Behavior of the Class by specifying the interaction as a Classifier Behavior of the Class. You can also assign it to be a method of an operation of the Class. If an interaction is a Classifier Behavior, it will be simulated whenever the object is started. If it is a method of an operation, it will be simulated when the operation is called.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit comes with an interaction engine that can simulate an interaction element based on the UML semantics. An interaction is a unit of Behavior that focuses on information exchange among internal properties of a Class. You can use it to describe the main Behavior of the Class by specifying the interaction as a Classifier Behavior of the Class. You can also assign it to be a method of an operation of the Class. If an interaction is a Classifier Behavior, it will be simulated whenever the object is started. If it is a method of an operation, it will be simulated when the operation is called.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249569695 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00642: Internal simulation clock

- page_id: `249569695`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249569695/Internal+simulation+clock
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:06.390+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > Simulation time and simulation clock
- labels: ['internal-simulation-clock']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

1349363306

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

1349363308

1349363305

**On this page**

5

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

1349363307

The internal simulation clock in Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to use a new simulation clock that can increase its simulation time based on time Events and duration in a model. The following properties in the **Timing** group in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SimulationConfig stereotype' space=''] Specification window enable you to specify necessary options in the internal simulation clock

- Start Time An integer value applies for starting the simulation clock.
- Step Size A decimal value applies for increasing the simulation time. This value, as 1.0 by default unless specified, is used together with Start Time .
- Step Delay A decimal value applies for delaying each time step in the simulation clock.
- End Time An integer value applies for stopping the simulation clock.
- Number Of Steps An integer value together with Start Time and End Time , to calculate Step Size (if not specified) for the simulation clock.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

Toenable the internal simulation clock, **startTime** must be specified as the Trigger, along with **stepSize**, which is 1.0 by default unless specified. The [FlashingLight.mdzip](https://docs.nomagic.com/download/attachments/20851796/FlashingLight.mdzip?version=1&modificationDate=1505276946588&api=v2), [ATTACHMENT filename='StopWatch.mdzip'], [ATTACHMENT filename='CruiseControl.mdzip'], and [ATTACHMENT filename='CoffeeMachine.mdzip'] built-in sample projects apply the internal simulation clock.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']FlashLight SimulationConfig.

##### Simulation time in the console log

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit's clock display in the console log has undergone a change. From now on, the simulation console will display the simulation time instead of the current date and time. The simulation clock appears as "00:05:30,000" which is in the format of "HH:MM:SS,Milisecond". The simulation time will be used for all simulations. The current date and time will no longer be displayed in the simulation console log.

From the following State Machine diagram, **s1** (State 1) takes two seconds to go to **s2** (State 2), and **s2** takes three seconds to go to **s1** again. You can see the simulation time in the following **Console** log.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The simulation Console log showing the simulation time.

1349381048

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model-based clock' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

This value, as 1.0 by default unless specified, is used together with <strong>Start Time</strong>.</li><li>Step Delay <br />A decimal value applies for delaying each time step in the simulation clock.</li><li>End Time<br />An integer value applies for stopping the simulation clock.</li><li>Number Of Steps<br />An integer value together with <strong>Start Time</strong> and <strong>End Time</strong>, to calculate <strong>Step Size</strong> (if not specified) for the simulation clock.</li></ul><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="060d37b1-cefc-418d-82a4-98b7c053e2e1"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span class="confluence-link"><span>To </span><span class="confluence-link">enable the internal simulation clock, <strong>startTime</strong> must be specified as the Trigger, along with <strong>stepSize</strong>, which is 1.0 by default unless specified.</span> The <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/download/attachments/20851796/FlashingLight.mdzip?version=1&amp;modificationDate=1505276946588&amp;api=v2">FlashingLight.mdzip</a>, <span><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="StopWatch.mdzip" /></ac:link></span>, <span><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="CruiseControl.mdzip" /></ac:link></span>, and <span class="confluence-link"><span><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="CoffeeMachine.mdzip" /></ac:link></span></span> <span class="confluence-link">built-in sample projects apply the internal simulation clock.</span><br /></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="6742-6.PNG" /></ac:image>FlashLight SimulationConfig.<br /></span></h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Simulation time in the console log</h3><p>Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit's clock display in the console log has undergone a change. From now on, the simulation console will display the simulation time instead of the current date and time. The simulation clock appears as &quot;00:05:30,000&quot; which is in the format of &quot;HH:MM:SS,Milisecond&quot;. The simulation time will be used for all simulations. The current date and time will no longer be displayed in the simulation console log. </p><p>From the following State Machine diagram, <strong>s1</strong> (State 1) takes two seconds to go to <strong>s2</strong> (State 2), and <strong>s2</strong> takes three seconds to go to <strong>s1</strong> again. You can see the simulation time in the following <strong>Console</strong> log.<br /> </p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="simulation-time-a.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The simulation Console log showing the simulation time.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="df039a90-45c7-4ef6-bec9-500299bc7d3f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1349381048</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Model-based clock" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570352 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00643: Internal Transition

- page_id: `249570352`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570352/Internal+Transition
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-03-10T15:55:27.604+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > State Machine simulation > Adapting models for State Machine simulation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An Internal Transition allows a State Machine to change its behavior in response to an event without changing its overall state. This type of transition is defined within a State and is triggered by an event. Its effect is to modify the State's behavior or attributes without changing the State itself. Internal Transitions are semantically equivalent to self transitions except that the State is neither exited nor re-entered; thus, the corresponding exit and entry actions are not performed.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Frothing Milk State has Internal Transitions.

To create an Internal Transition and set an effect

1. Drag an element (OpaqueBehavior, Activity, Interaction, StateMachine, ProtocolStateMachine, FunctionBehavior) from the Containment Tree onto the State symbol and hold it down until the Tooltip appears (long drag) and drop it.
2. Select **Create Internal Transition and set as effect**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
The new Internal Transition is created for a specific State, and the dragged element is specified as the effect of the newly created Internal Transition.

To create an Internal Transition and set a trigger

1. Drag an element (Signal, Operation) from the Containment Tree onto the State symbol and hold it down until the Tooltip appears (long drag) and drop it.
2. Select **Create Internal Transition and set as trigger**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
The new Internal Transition is created for a specific State, and the dragged element is specified as the trigger of the newly created Internal Transition.

Internal transition with a relative time event is not fully supported. Currently, the transition is fired only once.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>An Internal Transition allows a State Machine to change its behavior in response to an event without changing its overall state. This type of transition is defined within a State and is triggered by an event. Its effect is to modify the State's behavior or attributes without changing the State itself. Internal Transitions are semantically equivalent to self transitions except that the State is neither exited nor re-entered; thus, the corresponding exit and entry actions are not performed.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Internal Transition.jpg" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Frothing Milk State has Internal Transitions.</h6><p>To create an Internal Transition and set an effect</p><hr /><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Drag an element (OpaqueBehavior, Activity, Interaction, StateMachine, ProtocolStateMachine, FunctionBehavior) from the Containment Tree onto the State symbol and hold it down until the Tooltip appears (long drag) and drop it.</p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Select <strong>Create Internal Transition and set as effect</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Set as effect.jpg" /></ac:image><br /><br />The new Internal Transition is created for a specific State, and the dragged element is specified as the effect of the newly created Internal Transition.</p></li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To create an Internal Transition and set a trigger</p><hr /><ol><li class="auto-cursor-target"><p class="auto-cursor-target">Drag an element (Signal, Operation) from the Containment Tree onto the State symbol and hold it down until the Tooltip appears (long drag) and drop it.</p></li><li class="auto-cursor-target"><p class="auto-cursor-target">Select <strong>Create Internal Transition and set as trigger</strong>. <br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Set as trigger.jpg" /></ac:image><br /><br />The new Internal Transition is created for a specific State, and the dragged element is specified as the trigger of the newly created Internal Transition.</p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0fa0012e-cef9-48c7-b53e-dee994a0099e"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Internal transition with a relative time event is not fully supported. Currently, the transition is fired only once.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````
