<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249569548 space=MMA version=3 -->
## PAGE 00645: Key features

- page_id: `249569548`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249569548/Key+features
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-09-12T15:11:52.260+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Getting started
- labels: ['key-features', 'main-features', 'features']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit is capable of running your UML or SysML models. The key features of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit are as follows

- Simulation Framework The general infrastructure (including the simulation toolbars, context menu, and panes) and Open API for simulation.
- Activity Engine (fUML Engine) The OMG fUML (a foundational subset of the Executable UML) standard.
- State-Machine Engine (SCXML Engine) The W3C SCXML (State Charts XML) standard, which is an open-source Apache implementation.
- Interaction Engine Enabling Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit to simulate interactions that are modeled with Sequence diagrams.
- Parametric Engine Enabling Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit to simulate SysML Parametric diagrams.

The simulation sample projects are available in the *<**install_root**>/samples/simulation* directory.

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity simulation engine' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State Machine' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interaction' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Parametric Diagram' space='MT']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit is capable of running your UML or SysML models. The key features of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit are as follows</p><ul><li><strong>Simulation</strong> <strong>Framework</strong> <br />The general infrastructure (including the simulation toolbars, context menu, and panes) and Open API for simulation.</li><li><strong>Activity Engine</strong> <strong>(fUML Engine)</strong> <br />The OMG fUML (a foundational subset of the Executable UML) standard.</li><li><strong>State-Machine Engine</strong> <strong>(SCXML Engine)</strong> <br />The W3C SCXML (State Charts XML) standard, which is an open-source Apache implementation.</li><li><strong>Interaction</strong> <strong>Engine</strong> <br />Enabling Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit to simulate interactions that are modeled with Sequence diagrams.</li><li><strong>Parametric Engine</strong> <br />Enabling Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit to simulate SysML Parametric diagrams. </li></ul><p>The simulation sample projects are available in the <em>&lt;<em><em style="text-align: left;">install_root</em></em>&gt;/samples/simulation</em> directory. </p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Activity simulation engine" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="State Machine" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Interaction" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="SysML Parametric Diagram" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570560 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00646: Lifeline

- page_id: `249570560`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570560/Lifeline
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:13.368+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Interaction simulation > Supported elements in interaction simulation
- labels: ['lifeline']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Information

1010481082

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Information

1010481084

1010481081

**On this page**

**33**

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Information

1010481083

You can use a lifeline in a Sequence diagram to represent a property owned by a Class or a block that is the context of the diagram. Sending messages between objects specifying the properties presented by the lifelines can occur. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit uses lifelines to find a source and a target object of the message.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Simulation console showing how lifelines can be used to find the source and target of a message.

You can use a lifeline in a SysML project to represent a nested property of a Class or a block, which is the context of a diagram. A path to the nested property appears as a dot notation on the lifeline symbol. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit uses the path on the lifeline symbol to find the object.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit uses a lifeline with a dot notation to represent a nested property when you record a simulation as a Sequence diagram. See [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Recording simulation as a Sequence diagram' space=''] for more information about recording a simulation.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Simulation console showing how a lifeline with a dot notation can represent a nested property.

Because Messages recorded in the system are not executed that they are internal mechanisms, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit only executes Messages that are external to the system, e.g., a Message going from *User* Lifeline (without types) and *Found* Messages. All other Messages are not executed but verified and printed in the **Console** pane.

#### INFO: Information

Information

A *Found* Message is a Message where the occurrence of a receiving Event is known, but the occurrence of a sending Event is not.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Executing only external outgoing Messages, e.g., User Lifeline and Found Messages.

##### Storing nested property paths in a model

From version 2021x Refresh2, the Lifelines used to represent nested properties have the «NestedPropertyPath» stereotype applied to them. As a result, the path of a nested property is stored in the **propertyPath** tag of a Lifeline. Nested property paths of the Lifelines created with an earlier version of a modeling tool are stored in diagram symbols and you should save them in the model manually as described below.

To store nested property paths of Lifelines in a model

1. Right-click a Sequence Diagram in the Containment tree or an empty space in a Sequence Diagram.
2. In the context menu, select Refactor > Store Nested Property Paths in the Model . [ATTACHMENT filename='storing_nested_property_paths_in_model.png']

The nested property path of the selected Lifeline or the nested property paths of all Lifelines in the selected diagram are now stored in the model.

##### Lifeline selector

If a lifeline represents a part with the multiplicity of more than 1 (neither * nor 1..*), the list will also contain more than one object (not only one object) at runtime. The lifeline has the Selector expression that specifies the particular part represented by the lifeline.

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports the lifeline selector which allows specifying an exact object from the Selector expression. The Selector expression will be evaluated as an index integer to get an Object from the object collection.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The ledger[3] object is selected according to the index integer of lifeline Selector 3 of the Ledger object.

If the selector of a receiver is invalid or cannot be evaluated as an index integer, e.g., "X", none of the objects will be used, and a warning message will display in the simulation console.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="25499c7f-9249-4ba6-9ce1-5c36da27b323"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1010481082</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0850e536-6317-448a-b379-246e79a80c97"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1010481084</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8985538e-15ce-45b5-8d8c-fd3f0f7e9012"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1010481081</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="22833a8c-ad76-4544-ac98-21fc22209f26"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">3</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="minLevel">3</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="06a45f05-b7d0-4d89-ac03-0020e5cc5038"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1010481083</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can use a lifeline in a Sequence diagram to represent a property owned by a Class or a block that is the context of the diagram. Sending messages between objects specifying the properties presented by the lifelines can occur. See <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Recording simulation as a Sequence diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Recording a Simulation as a Sequence Diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for more information about recording a simulation.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h6 style="text-align: center;"><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Interaction Simulation with Lifelines Representing Nested Parts of a Diagram Context" ac:alt="Interaction Simulation with Lifelines Representing Nested Parts of a Diagram Context"><ri:attachment ri:filename="lifelines_repNestedparts.png" /></ac:image><br />Simulation console showing how a lifeline with a dot notation can represent a nested property.</h6><p>Because Messages recorded in the system are not executed that they are internal mechanisms, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit only executes Messages that are external to the system, e.g., a Message going from <em>User</em> Lifeline (without types) and <em>Found</em> Messages. All other Messages are not executed but verified and printed in the <strong>Console</strong> pane.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c0ab35ef-8fbb-44ac-87a9-e0d24124410a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Information</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>A <em>Found</em> Message is a Message where the occurrence of a receiving Event is known, but the occurrence of a sending Event is not.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sequence Diagram with Found Messages.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Executing only external outgoing Messages, e.g., User Lifeline and Found Messages.</h6><h3>Storing nested property paths in a model</h3><p>From version 2021x Refresh2, the Lifelines used to represent nested properties have the «NestedPropertyPath» stereotype applied to them. As a result, the path of a nested property is stored in the <strong>propertyPath</strong> tag of a Lifeline. Nested property paths of the Lifelines created with an earlier version of a modeling tool are stored in diagram symbols and you should save them in the model manually as described below.</p><p><br /></p><p>To store nested property paths of Lifelines in a model</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click a Sequence Diagram in the Containment tree or an empty space in a Sequence Diagram.</li><li>In the context menu, select <strong>Refactor</strong> &gt; <strong>Store Nested Property Paths in the Model</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="storing_nested_property_paths_in_model.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li></ol><p>The nested property path of the selected Lifeline or the nested property paths of all Lifelines in the selected diagram are now stored in the model.<br /><br /></p><h3>Lifeline selector</h3><p class="Textbody">If a lifeline represents a part with the multiplicity of more than 1 (neither * nor 1..*), the list will also contain more than one object (not only one object) at runtime. The lifeline has the Selector expression that specifies the particular part represented by the lifeline.</p><p>Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit supports the lifeline selector which allows specifying an exact object from the Selector expression. The Selector expression will be evaluated as an index integer to get an Object from the object collection.</p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Lifeline.PNG" /></ac:image>The ledger[3] object is selected according to the index integer of lifeline Selector 3 of the Ledger object.</h6><p>If the selector of a receiver is invalid or cannot be evaluated as an index integer, e.g., &quot;X&quot;, none of the objects will be used, and a warning message will display in the simulation console.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570717 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00647: Logical expression

- page_id: `249570717`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570717/Logical+expression
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:15.599+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator > Evaluating expressions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

402984464

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

402984466

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

402984465

A logical expression is an expression that uses comparison operators. You can use a logical expression to define a constraint. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit uses the expression to validate values at runtime.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

A logical expression must contain a comparison operator (==, <, >, <=, >=, and <>) as shown in the following examples:

- val1 >= "500" is a valid expression.
- val2.equals("500") == true is a valid expression.
- val2.equals("500") is an invalid expression.

#### Red background for the labels and text fields in UI

If some constraints failed during evaluation, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit (CST) will highlight the values in the **Variables** pane with a red background to denote that the values fail the constraint. All parent nodes (Name column) that contain the failed constraints will have a red background (until the root one in the tree view), which indicates failures deeper in the structure tree. You can also see these values in the **Simulation Console** pane.

Furthermore, both the values and validation that are highlighted must always be consistent with the labels and text boxes of both the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UI modeling diagram simulation' space=''] and the HTML UI during simulation.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Red backgrounds indicating failures during evaluation.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

Neither green background nor tooltip will be used for "passed" verification status for both Swing mockups and HTML UI.

A tooltip will also appear if you hover the mouse over the failed constraint. Each parent of the failed constraint will appear with a contextual path "dot notation" starting from the node on which tooltip is shown.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The parent node with a red background indicates failures deeper in the structure tree.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Nodes with green background indicating valid values.

This example uses the [ATTACHMENT filename='equationSysml.png']block **Circle** previously shown in the section [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Mathematical equation' space='']. If we modify the expression of the constraint block **Circle** **Area** by changing the assignment operator "**=**" to the equality operator "**==**", the parametric evaluator will use the expression to validate the values of both area and radius. If the result is false, the values will be highlighted in red (see Figure: The parent node with a red background indicates failures deeper in the structure tree). But, if the result is true, the values will be highlighted in green (see Figure: Nodes with green background indicating valid values).

#### Finding a constraint object that binds the value property

You can find the constraint object to which the value of the property is bound in the **Variables** pane.

To find a constraint object to which a value property is bound

- Right-click a constraint object row in the **Variables** pane, and select **Go** **To**. The constraint to which the value of the property is bound will appear, as shown in the figure below. 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] Finding a constraint object that binds the value property through the shortcut menu.

When you export the object to the instance specification, if the exported object contains a constraint object (an object of a constraint block) and if the expression of the constraint block is a boolean expression, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will set the result of the evaluation, which is a literal boolean, to a slot defined by the constraint property typed by the constraint block.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Exporting an instance as a slot value of the constraint property.

402984463

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Evaluating expressions' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Mathematical equation' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1b499149-7d9e-4c32-a902-b91152c31ca9"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402984464</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a523c23c-1cd5-4ddf-8b2b-fc889766c906"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402984466</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="491e0a58-cf88-478e-81bb-67182c8b8264"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402984465</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>A logical expression is an expression that uses comparison operators. You can use a logical expression to define a constraint. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit uses the expression to validate values at runtime.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ee9f0260-2353-4e52-b390-032a8411a006"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>A logical expression must contain a comparison operator (==, &lt;, &gt;, &lt;=, &gt;=, and &lt;&gt;) as shown in the following examples:</p><ul><li><em>val1 &gt;= &quot;500&quot;</em> is a valid expression.<em><br /></em></li><li><em>val2.equals(&quot;500&quot;) == true</em> is a valid expression.</li><li><em>val2.equals(&quot;500&quot;)</em> is an invalid expression.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h2>Red background for the labels and text fields in UI</h2><p>If some constraints failed during evaluation, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit (CST) will highlight the values in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane with a red background to denote that the values fail the constraint. All parent nodes (Name column) that contain the failed constraints will have a red background (until the root one in the tree view), which indicates failures deeper in the structure tree. You can also see these values in the <strong>Simulation Console</strong> pane. </p><p class="Standard">Furthermore, both the values and validation that are highlighted must always be consistent with the labels and text boxes of both the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="UI modeling diagram simulation" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[UI modeling diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and the HTML UI during simulation.</p><p class="Standard"><br /></p><p class="Standard"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="redbackground.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 class="Standard" style="text-align: center;">Red backgrounds indicating failures during evaluation.</h6><p class="Standard"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="460efdba-3bcb-43ba-9399-06b4ef36d21b"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Neither green background nor tooltip will be used for &quot;passed&quot; verification status for both Swing mockups and HTML UI.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="Standard"><br /></p><p class="Standard">A tooltip will also appear if you hover the mouse over the failed constraint. Each parent of the failed constraint will appear with a contextual path &quot;dot notation&quot; starting from the node on which tooltip is shown. </p><p class="Standard"><br /></p><p class="Standard"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="logicalExpression2.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 class="Standard" style="text-align: center;">The parent node with a red background indicates failures deeper in the structure tree.</h6><p class="Standard"><br /></p><p class="Standard"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="logicalExpression3.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 class="Standard" style="text-align: center;">Nodes with green background indicating valid values.</h6><p>This example uses the <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="equationSysml.png" /><ac:link-body>block <strong>Circle</strong></ac:link-body></ac:link> previously shown in the section <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Mathematical equation" /></ac:link>. If we modify the expression of the constraint block <strong>Circle</strong> <strong>Area</strong> by changing the assignment operator &quot;<strong>=</strong>&quot; to the equality operator &quot;<strong>==</strong>&quot;, the parametric evaluator will use the expression to validate the values of both area and radius. If the result is false, the values will be highlighted in red (see Figure: The parent node with a red background indicates failures deeper in the structure tree). But, if the result is true, the values will be highlighted in green (<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="cc81ea82-e052-46f3-9aaf-f96d747383e1">see Figure: Nodes with green background indicating valid values</ac:inline-comment-marker>). </p><h2>Finding a constraint object that binds the value property</h2><p>You can find the constraint object to which the value of the property is bound in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><p>To find a constraint object to which a value property is bound</p><hr /><ul><li><p>Right-click a constraint object row in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane, and select <strong>Go</strong> <strong>To</strong>. The constraint to which the value of the property is bound will appear, as shown in the figure below.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="logicalExpression4.png" /></ac:image></p><h6>Finding a constraint object that binds the value property through the shortcut menu. </h6></li></ul><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" />When you export the object to the instance specification, if the exported object contains a constraint object (an object of a constraint block) and if the expression of the constraint block is a boolean expression, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will set the result of the evaluation, which is a literal boolean, to a slot defined by the constraint property typed by the constraint block. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="logicalExpression5.png" /></ac:image><br class="atl-forced-newline" />Exporting an instance as a slot value of the constraint property.</h6><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b8570066-61e1-4e77-8ffc-27dedf29d275"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402984463</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Evaluating expressions" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Mathematical equation" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249569468 space=MMA version=6 -->
## PAGE 00648: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation

- page_id: `249569468`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249569468/Magic+Model+Analyst+Cameo+Simulation+Toolkit+Documentation
- version_number: 6
- version_date: `2026-05-13T09:14:18.974+02:00`
- ancestors: 
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit provides the first in the industry extendable model execution framework based on OMG fUML and W3C SCXML standards. It extends UPDM plugin to validate system behavior by executing, animating, and debugging SysML Parametric models in the context of realistic mock-ups of the intended user interface.

##### Why Simulation?

The purpose of a simulation is to gain system understanding without manipulating the real system, either because it is not yet defined or available, or because it cannot be exercised directly due to cost, time, resources or risk constraints. Simulation is typically performed on a model of the system. 
 
With the plugin, users can test how the system reacts to user interaction or predefined testing data and execution scenarios.

##### Business Benefits

Users will find that one of the key business benefits of the Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit is cost efficiency. Employing Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will assist enterprises in significantly reducing project costs, allowing users to identify design errors before the actual production of components.

To learn how to install and use Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit, see:

**Documentation of earlier versions**

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249571248 space=MMA version=5 -->
## PAGE 00649: Managing active simulations

- page_id: `249571248`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249571248/Managing+active+simulations
- version_number: 5
- version_date: `2025-12-03T10:09:37.890+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Server-side simulation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can view and manage all active server-side simulations in the Task Manager. The Task Manager not only provides information about all currently running and queued simulations but also allows you to terminate them and open the simulation UI window.

To view and manage all active simulations, you need to have the Manage Simulations permission. Without the Manage Simulations permission, you can only view and manage the simulations you have started yourself.

To view and manage active simulations

1. Open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Resources application' space='MCS'] or a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Opening Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud' space='MCS'] .
2. In the top right corner of the screen, click [ATTACHMENT filename='menu.png'] and select Task manager .
3. In the Task manager dialog, select the Simulation tab.
4. Depending on what you need, complete one or several of the following actions:
  - In the **Task manager** dialog, view all active simulations and their information, like the simulated projects, users who started the simulations, their run time, and the simulation status. The **Run at** column shows the simulation run time in the local time zone of your computer.
  - Click a simulation line to see the simulation ID and the Simulation Configuration name.
  - Click [ATTACHMENT filename='open_ui.png'] next to a simulation to open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Simulation with UI' space=''] if the simulated model has a UI mockup, Time Series chart, or Timeline chart.
  - Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] next to a simulation to terminate it.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The **Task manager** dialog allows you to view and manage active simulations.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249571069 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00650: Manipulating global variables

- page_id: `249571069`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249571069/Manipulating+global+variables
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-04-02T10:34:44.884+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Action languages > Action scripts APIs > Manipulating runtime objects using scripts
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

4

You can use global variables in ALH to share data across scripts during simulation runtime. Unlike standard variables, they are not limited to a single script and retain their values. You can check if a global variable exists, get its value, add new data, or remove it when no longer needed.

##### Checking an existing global variable

You can use script tocheck if a global variable is defined in an environment by using **isGlobalVariable("variableName")** argument.

If the global variable is specified by the given variable name in the scripting environment, then the argument returns true. Otherwise, it returns false.

```text

```

The following code fragment shows how to check if a global variable, e.g., GLOBAL_COUNT, is defined through ALH API.

```text

```

##### Getting a value from a global variable

During the execution of a model, you can obtain a value of a global variable which is already defined by calling **getGlobalVariable("variableName")** of the ALH API. This argument returns a Java object, which is the value of the global variable specified by the given variable name.

```text

```

The example code is as follows:

```text

```

#### NOTE: Note

Note

You can obtain the value of a global variable directly by using its name. 
a = "GLOBAL_COUNT" + 1;

##### Adding a value to a global variable

A global variable is a variable that is accessible from any session in the same execution. You can use ALH API to create a global variable and specify its value by calling**ALH.setGlobalVariable("variableName", value)**.

```text

```

The code example is as follows

```text

```

The example code assigns 1 to a global variable named GLOBAL_COUNT. If the global variable with the given name does not exist, this method will create a new global variable with the same name and add the value to it.

##### Removing a defined global variable

You can remove a global variable that is previously defined by calling a **removeGlobalVariable("variableName")** of the ALH API.

```text

```

The following code fragment shows how to remove a global variable, e.g., *GLOBAL_COUNT*, that was previously defined through ALH API.

```text

```

If the global variable with the given name does not exist, this method will create a new global variable with the same name and add the value to it.</p><h3>Removing a defined global variable</h3><p>You can remove a global variable that is previously defined by calling a <strong>removeGlobalVariable(&quot;variableName&quot;)</strong> of the ALH API.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0513a0d7-1d02-43ea-b6ba-a7a5f1711c7f"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.removeGlobalVariable("variableName")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The following code fragment shows how to remove a global variable, e.g., <em>GLOBAL_COUNT</em>, that was previously defined through ALH API.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f6ea4d87-c715-4129-ab7d-e1fca10254ba"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.removeGlobalVariable("GLOBAL_COUNT");]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249571033 space=MMA version=3 -->
## PAGE 00651: Manipulating runtime objects using scripts

- page_id: `249571033`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249571033/Manipulating+runtime+objects+using+scripts
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-09-15T18:00:48.348+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Action languages > Action scripts APIs
- labels: ['alh-api']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Manipulating run-time objects with scripts lets you dynamically control simulation environment, structural elements, and behavioral workflows. You can modify structural and global variables, invoke behaviors and operations. You can also create and send signals, and retrieve key runtime data like token values, simulation time, and callers of the scripts. Additionally, you can evaluate expressions and check behaviors to gain information about the simulation.

Action scripts can be used where the scripts are evaluated during simulation, for example in the Opaque Behaviors, Actions, Guards, or directly in the simulation **Console** pane). You can use the following types of action scripts to manipulate runtime objects:

- fUML object syntax, which simplifies some ALH actions. Example: object.get("property")
- ALH (Action Language Helper) scripts. Example: ALH.getValue(object, "property")
- Alf - Action Language for fUML (requires an additional plugin). Example: object.property

It is easy to access elements that are deeper in the hierarchy tree, but accessing parent elements is more complicated. Therefore, it is important to plan where executing a particular script would be the most efficient.

##### Specifying context

Scripts are context sensitive, whenever you execute a script, you must specify its context (object). Mostly, it is the current simulation context (marked as "self" in a script). However, it can also be an input pin (pin name) or an object created in a script itself (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a runtime object' space='']). When evaluating from Simulation console, the context depends on the selected element in the variables pane. For example:

To get the*Diameter*Value Property when the script is executed in the*Car*Block, the script should be**self.get("tyre.wheel.Diameter")**.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Getting the *Diameter* Value Property when the simulation context is the *Car* Block.

To get the*Diameter*Value Property when the script is executed after selecting the wheel, the script should be **D****iameter.**

The first level properties can be directly accessed using their name.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Getting the *Diameter* Value Property when the script is executed in the Console pane.

To learn more, refer to the following topics:

**Additional resources**

- You can find more examples of API usage at <install_root> \samples\simulation\SmallTestSamples.mdzip (Tests section).
- For more information, see the JavaDoc at <install_root> \openapidocs\SimulationJavaDoc .

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570122 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00652: Manipulating simulation information

- page_id: `249570122`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570122/Manipulating+simulation+information
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:50:57.732+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Animation > Simulation information in diagrams
- labels: ['changing-runtime-values', 'sending-singals']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Prerequisites

1903536946

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Prerequisites

1903536952

1903536944

**On this page**

4

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Prerequisites

1903536951

When simulating a model, you can manipulate simulation information not only in the **Simulation** window but in diagrams as well. For example, you can change runtime values or send a trigger directly on Part shapes and Port lablels.

#### INFO: Types of diagrams

Types of diagrams

You can manipulate simulation information only in diagrams that are based on a Composite Structure Diagram.

##### Changing runtime values

You can change the runtime values of Part properties and Port labels directly in diagrams the same way you change them in the **Variables** pane of the **Simulation** window.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

To display and change runtime values in diagrams, the **Show Runtime Values on Part Shapes**and/or ****Show Runtime Values on Port Labels****animation options must be enabled. For more information, see[Disabling and enabling animation options](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/Displaying+simulation+information#Displayingsimulationinformation-Disablingandenablinganimationoptions).

To change runtime values on Part shapes or Port labels

1. During model simulation, select a Part shape or Port labels and click a runtime value you want to change.
2. Enter a new runtime value and press Enter. The figure below shows how to change different types of values. 33%[IMAGE alt='' src='']Changing a numeric runtime value.33%[IMAGE alt='' src='']Changing an enumeration runtime value.33%[IMAGE alt='' src='']Changing a boolean runtime value.

When you change runtime values of Value or PortProperties as described above, the simulation data is updated in real-time accordingly.

##### Sending a signal

When simulating a model, you cansend a signaldirectly from a Part shape in a diagram the same way you would send it in the **Sessions** pane of the **Simulation** window. If it is possible to send a signal, a special button is displayed on a Part shape after selecting it.

To send a signal from a Part shape

1. During model simulation, select the shape of the Part from which you want to send a signal .
2. Click the signal icon next to the part shape as displayed below. If you can send more than one signal, the icons of all available signals are displayed. [ATTACHMENT filename='sending_signal.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249571047 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00653: Manipulating structural values

- page_id: `249571047`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249571047/Manipulating+structural+values
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:51:01.709+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Action languages > Action scripts APIs > Manipulating runtime objects using scripts
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

4

You can retrieve, modify, add, or remove structural feature values using the Action Language Helper (ALH) API and action scripts in the modeling tool. ALH allows you to dynamically control object properties, and update data during a simulation. You can also retrieve tag values, which store additional metadata.

##### Getting a structural feature value

You can use script to get a structural value.You can use the fUML object syntax, with the **object.get("****featureName")***.* featureName can use dot notation for nested features. For example:

- value = self.get("car.speed");

Using Groovy scripting language, you can get nested structural features using just dot notation. For example, value=car.speed

Alternatively, structural value can also be acquired using ALH API.

```text

```

The following example shows how to get the value of the structural feature indicated above in ALH API.

```text

```

#### INFO: Information

Information

While using Rhino JavaScript, the return value of ALH.getValue() is not automatically UnBoxed to a primitive type. Users need to handle such UnBoxing by themselves, by using ALH.getValue(object, "speed").intValue() to get the value of primitive integer type.

##### Specifying a structural feature value

You can use script to specify a structural value.You can use the fUML object syntax, with the **object.set("featureName", value)***.* featureName can use dot notation for nested features. For example:

- value = self .set("speed", 10);

Using Groovy scripting language, you can set nested structural features using just dot notation. For example, car.speed=100

Alternatively, structural value can also be specified using ALH API.

```text

```

The following example shows how to specify the value of the structural feature in ALH API:

```text

```

In addition, the RuntimeObject can be omitted. The following example shows how to set a structural feature value without specifying a RuntimeObject in ALH API:

```text

```

##### Adding a value to an object

You can use script to add a value to an object. You can use the fUML object syntax, with**object.addValue("featureName", value)**and**object.addValueAt("featureName", value, insertAt)***.*For example:

- self.addValue("p1",10); // System.p1 = [10]
- self.addValue("p1",30); // System.p1 = [10, 30]
- self.addValueAt("p1",20,2); // System.p1 = [10, 20, 30]

Alternatively, structural value can also be added using ALH API.

```text

```

If you have more than one value in an object and if you want to add a value at a specific position, you can use insertAt argument.

```text

```

The following code fragment shows how to add values to an object, e.g.,*System.p1*, through ALH API.

```text

```

##### Removing a value of an object

You can use script to remove a value from an object. You can use the fUML object syntax, with **object.removeAt("****featureName", removeAt)***.*For example:

- print(self); // System.p1 = [10, 20, 30]
- self.removeAt("p1",3); // System.p1 = [10, 20]

If feature has only one value, removeAt index 1 should be used.

Alternatively, structural value can also be removed using ALH API.

```text

```

You can also use the fUML object syntax, with**object.remove("featureName", objectToRemove)**to remove an exact fUML object from a feature. For example:

- self.remove("myPart", myPart)

object.remove("featureName", object) is useful when you have direct reference to fUML object you want to remove. Remove can be used in conjunction with [get method](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/Manipulating+structural+values#Manipulatingstructuralvalues-Gettingastructuralfeaturevalue) to get a direct reference to the fUML object.

Alternatively, structural value can also be removed using ALH API.

```text

```

##### Getting a tag value

You can use script to get a tag value.You can use the fUML object syntax, with **object.getTagValue("tagName")***.* For example:

- self.getTagValue("uniform.max"); // 10

Alternatively, tag value can also be acquired using ALH API.

```text

```

If the RuntimeObject is omitted,**$context$**or**self**will be used as an object. The context object in the parametric is the constraint block object that owns the constraint, not the actual context of the diagram. The following example shows how to get a tag value without specifying a RuntimeObject in ALH API:

```text

```

The following code fragment shows how to get tag values, e.g.,*max*, through ALH API. Also refer to[Value access and references by tags](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/Value+access+and+references+by+tags).

```text

```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

For example:</p><ul><li>value = self.get(&quot;car.speed&quot;);</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="07c4765d-14b8-47b1-be19-08de23d5940d"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Using Groovy scripting language, you can get nested structural features using just dot notation. Users need to handle such UnBoxing by themselves, by using ALH.getValue(object, &quot;speed&quot;).intValue() to get the value of primitive integer type.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3 class="auto-cursor-target">Specifying a structural feature value</h3><p><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">You can use script to specify a structural value. </span>You can use the fUML object syntax, with the <strong>object.set(&quot;featureName&quot;, value)</strong><em>.</em> featureName can use dot notation for nested features. For example:</p><ul><li>value = <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="7d832eec-86ec-4378-92bb-03c8bd89c222">self</ac:inline-comment-marker>.set(&quot;speed&quot;, 10);</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="03be5160-5ee3-4e8a-ad07-8ec6d75553b3"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Using Groovy scripting language, you can set nested structural features using just dot notation. For example, <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="cbe26d64-109a-4b84-a300-e16eaf933033">car.speed=100</ac:inline-comment-marker></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Alternatively, structural value can also be specified using ALH API.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0f440c3d-30d1-4e49-af0b-580dc4b66608"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.setValue(object, "featureName", value)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The following exam<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="eb7a6c1b-afb4-4ff7-bd58-ed1fa3f708a2">ple shows ho</ac:inline-comment-marker>w to specify the value of the structural feature in ALH API:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6c608c3e-a40e-456f-86ac-4427a5ad2506"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[value = ALH.setValue(self, "speed", 10);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>In addition, the Runtime<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="69118d9e-6442-40fe-b289-9c043e8d5a5d">Object can be om</ac:inline-comment-marker>itted. The following example shows how to set a structural feature value without specifying a RuntimeObject in ALH API:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="38b0ddfb-2cb0-4750-815f-12dc55bb4044"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.setValue("featureName", value)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3 class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="letter-spacing: -0.006em;">Adding a value to an object</span></h3><p>You can use script to add a value to an object. <span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">You can use the fUML object syntax, with </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">object.addValue(&quot;featureName&quot;, value)</strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> and </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">object.addValueAt(&quot;featureName&quot;, value, insertAt)</strong><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">.</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> For example:</span></p><ul><li>self.addValue(&quot;p1&quot;,10);          // System.p1 =<span class="error"> [10]</span></li><li>self.addValue(&quot;p1&quot;,30);          // System.p1 = <span class="error">[10, 30]</span></li><li>self.addValueAt(&quot;p1&quot;,20,2);    // System.p1 = <span class="error">[10, 20, 30]</span></li></ul><p>Alternatively, structural value can also be added using ALH API.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a324661f-5913-4703-8ecb-1a834cf4721a"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.addValue(object, "featureName", value)
````html
]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">If you have more than one value in an object  a<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="28798f2d-b609-49c9-be7a-531c4993a9f9">nd if you want</ac:inline-comment-marker> to add a value at a specific position, you can use insertAt argument.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f3670fff-b34c-4593-b11b-cb0f8599085e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.addValueAt(object, "featureName", value, insertAt)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">The following code fragment shows how to add values to an object, e.g., </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">System.p1</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">, through ALH API.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e7d47be2-6ab7-4876-a9b8-4c763586e0e6"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.addValue(self,"p1",10);            // System.p1 = [10]
ALH.addValue(self,"p1",30);            // System.p1 = [10, 30]
ALH.addValueAt(self,"p1",20,2);        // System.p1 = [10, 20, 30]]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3 class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="6fa068df-ca4f-48bd-88c2-3f2affa282e7">Removing a value of an object</ac:inline-comment-marker></h3><p>You can use script to remove a value from an object. <span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">You can use the fUML object syntax, with <strong>object.removeAt(&quot;</strong><strong>featureName&quot;, removeAt)</strong></span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">.</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="89bff52b-7be2-45f5-b135-1d766858079d">For example:</ac:inline-comment-marker></span></p><ul><li>print(self);                                   // System.p1 = <span class="error">[10, 20, 30]</span></li><li>self.removeAt(&quot;p1&quot;,3);               // System.p1 = <span class="error">[10, 20]</span></li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ac4ca2c0-f7f4-4ba0-ad05-b9a9ab7b226a"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If feature has only one value, removeAt index 1 should be used.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="593b7398-271a-46d5-ab04-1562b476483c">Alternatively, str</ac:inline-comment-marker>uctural value can also be removed us<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="1c9da7ff-c75e-40ed-adf3-d89b2fc26560">ing ALH API.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="54276771-a4a3-4a39-b30b-cb3236c5e349"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.removeValueAt(object, "featureName", removeAt)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">You can also use the fUML object syntax, with<strong><span> </span>object.remove(&quot;featureName&quot;, objectToRemove) </strong>to remove an exact fUML object from a feature. For example:</p><ul><li class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">self.remove(&quot;myPart&quot;, myPart)</span></li></ul><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">object.remove(&quot;featureName&quot;, object) is useful when you have direct reference to fUML object you want to remove. Remove can be used in conjunction with <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/Manipulating+structural+values#Manipulatingstructuralvalues-Gettingastructuralfeaturevalue">get method</a> to get a direct reference to the fUML object.</span></p><p>Alternatively, structural value can also be removed using ALH API.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="60cd9055-11ec-4638-b436-5e2665690d99"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.removeValue(object, "featureName", objectToRemove)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3 class="auto-cursor-target">Getting a tag value</h3><p><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">You can use script to get a tag value. </span>You can use the fUML object syntax, with <strong>object.getTagValue(&quot;tagName&quot;)</strong><em>.</em> For example:</p><ul><li>self.getTagValue(&quot;uniform.max&quot;);  // 10</li></ul><p>Alternatively, tag value can also be acquired using ALH API.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a52cbee4-d7c7-4d66-84f8-d15bd36b291e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.getTagValue(object, "tagName")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">If the RuntimeObject is omitted, </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><span class="confluence-link">$context$</span></strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> or </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">self</strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> will be used as an object. The context object in the parametric is the constraint block object that owns the constraint, not the actual context of the diagram. The following example shows how to get a tag value without specifying a RuntimeObject in ALH API:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="76aac9e1-855d-4bb5-b98c-b9d2f153848b"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.getTagValue("tagName")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">The following code fragment shows how to get tag values, e.g., </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">max</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">, through ALH API. Also refer to </span><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/Value+access+and+references+by+tags" style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Value access and references by tags</a><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d3cb6ad5-0daa-4d6d-af44-9d520c1f7b9a"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.getTagValue(self, "uniform.max");	// 10]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570754 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00654: Manual value updates using the Parametric Evaluator

- page_id: `249570754`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570754/Manual+value+updates+using+the+Parametric+Evaluator
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2026-04-13T10:12:00.684+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator
- labels: ['value-updates-with-parametric-evaluator']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Changing values on a property of an object will cause the parametric evaluator to automatically update the other related values with the constraint defined on the object. Or, you may want to update the values with the constraint once you set all necessary values of the properties. The latter is possible by executing your model with the SimulationConfig whose fireValueChangeEvent = false (see section [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SimulationConfig stereotype' space=''] for more information about the SimulationConfig and its tag definitions) and call the API provided by the parametric evaluator whenever you desire. The following figure shows an example of updating values manually with the parametric evaluator.

**Signature**: com.nomagic.magicdraw.simulation.parametrics.ParametricsEngine.executeObject(Object object)

**Parameter**: Object is an object whose internal values will be updated by the parametric evaluator.

You can call the API with the MagicDraw script engine. For example, you may define it in the body of an opaque behavior. Then, use a call behavior action to call the opaque behavior somewhere in your model.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Manual Value Update with Activity in CylinderPipe.mdzip.

#### Sample model

The model used in the figures on this page is the *CylinderPipe.mdzip* sample model that comes with the modeling tool. You can find it in *<modeling tool installation directory>\samples\simulation\Parametrics\CylinderPipe.mdzip.*

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying the language for the expression' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Value binding' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Evaluating expressions' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dynamic constraint' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Communicating with evaluators through simulation console' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Built-in Math' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Integration with external Evaluators' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Trade study analysis' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sample project' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>Changing values on a property of an object will cause the parametric evaluator to automatically update the other related values with the constraint defined on the object. Or, you may want to update the values with the constraint once you set all necessary values of the properties. The latter is possible by executing your model with the SimulationConfig whose fireValueChangeEvent = false (see section <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SimulationConfig stereotype" /></ac:link> for more information about the SimulationConfig and its tag definitions) and call the API provided by the parametric evaluator whenever you desire. The following figure shows an example of updating values manually with the parametric evaluator. </p><p><strong>Signature</strong>: com.nomagic.magicdraw.simulation.parametrics.ParametricsEngine.executeObject(Object object)</p><p><strong>Parameter</strong>: Object is an object whose internal values will be updated by the parametric evaluator. </p><p>You can call the API with the MagicDraw script engine. For example, you may define it in the body of an opaque behavior. Then, use a call behavior action to call the opaque behavior somewhere in your model. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Manual Value Update with Activity in CylinderPipe.mdzip" ac:alt="Manual Value Update with Activity in CylinderPipe.mdzip"><ri:attachment ri:filename="manualUpdate.png" /></ac:image><br />Manual Value Update with Activity in CylinderPipe.mdzip.</h6><p><br /></p><h2>Sample model</h2><p>The model used in the figures on this page is the <em>CylinderPipe.mdzip</em> sample model that comes with the modeling tool. You can find it in <em>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\samples\simulation\Parametrics\CylinderPipe.mdzip.</em></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="50d8e1b9-501e-49ea-b2ea-395122b7c8e1"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specifying the language for the expression" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="79235d71-d19e-4dc8-ba7f-199e90e4de44"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Value binding" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="c3290f7e-8587-4040-b253-b77cdbb084a7"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Evaluating expressions" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="29676e63-4175-47b3-b3ac-2c55d218ffff"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Dynamic constraint" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="10b0ee4f-5271-4015-b130-ddb5bf7c1b01"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Communicating with evaluators through simulation console" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="64ca3768-bfb0-49d9-9e0f-983f9d3d2029"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Built-in Math" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="5650be90-c3f8-44d4-8635-386d3898b3d1"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Integration with external Evaluators" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="3e571c6c-8afb-4e1b-9c00-cbfdc78726bf"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Trade study analysis" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="a8394157-362f-48a4-9f8e-464a32d75c40"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Sample project" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570703 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00655: Many-to-one binding calculations

- page_id: `249570703`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570703/Many-to-one+binding+calculations
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:15.147+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator > Value binding
- labels: ['many-to-one-binding-calculations']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Constraint Blocks in Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit support many value properties connected to a constraint parameter with multiplicity [*] to perform a specification without updating values back to those properties. Any value properties which are not connected to the constraint parameter will not be used as an input. The constraint parameter [*] shows a list of the connected value properties in the **Variables** pane, **Console** pane (Info), and during debugging. The result from the specification of the constraint Block is calculated from the list of value properties. For example:

```text

```

You can also use Add/Remove values with the parameter [*] in the **Variables** pane as shown in the following figure.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Many-to-one binding supported by constraint Blocks through many value properties.

A constraint Block supports many multiplicities of Part property connected to a constraint parameter with multiplicity [*] to perform a specification as in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Many-to-one binding supported by constraint Blocks through multiplicities of Part property.

A constraint Block supports many multiplicities of Participant property of an association Block connected to a constraint parameter with multiplicity [*] to perform a specification. The Connector must be set type as the association Block in order to be a member of the input. Also, **{end=...}** must be specified for each Participant. Any participant properties of a Connector property will be synchronized with both the constraint parameter [*] and value properties as shown in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Many-to-one binding supported by constraint Blocks through multiplicities of Participant property.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Constraint Blocks in Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit support many value properties connected to a constraint parameter with multiplicity [*] to perform a specification without updating values back to those properties. Any value properties which are not connected to the constraint parameter will not be used as an input. The constraint parameter [*] shows a list of the connected value properties in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane, <strong>Console</strong> pane (Info), and during debugging. The result from the specification of the constraint Block is calculated from the list of value properties. For example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="907df08e-f0bb-4aef-a64a-ba1e1ebd20ed"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[inputs [*] = [2.0;3.0;4.0];
output = sum(inputs);
output = 9.0;]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>You can also use Add/Remove values with the parameter [*] in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane as shown in the following figure.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Many-to-one Binding Supported through Value Properties.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Many-to-one binding supported by constraint Blocks through many value properties.</h6><p>A constraint Block supports many multiplicities of Part property connected to a constraint parameter with multiplicity [*] to perform a specification as in the figure below.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Many-to-one Binding Supported through Multiplicity.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Many-to-one binding supported by constraint Blocks through multiplicities of Part property.</h6><p>A constraint Block supports many multiplicities of Participant property of an association Block connected to a constraint parameter with multiplicity [*] to perform a specification. The Connector must be set type as the association Block in order to be a member of the input. Also, <strong>{end=...}</strong> must be specified for each Participant. Any participant properties of a Connector property will be synchronized with both the constraint parameter [*] and value properties as shown in the figure below.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Many-to-one Binding Supported through Multiplicities of Participant Property.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Many-to-one binding supported by constraint Blocks through multiplicities of Participant property.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570714 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00656: Mathematical equation

- page_id: `249570714`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570714/Mathematical+equation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:15.430+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator > Evaluating expressions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

402879988

402879990

402879989

When solving a constraint that is specified by a mathematical equation, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will find a 'target' by evaluating a 'given'. By default, a target is on the left-hand side of the equation and a given on the right-hand side of an equation.

The following figure shows an example of constraint parameters target and given in a mathematical equation.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Evaluating a Mathematical Equation in a SysML Model.

As you can see from the example shown in the preceding figure, the block **Circle** contains a constraint property typed by the constraint block **Circle** **Area**. The constraint block **Circle** **Area** has a constraint that is defined by the mathematical expression **{area =** **3.14159 * (radius** **^** **2)}**. The target and the given in the equation are the constraint parameters '**area**' and '**radius**' respectively. These constraint parameters are bound to the value properties of the block **Circle**. The constraint parameter area of e1 is bound to the value property area of the block **Circle**, and the constraint parameter radius of e1 is bound to the value property radius of the block **Circle**. So, the values of the constraint parameters and the value properties bound together are always equal.

If you simulate the block **Circle**, the parametric evaluator in Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will create an object **Circle** and its internal values. It will substitute the value of radius of the block object **Circle** to the equation and calculate the value of area. Once it obtains the value of area, parametric evaluator will assign it to the area value property of the object **Circle**. If you change the value of the radius, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will automatically calculate and update the value of the area with the result from evaluating the equation.

You can also define a constraint on a UML class to constrain the values of its properties. The following figure shows a constraint on the UML class **Circle**. The constraint is defined by the same Mathematical expression as in the figure below **{area** **=** **3.14159** *** **(radius** **^** **2)}**. If you simulate the class **Circle**, the parametric evaluator will evaluate the value of radius in the equation. It will then use the value resulting from evaluating the value of radius to update the value of property **area**.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Evaluating Mathematical Equation on a UML Class.

402879987

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Evaluating expressions' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Logical expression' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6f229143-5138-4118-b649-9ddcb675ec8c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402879988</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="45810f19-0e7a-440c-8eed-6a121588c841"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402879990</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="db17be82-b2d5-4bda-91e8-059e715cf533"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402879989</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>When solving a constraint that is specified by a mathematical equation, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will find a 'target' by evaluating a 'given'. By default, a target is on the left-hand side of the equation and a given on the right-hand side of an equation. </p><p>The following figure shows an example of constraint parameters target and given in a mathematical equation. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Evaluating a Mathematical Equation in a SysML Model" ac:alt="Evaluating a Mathematical Equation in a SysML Model"><ri:attachment ri:filename="equationSysml.png" /></ac:image><br />Evaluating a Mathematical Equation in a SysML Model.</h6><p>As you can see from the example shown in the preceding figure, the block <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Circle</strong> contains a constraint property typed by the constraint block <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Circle</strong> <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Area</strong>. The constraint block <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Circle</strong> <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Area</strong> has a constraint that is defined by the mathematical expression <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">{area =</strong> <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">3.14159 * (radius</strong> <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">^</strong> <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">2)}</strong>. The target and the given in the equation are the constraint parameters '<strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">area</strong>' and '<strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">radius</strong>' respectively. These constraint parameters are bound to the value properties of the block <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Circle</strong>. The constraint parameter area of e1 is bound to the value property area of the block <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Circle</strong>, and the constraint parameter radius of e1 is bound to the value property radius of the block <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Circle</strong>. So, the values of the constraint parameters and the value properties bound together are always equal. </p><p>If you simulate the block <strong>Circle</strong>, the parametric evaluator in Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will create an object <strong>Circle</strong> and its internal values. It will substitute the value of radius of the block object <strong>Circle</strong> to the equation and calculate the value of area. Once it obtains the value of area, parametric evaluator will assign it to the area value property of the object <strong>Circle</strong>. If you change the value of the radius, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will automatically calculate and update the value of the area with the result from evaluating the equation. </p><p>You can also define a constraint on a UML class to constrain the values of its properties. The following figure shows a constraint on the UML class <strong>Circle</strong>. The constraint is defined by the same Mathematical expression as in the figure below <strong>{area</strong> <strong>=</strong> <strong>3.14159</strong> *** <strong>(radius</strong> <strong>^</strong> <strong>2)}</strong>. If you simulate the class <strong>Circle</strong>, the parametric evaluator will evaluate the value of radius in the equation. It will then use the value resulting from evaluating the value of radius to update the value of property <strong>area</strong>. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Evaluating Mathematical Equation on a UML Class" ac:alt="Evaluating Mathematical Equation on a UML Class"><ri:attachment ri:filename="equationUml.png" /></ac:image><br />Evaluating Mathematical Equation on a UML Class.</h6><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7eae6f3c-1c04-44c0-9baf-5c7f5b40112a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402879987</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Evaluating expressions" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Logical expression" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570567 space=MMA version=3 -->
## PAGE 00657: Message

- page_id: `249570567`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570567/Message
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:13.506+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Interaction simulation > Supported elements in interaction simulation
- labels: ['message']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Warning

379978608

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Warning

379978610

379978607

**On this page**

22

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Warning

379978609

According to the UML specification, a message defines a particular communication between lifelines of an Interaction. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit simulates messages whose message sorts are **asynchSignal**, **synchCall**, **asynchCall**, and **reply**. You can specify a connector for the message. If the role of one connector end is the property specified by a source object of the message, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will send the message along the connector. A target object will be the object at the other end of the connector.

#### Asynchronous Signal Message (asynchSignal)

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit simulates an asynchronous signal message by creating a signal object from a signal specified as a signature of the message. It sends the signal object to a target object asynchronously. The following figure shows that the bank sent the signal **maintain** to the teller using an asynchronous signal message. When the target object (the ATM object specifying the teller) received the signal, the State changed to **Maintained**.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Simulation console showing the simulation of an asynchronous signal message.

#### Synchronous Call Message (synchCall)

When executing a synchronous call message, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit simulates a Behavior (the method of called operation of a target object) from beginning to end before it can carry out the next message. The simulation method is similar to that of the call operation Action with "isSynchronous = true".

You can substitute values for input parameters of the called operation by specifying argument values on a synchronous call message.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Simulation console showing the execution of an asynchronous call message.

The preceding figure illustrates the synchronous call messages simulation. A synchronous call message **1** shows the bank object calling the operation **retrieveAccount(accountNumber : String)** of the ledger object. The substitution value for **accountNumber** is the value of **lookupAccountNumber**, which is 0003 in this example. **lookupAccountNumber** is the property of the bank.

The simulation looked for the account object with that particular **accountNumber** and returned it to the bank object with a Reply Message **2** (2 is a Reply Message of 1). The returned account object would be specified as the value of the **buyerAccount** represented by the lifeline **buyerAccount:Account**. The bank object would then call the operation **getBalance()** of the account object with a synchronous call message **3**that caused the balance value to reply to the bank with a Reply Message **4** (4 is a Reply Message of 3). Finally, the bank called itself to print the balance value with the operation **printBalance(balance:Real)**.

#### Asynchronous Call Message (asynchCall)

The simulation of an asynchronous call message is similar to that of a synchronous call message. In this simulation, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit simulates a Behavior (a method of a called operation of a target object) on a new thread. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will immediately proceed to the next message once the simulation of the Behavior starts. It will not wait until the simulation of the Behavior completes. This type of simulation is similar to that of a call operation Action with "isSynchronous = false".

#### Reply Message (Reply)

A Reply Message is a Message sent as a Reply to a Call Message (synchronous or asynchronous). If there is an argument value specified in the Reply Message, Simulation will evaluate the argument value.

#### WARNING: Warning

Warning

A Reply Message must have only one returned argument. If the Reply Message violates the rule, Simulation will print an error message in the [**Console** pane](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/Console+pane).

If the argument is not the name of a property, Simulation will verify (compare) the returned value from the called operation with the argument value.

If the result of evaluating the argument is the name of a property owned by a target object, a context object, or a source object, the value returned from the operation will be defined as the value of that property. The Reply Message **2** in the preceding figure has an opaque expression whose body is **buyerAccount** as an argument value.

**buyerAccount** is the property of the **Balance Lookup** Class which is the context of a sequence diagram. Therefore, the returned account object from the operation of the ledger object **retrieveAccount(accountNumber : String)** will be set as a value of the property **buyerAccount** when the Reply Message 2 is simulated.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE
