<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570643 space=MMA version=3 -->
## PAGE 00678: Recording simulation as a Sequence diagram

- page_id: `249570643`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570643/Recording+simulation+as+a+Sequence+diagram
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:14.230+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Interaction simulation
- labels: ['recording-simulation-as-sequence-diagram']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The recording capability of the Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to:

- record created objects as CreateMessages connected between Lifelines that represent the object creator and features of the created object respectively.
- record signals as SendMessages connected between Lifelines that represent signal senders and signal receivers respectively. Connectors will be assigned to the messages if signals are sent via ports or connectors.
- record operation calls as CallMessages connected between Lifelines that represent operation callers and operation owners respectively. Connectors will be assigned to messages if operations are called via ports.
- record changes of states and primitive values as StateInvariants on Lifelines that represent features of objects that own the states or the values.

This section demonstrates how to record the signal, state change, operation call, and value change as a sequence diagram during a model simulation. The sample FlashingLight.mdzip, located in the *<*install_root*>/samples/simulation/* directory, will be used throughout this section.

To record signals sent from and to a runtime object and subsequent state/value changes of the related objects as a sequence diagram

1. In the Variables pane, select and right-click a runtime object.
2. Click Create Sequence Diagram on the context menu. An empty sequence diagram will be created. [ATTACHMENT filename='Create Sequence Diagram.png']

Whenever you simulate a model (such as Flashing Light as shown in the figure above), Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will:

- create the first Lifeline, which represents the selected runtime object.
- record each signal sent from the selected runtime object as a Message in the sequence diagram.
- record each operation call caused by a call message, a CallOperationAction, or an ALH.callOperation with argument and return value as messages in the sequence diagram.
- record an object that receives a signal and/or an operation call as a Lifeline. Unless it exists in the diagram, the object will be called a 'lifeline object'.
- record each change in the state of a lifeline object as a StateInvariant on the Lifeline, with the changed state symbol.
- record each change in the feature value of a lifeline object as a StateInvariant on the Lifeline. Changes in value are enclosed in constraint brackets, i.e., {a=10}.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

StateInvariants are designated by yellow rounded rectangles. See the following figure for examples.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
A sequence diagram depicting call messages running between lifelines.

To see what connector a signal or an operation call is sent through

- Double-click the message or right-click it and select Specification to open the specification window. [ATTACHMENT filename='Specification_Window_Message.png']

To see the values of arguments sent with a signal or an operation call

1. Either double-click a message or right-click it and select Specification to open the specification window.
2. Select Arguments in the tree on the left-hand side of the specification dialog to see the value of each argument.

You can customize recorded messages (signals) and lifelines using **SequenceDiagramGeneratorConfig**.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
A SequenceDiagramGeneratorConfig element showing its parameters.

A **SequenceDiagramGeneratorConfig** is a stereotype that is inherited from an **ExecutionListener** stereotype. It contains the following six tag definitions:

- Owner An element that owns a generated Interaction element. A generated Sequence diagram will be created under that particular Interaction element. You need to select only the element that can own an Interaction element; otherwise, a model inconsistency will occur.
- Ignored Signals Signals that will be ignored (will not be recorded) during a simulation recording.
- Ignored Lifelines A list of elements (objects) that will be ignored (will not be recorded as lifelines) during a simulation recording. This list takes priority over the value list.
- Recorded Object Path Used to specify a property path to an object that will be recorded by a sequence diagram generator. The path must start from a property owned by either a classifier which is the target of the simulation configuration, or a classifier of an instance specification which is the target of the simulation configuration. The property at each successive position following the first position must be contained in the classifier that types the property at the immediately preceding position.
- Record State Change A boolean option. If true, state changes will be recorded.
- Record Value Change A boolean option. If true, value changes will be recorded.
- Record Timestamp A boolean option. If true, timestamps will be recorded on messages.
- Value A structural feature whose value is represented by the configuration.

You can specify the default values of *recordStateChange*,*recordValueChange*, and *recordTimestamp* through the[**Project** **Options**](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/Project+options) dialog. The values in the project options will be used if the tagged values of the sequence diagram generator are not specified. The default values of these project options are **true**,**true**, and **false** (see the following figure). **True** means all values will be recorded by default.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Options in the Sequence Diagram Generator group in the Project Options dialog.

To customize a Sequence diagram recording

1. In the Containment tree, right-click the Model folder (root folder) and select Create Diagram . [ATTACHMENT filename='Creating Simulation Config Diagram in Containment Tree.png']
2. Do one of the following:
  - I n the dialog, expand**Simulation**and select**Simulation Configuration Diagram****.** [ATTACHMENT filename='Selecting Simulation Config Diagram 1.png']
  - In the search tab, type the keyword simulation and then select**Simulation Configuration Diagram** . 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. From the Simulation Configuration Diagram palette, drag the Sequence Diagram Generator to the diagram pane. [ATTACHMENT filename='Drag and Drop.png']
4. Right-click the newly created **SequenceDiagramGeneratorConfig** and select **Specification** to open its**Specification**dialog.[IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Specify the value(s) of the tag definition(s) of the config, e.g., **Name**. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] TipYou can specify **Represents** and **Value** to be captured by the **SequenceDiagramGeneratorConfig** along with other settings, such as **RecordTimestamp**.[IMAGE alt='' src='']
6. Drag the configured SequenceDiagramGeneratorConfig to the SimulationConfig element (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SimulationConfig stereotype' space=''] ). The executionListeners tag of the SimulationConfig will be shown with the specified name of the SequenceDiagramGeneratorConfig , as shown below. [ATTACHMENT filename='Final Specified Simulation.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The recording capability of the Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to:</p><ul><li>record created objects as CreateMessages connected between Lifelines that represent the object creator and features of the created object respectively.</li><li>record signals as SendMessages connected between Lifelines that represent signal senders and signal receivers respectively. Connectors will be assigned to the messages if signals are sent via ports or connectors.</li><li>record operation calls as CallMessages connected between Lifelines that represent operation callers and operation owners respectively. Connectors will be assigned to messages if operations are called via ports.</li><li>record changes of states and primitive values as StateInvariants on Lifelines that represent features of objects that own the states or the values. </li></ul><p>This section demonstrates how to record the signal, state change, operation call, and value change as a sequence diagram during a model simulation. The sample FlashingLight.mdzip, located in the <em>&lt;<em style="text-align: left;">install_root</em>&gt;/samples/simulation/</em> directory, will be used throughout this section. <br /> </p><p>To record signals sent from and to a runtime object and subsequent state/value changes of the related objects as a sequence diagram </p><hr /><ol><li>In the <strong>Variables</strong> pane, select and right-click a runtime object.</li><li>Click <strong>Create Sequence Diagram</strong> on the context menu. An empty sequence diagram will be created.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Create Sequence Diagram.png" /></ac:image></li></ol><p>Whenever you simulate a model (such as Flashing Light as shown in the figure above), Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will:</p><ul><li>create the first Lifeline, which represents the selected runtime object.</li><li>record each signal sent from the selected runtime object as a Message in the sequence diagram.</li><li>record each operation call caused by a call message, a CallOperationAction, or an ALH.callOperation with argument and return value as messages in the sequence diagram.</li><li>record an object that receives a signal and/or an operation call as a Lifeline. Unless it exists in the diagram, the object will be called a 'lifeline object'. </li><li>record each change in the state of a lifeline object as a StateInvariant on the Lifeline, with the changed state symbol.</li><li>record each change in the feature value of a lifeline object as a StateInvariant on the Lifeline. Changes in value are enclosed in constraint brackets, i.e., {a=10}. </li></ul><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6c49bee1-aafd-4a17-82fc-e55ab57bd3aa"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>StateInvariants are designated by yellow rounded rectangles. See the following figure for examples.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h6 style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Block System.png" /></ac:image><br />A sequence diagram depicting call messages running between lifelines.<br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></h6><p>To see what connector a signal or an operation call is sent through</p><hr /><ul><li>Double-click the message or right-click it and select <strong>Specification</strong> to open the specification window. <br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Specification_Window_Message.png" /></ac:image></li></ul><p><br /></p><p>To see the values of arguments sent with a signal or an operation call</p><hr /><ol><li>Either double-click a message or right-click it and select <strong>Specification</strong> to open the specification window.</li><li>Select <strong>Arguments</strong> in the tree on the left-hand side of the specification dialog to see the value of each argument. </li></ol><p>You can customize recorded messages (signals) and lifelines using <strong>SequenceDiagramGeneratorConfig</strong>. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="219"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sequence Diagram Generator.png" /></ac:image><br />A SequenceDiagramGeneratorConfig element showing its parameters.</h6><p>A <strong>SequenceDiagramGeneratorConfig</strong> is a stereotype that is inherited from an <strong>ExecutionListener</strong> stereotype. It contains the following six tag definitions: </p><ul><li><strong>Owner</strong> <br />An element that owns a generated Interaction element. A generated Sequence diagram will be created under that particular Interaction element. You need to select only the element that can own an Interaction element; otherwise, a model inconsistency will occur.<br /> </li><li><strong>Ignored Signals</strong> <br />Signals that will be ignored (will not be recorded) during a simulation recording.<br /> </li><li><strong>Ignored Lifelines</strong> <br />A list of elements (objects) that will be ignored (will not be recorded as lifelines) during a simulation recording. This list takes priority over the <strong>value</strong> list.<br /><br /></li><li><strong>Recorded Object Path</strong> <br />Used to specify a property path to an object that will be recorded by a sequence diagram generator. The path must start from a property owned by either a classifier which is the target of the simulation configuration, or a classifier of an instance specification which is the target of the simulation configuration. The property at each successive position following the first position must be contained in the classifier that types the property at the immediately preceding position. <br /><br /></li><li><strong>Record State Change</strong> <br />A boolean option. If true, state changes will be recorded. <br /><br /></li><li><strong>Record Value Change</strong> <br />A boolean option. If true, value changes will be recorded. <br /><br /></li><li><strong>Record Timestamp</strong> <br />A boolean option. If true, timestamps will be recorded on messages.<br /><br /></li><li><strong>Value<br /></strong>A structural feature whose value is represented by the configuration.</li></ul><p>You can specify the default values of <em>recordStateChange</em>,<em> recordValueChange</em>, and <em>recordTimestamp</em> through the<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> </span><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/Project+options"><strong>Project</strong> <strong>Options</strong></a> dialog. The values in the project options will be used if the tagged values of the sequence diagram generator are not specified. The default values of these project options are <strong>true</strong>,<strong> true</strong>, and <strong>false</strong> (see the following figure). <strong>True</strong> means all values will be recorded by default.<br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sequence Diagram Generator options.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Options in the Sequence Diagram Generator group in the Project Options dialog.</h6><p>To customize a Sequence diagram recording <span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);"> </span></p><hr /><ol><li>In the Containment tree, right-click the <strong>Model</strong> folder (root folder) and select <strong>Create Diagram</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Creating Simulation Config Diagram in Containment Tree.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Do one of the following:</span><ul><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">I</span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">n the dialog, expand<span> </span><strong>Simulation </strong>and select<span> <strong style="text-align: left;">Simulation Configuration Diagram</strong></span><strong>.<br /></strong></span><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Selecting Simulation Config Diagram 1.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">In the search tab, type the keyword simulation and then select<span> <strong style="text-align: left;">Simulation Configuration Diagram</strong></span></span><strong style="text-align: left;">.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Search Tab.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></strong></li></ul></li><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="2633e203-3b24-46e7-bb1a-f378373a90ad">From the </ac:inline-comment-marker><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="2633e203-3b24-46e7-bb1a-f378373a90ad">Simulation Configuration Diagram</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="2633e203-3b24-46e7-bb1a-f378373a90ad"> palette, drag the </ac:inline-comment-marker><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="2633e203-3b24-46e7-bb1a-f378373a90ad">Sequence Diagram Generator </ac:inline-comment-marker></strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="2633e203-3b24-46e7-bb1a-f378373a90ad">to the diagram pane.<br /></ac:inline-comment-marker><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Drag and Drop.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="2633e203-3b24-46e7-bb1a-f378373a90ad">Right-click the newly created <strong>SequenceDiagramGeneratorConfig</strong> and select <strong>Specification</strong> to open its </ac:inline-comment-marker><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="2633e203-3b24-46e7-bb1a-f378373a90ad">Specification</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="2633e203-3b24-46e7-bb1a-f378373a90ad"> dialog</ac:inline-comment-marker><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="2633e203-3b24-46e7-bb1a-f378373a90ad">.<br /><br /></ac:inline-comment-marker><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Selecting Specification Menu.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="2633e203-3b24-46e7-bb1a-f378373a90ad">Specify the value(s) of the tag definition(s) of the config, e.g., <strong>Name</strong>.<br /><br /></ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Specification_Window.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b560b0c8-6420-4c5a-a2d6-3c82d6e99cfd"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Tip</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can specify <strong>Represents</strong> and <strong>Value</strong> to be captured by the <strong>SequenceDiagramGeneratorConfig</strong> along with other settings, such as <strong>RecordTimestamp</strong>.<br /><br /></p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Specification_Represents and Value.png" /></ac:image></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li>Drag the configured <strong>SequenceDiagramGeneratorConfig</strong> to the <strong>SimulationConfig</strong> element (see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SimulationConfig stereotype" /></ac:link>). The <strong>executionListeners</strong> tag of the <strong>SimulationConfig</strong> will be shown with the specified name of the <strong>SequenceDiagramGeneratorConfig</strong>, as shown below.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Final Specified Simulation.png" /></ac:image></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570278 space=MMA version=4 -->
## PAGE 00679: Recording Verification status of runtime values

- page_id: `249570278`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570278/Recording+Verification+status+of+runtime+values
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-09-12T15:11:51.948+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation debugging > Runtime Value Monitoring
- labels: ['runtime-value-verification-status', 'recording-runtime-value-verification-status']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

46423820

46423823

1996096896

**On this page**

33

46423822

##### Instance Table with simulation results

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit allows you to record the verification status of runtime values. The cells in the Variables pane and in Instance tables are highlighted in green if runtime values meet the constraints during the simulation, and in red - if they do not. You can do the normal recording verification status of runtime values by using the**resultLocation**tag in a[Simulation Configuration](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/SimulationConfig+stereotype). The instances with recorded values in slots are created at the end of the simulation.

To export the simulation results to an Instance Specification

- After running the simulation, do one of the following :
  - In the Variables pane of the Simulation window, click [ATTACHMENT filename='export_to_instance.png'] .
  - In the Variables pane of the Simulation window, right-click the simulated element and select Export Value To > Instance .
  - Specify the resultLocation tag in a Simulation Configuration . The instances with recorded values in slots are created at the end of the simulation.

The variables are evaluated and colored in red and/or green depending on whether there are any constraint failures in the Instance Table.

Verification Status

Display Legends

Verification Status

Display

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The instance table with highlighted passed and failed values.

The verification status and instances for constraints are recorded using the **VerificationStatus**stereotype that is applied to the **Value** specification of the properties you are working on. You can also see the details of the constraint failure in the tooltip when hovering the mouse over any highlighted values in the **Variables** tab of the Simulation window as shown in the following figure.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Variables pane displaying values with verification status.

In addition, the failed verdict values of [State Invariant](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MMA/State+invariant) are highlighted in red, and the passed values are highlighted in green. However, you must create a Dependency relationship between the test case and a value property as shown in the following figure.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Failed State Invariant with highlighted red values and the tooltip.

If the **VerificationStatus** state on the **Value** specification fails, the active validation will be marked for notification with the **fail** status tag as shown in the following figure

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Specification window showing verification status with the fail status tag.

The **VerificationStatus** stereotype, as shown in the figure above, has the tags as follows

| Tag | Description |
| --- | --- |
| constraint | The name of the failure constraint. |
| margin | The Requirement margin value calculated from value properties and the Requirement boundary (a satisfy Relation). |
| requirement | The name of the failure Requirement. |
| status | The result of the verification, either pass or fail. |
| timestamp | The timestamp of the first failure recorded. |

##### Remember Failure Status option

During the evaluation of particular configurations and scenarios, some Requirements/constraints can fail, and those values are marked in red. However, they may pass later and are marked in green again, but the failure is not recorded by default. To solve this problem, Simulation Toolkit provides the **Remember Failure Status** option in «SimulationConfig» to remember any failure status until the termination and record it as fail, even though it passes at the end. If the **Remember Failure Status** option is set true, and there are constraint failures during the simulation, the first fail status will be recorded in the status of «VerificationStatus» of new/recorded instance, and the time of the first failure will also be recorded in the **timestamp** tag as shown in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Setting the Remember Failure Status tag of a SimulationConfig to record the fail status and timestamp if available.

46423819

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Requirement Table1' space='MT']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Variables pane' space='']

The instances with recorded values in slots are created at the end of the simulation.</li></ul></li></ul><p><br />The variables are evaluated and colored in red and/or green depending on whether there are any constraint failures in the Instance Table.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ae6092c5-411e-49b1-8381-a6bb19c71b59"><ac:rich-text-body>For variables to be highlighted in an Instance Table as shown below, the <strong>Verification Status</strong> legend must be applied to an Instance Table. To do that, click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="16"><ri:attachment ri:filename="legend_icon.png" /></ac:image>, select <strong>Display Legends</strong>, select the <strong>Verification Status</strong> legend, and click <strong>Display</strong>.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="simulation_results_in_instance_table.png" /></ac:image><br />The instance table with highlighted passed and failed values.</h6><p>The verification status and instances for constraints are recorded using the <strong>VerificationStatus </strong>stereotype that is applied to the <strong>Value</strong> specification of the properties you are working on. However, you must create a Dependency relationship between the test case and a value property as shown in the following figure.</p><p><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="State Invariant Failed with Highlighted Red Values.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Failed State Invariant with highlighted red <strong>fail</strong> status tag as shown in the following figure</p><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification_window_with_verification_status.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Specification window showing verification status with the fail status tag.</h6><p>The <strong>VerificationStatus</strong> stereotype, as shown in the figure above, has the tags as follows</p><table class="relative-table wrapped"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th><p>Tag</p></th><th><p>Description</p></th></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><strong>constraint</strong></td><td colspan="1">The name of the failure constraint.</td></tr><tr><td Failure Status option</h3><p>During the evaluation of particular configurations and scenarios, some Requirements/constraints can fail, and those values are marked in red. However, they may pass later and are marked in green again, but the failure is not recorded by default. To solve this problem, Simulation Toolkit provides the <strong>Remember Failure Status</strong> option in «SimulationConfig» to remember any failure status until the termination and record it as fail, even though it passes at the end. If the <strong>Remember Failure Status</strong> option is set true, and there are constraint failures during the simulation, the first fail status will be recorded in the status of «VerificationStatus» of new/recorded instance, and the time of the first failure will also be recorded in the <strong>timestamp</strong> tag as shown in the figure below.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="1000"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Remember Failure Status for Fail Status Record.PNG" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Setting the Remember Failure Status tag of a SimulationConfig to record the fail status and timestamp if available.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e47a68d4-2ab5-4094-bcff-418b02a032db"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">46423819</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Requirement Table1" ri:space-key="MT" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Variables pane" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249571010 space=MMA version=4 -->
## PAGE 00680: References to elements with HTML

- page_id: `249571010`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249571010/References+to+elements+with+HTML
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-09-15T15:51:02.919+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Action languages
- labels: ['references-to-elements-using-html']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit can make references to model Elements using HTML through the [Edit Hyperlink dialog](https://docs.nomagic.com/MT/?contextKey=edit-hyperlink-dialog&version=latest&variant=default). Those objects that allow references are ValueSpecification.Value, LiteralString.Value, OpaqueExpression.Body, OpaqueAction.Body, OpaqueBehavior.Body, Constraint.Specification, and ObjectFlow.Guard.

This feature has been developed to have references in text to model elements in various scripts. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit converts HTML into plain texts before execution. In the following figure, ValueSpecification.Value (b) can refer to slot **(b = 4)** of an InstanceSpecification (Ins). If the value of slot **(b = 4)** changes, ValueSpecification.Value (b) will change accordingly.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A sample of references to elements with HTML.

**Related page**

- Edit Hyperlink dialog

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit can make references to model Elements using HTML through the <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/MT/?contextKey=edit-hyperlink-dialog&amp;version=latest&amp;variant=default">Edit Hyperlink dialog</a>. Those objects that allow references are ValueSpecification.Value, LiteralString.Value, OpaqueExpression.Body, OpaqueAction.Body, OpaqueBehavior.Body, Constraint.Specification, and ObjectFlow.Guard.</p><p>This feature has been developed to have references in text to model elements in various scripts. Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit converts HTML into plain texts before execution. In the following figure, ValueSpecification.Value (b) can refer to slot <strong>(b = 4)</strong> of an InstanceSpecification (Ins). If the value of slot <strong>(b = 4)</strong> changes, ValueSpecification.Value (b) will change accordingly.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="References to HTML elements.PNG" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">A sample of references to elements with HTML.</h6></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="c53f7a47-c2bd-4084-938e-3bc906aed77f"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/MT/?contextKey=edit-hyperlink-dialog&amp;version=latest&amp;variant=default">Edit Hyperlink dialog</a></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249570318 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00681: Removing breakpoints

- page_id: `249570318`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249570318/Removing+breakpoints
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-09-28T10:03:45.091+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation debugging > Breakpoints
- labels: ['removing-breakpoints']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can remove a Breakpoint from a model using the model's **Remove Breakpoint(s)** context menu.

To remove a Breakpoint

- Right-click a model element that has a breakpoint(s) and select Simulation > Remove Breakpoint(s) .

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can also click the **Remove****Breakpoint(s)** or **Remove****All****Breakpoints** toolbar button or select the **Remove Breakpoint(s)**context menu in the **Breakpoints** pane to remove all existing breakpoints. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can remove a Breakpoint from a model using the model's <strong>Remove Breakpoint(s)</strong> context menu. </p><p>To remove a Breakpoint</p><hr /><ul><li>Right-click a model element that has a breakpoint(s) and select <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Remove </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Breakpoint(s)</strong>. </li></ul><h6 style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:title="Removing a Breakpoint" ac:alt="Removing a Breakpoint"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Figure_0033.png" /></ac:image></h6><p>You can also click the <strong>Remove </strong><strong>Breakpoint(s)</strong> or <strong>Remove </strong><strong>All </strong><strong>Breakpoints</strong> toolbar button or select the <strong>Remove Breakpoint(s) </strong>context menu in the <strong>Breakpoints</strong> pane to remove all existing breakpoints. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Removing Breakpoints Using the Breakpoints Pane Context Menu" ac:alt="Removing Breakpoints Using the Breakpoints Pane Context Menu"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Figure_0034.png" /></ac:image></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249569781 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00682: Representing data from a CSV file in a line chart

- page_id: `249569781`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249569781/Representing+data+from+a+CSV+file+in+a+line+chart
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:07.208+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > Time Series Chart
- labels: ['csv-file-data-in-time-series-chart', 'showing-csv-file-data']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1418925668

1418925670

1418925669

The Time series chart can read data from a CSV file and represent them in a curved line in the chart. The property External CSV in the Time series chart enables you to import an external CSV file and load data from the file.

The time Series Chart can read a CSV file with data in multiple columns and show them in lines in the chart. (The first column is always the value of time on the y-axis) and read multiple CSV files and show them all at once.

To show data from a CSV file in the Time series chart

1. Click the Load Data... button on the toolbar of the Time series chart's plot pane. [ATTACHMENT filename='loaddata_button.png']
2. Select a CSV file (see an example of the result data in a CSV file), and the Time series chart will show data from the columns in the file in lines. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The following figure shows a line chart. The solid line in the chart represents the values plotted Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit while the dashed lines represent the values from the CSV file. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A line chart with the solid line representing the plotted values and dashed lines representing the values from the CSV file.

1418925667

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Exporting plots data to a CSV file' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='CSV export' space='']

(The first column is always the value of time on the y-axis) and read multiple CSV files and show them all at once.<br /><br /></p><p>To show data from a CSV file in the Time series chart</p><hr /><ol><li>Click the <strong>Load Data...</strong> button on the toolbar of the Time series chart's plot pane.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Load Data button" ac:alt="Load Data button"><ri:attachment ri:filename="loaddata_button.png" /></ac:image></li><li><p>Select a CSV file (see an example of the result data in a CSV file), and the Time series chart will show data from the columns in the file in lines. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="CSV file" ac:alt="CSV file"><ri:attachment ri:filename="csvfile.png" /></ac:image></h6><p>The following figure shows a line chart. The solid line in the chart represents the values plotted Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit while the dashed lines represent the values from the CSV file.</p><h6><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Lines chart" ac:alt="Lines chart"><ri:attachment ri:filename="csvdata_lines.png" /></ac:image></h6></li></ol><h6 style="text-align: center;">A line chart with the solid line representing the plotted values and dashed lines representing the values from the CSV file.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="07d09f2d-4a72-4cd8-82a7-962ea6385d08"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1418925667</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Exporting plots data to a CSV file" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="CSV export" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249569722 space=MMA version=2 -->
## PAGE 00683: Representing enumeration values

- page_id: `249569722`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249569722/Representing+enumeration+values
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-19T14:56:06.763+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage
- labels: ['representing-enumeration-values', 'enumeration-value-representation']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

###### To use the ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage to represent an enumeration value

1. Create an ImageSwitcher element, and specify the enumeration, whose values will be represented by an ActiveImage of the ImageSwitcher, as the tagged value of the represent tag definition of the ImageSwitcher element.
2. Create an Active image for each enumeration literal owned by the enumeration.
3. Specify an image that will be the image attribute of each created ActiveImage, and set the enumeration literal that will be represented by the ActiveImage as the activeElement tag definition.

You can use*SmallTestSamples.mdzip* as your example. This file is a sample project of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit. It is located in the *<*install_root*>\samples\simulation* folder. In this example, an ImageSwitcher was created to represent a VerdictKind enumeration. An ActiveImage was created for each enumeration literal of the VerdictKind as follows

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Image switcher and Active Image representing Enumeration and Enumeration Literals.

If a property is typed by an enumeration, you can use the ImageSwitcher and the ActiveImage to represent it with a UI frame mockup of the Classifier that owns the property. Once you drag the property to the panel owned by the UI frame mockup, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will prompt you to select either an ImageSwitcher or a group of radio buttons to represent the value. If you select the ImageSwitcher, the ActiveImage that represents the enumeration literal, which is the default value of the property, will open in the panel or in the group box to which the property has been dragged.

The figure below shows the User Interface Modeling diagram of the *SmallTestSamples.mdzip* sample project. It contains a UI frame that represents the **Test Result** dialog, which is the Classifier owning the property typed by the VerdictKind enumeration. The property is **result:VerdictKind,**whose default value is **pass**. Now, the ActiveImage that represents the value **pass** will be shown in the panel.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### UI Modeling diagram containing a UI frame with Image Switcher.

When the UI mockup opens in the MagicDraw window during model simulation, the image shown in the panel containing the ImageSwitcher will be the ActiveImage that represents the enumeration literal, which is the runtime value of the property.

You can simulate the Test Result dialog in the *SmallTestSamples.mdzip* sample project. You can also change a result value by selecting one of the radio buttons that represents the value: pass, fail, inconclusive, or error. Now the image that corresponds to the value will appear.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### UI Mockup whowing the property's value typed by enumeration.

You can view more information on reusing ImageSwitcher in the UI mockup in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Nested UI Configuration stereotype' space=''].

The image that appears on the web UI through ImageSwitcher is the same image that represents the current State of simulation UI. If the image on the simulation UI is changed, the image on the Web UI will change accordingly and vice versa. Setting the **show empty image for unspecified value** option (true/ false) on the «ImageSwitcher» will change the image on the web UI through ImageSwitcher to be the same as the image that represents the simulation UI.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The generated HTML button on the toolbar of the UI diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### ImageSwitcher on the web UI representing the enumeration value that corresponds to the simulation UI.

Another more comprehensive and realistic sample is available in Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit is *Blackjack.mdzip*. This sample contains a Blackjack game simulation in which Players can only stand or hit, and the Dealer can stand on 17 or higher. Once you run the Run Blackjack Simulation Config, the Blackjack game will begin by showing a Blackjack game table UI (see the following blackjack figure).

Note that this sample also demonstrates the **Nested UI Configuration** feature (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Nested UI Configuration stereotype' space=''] for more information). As the Dealer/Player has 7 possible card slots, according to the **Nested** **UI** **Configuration** feature, the Dealer/Player thus has 7 corresponding Value Properties, e.g., 'Card1', 'Card2', 'Card3', and so on.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### UI showing Blackjack Table in the Blackjack.mdzip sample.

The image showing the card slots demonstrates the application of ImageSwitcher and enumeration. Each card slot is actually a UI Panel having the **NestedUIConfig** stereotype applied in which the **NestedUIConfig****'s** **config** tag is set to the **Card Image Switcher** ImageSwitcher, and the **NestedUIConfig****'s** **feature** tag is set to the corresponding Value Property, e.g., 'Card1'. The **Card Image Switcher**ImageSwitcher is set to represent the **Card** **Value** enumeration. The **Card** **Image** **Switcher** ImageSwitcher contains 53 ActiveImages, each of which is the corresponding Enumeration Literal of the **Card Image** enumeration. Note the **Card Value** enumeration also contains 53 Enumeration Literals.

- '0' Enumeration Literal represents a blank image; a card slot without any card.
- '1' - '52' Enumeration Literals represent the images of 52 distinct cards.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Image Switcher containing active image of 52 cards and blank image.

When the value of one of the 7 Value Properties changes, the card slot image will change accordingly. See the Player's cards in the figure to better understand how it works as follows

- The value of the Player's 'Card1' Value Property is '49' Enumeration Literal, which is the activeElement of the '49' ActiveImage. The '49' ActiveImage represents the ten of spades.
- The value of the Player's 'Card2' Value Property is '28' Enumeration Literal, which is the activeElement of the '28' ActiveImage. The '28' ActiveImage represents the two of hearts.
- The value of the Player's 'Card3' Value Property is '0' Enumeration Literal, which represents a blank card slot. Therefore, no card is open next to the two of hearts card.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### UI and Variables panel showing the value of 7 value properties and the displayed image.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Representing object states' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h4>To use the ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage to represent an enumeration value </h4><hr /><ol><li>Create an ImageSwitcher element, and specify the enumeration, whose values will be represented by an ActiveImage of the ImageSwitcher, as the tagged value of the <strong>represent</strong> tag definition of the ImageSwitcher element.</li><li>Create an Active image for each enumeration literal owned by the enumeration.</li><li>Specify an image that will be the <strong>image</strong> attribute of each created ActiveImage, and set the enumeration literal that will be represented by the ActiveImage as the <strong>activeElement</strong> tag definition. </li></ol><p><br /></p><p>You can use<em> SmallTestSamples.mdzip</em> as your example. This file is a sample project of Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit. It is located in the <em>&lt;<em style="text-align: left;">install_root</em>&gt;\samples\simulation</em> folder. In this example, an ImageSwitcher was created to represent a VerdictKind enumeration. An ActiveImage was created for each enumeration literal of the VerdictKind as follows <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Image Switcher and Active Image Representing Enumeration and Enumeration Literals" ac:alt="Image Switcher and Active Image Representing Enumeration and Enumeration Literals"><ri:attachment ri:filename="imageswitcher_for_enum.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Image switcher and Active Image representing Enumeration and Enumeration Literals.</h6><p>If a property is typed by an enumeration, you can use the ImageSwitcher and the ActiveImage to represent it with a UI frame mockup of the Classifier that owns the property. Once you drag the property to the panel owned by the UI frame mockup, Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit will prompt you to select either an ImageSwitcher or a group of radio buttons to represent the value. If you select the ImageSwitcher, the ActiveImage that represents the enumeration literal, which is the default value of the property, will open in the panel or in the group box to which the property has been dragged. </p><p>The figure below shows the User Interface Modeling diagram of the <em>SmallTestSamples.mdzip</em> sample project. It contains a UI frame that represents the <strong>Test Result</strong> dialog, which is the Classifier owning the property typed by the VerdictKind enumeration. The property is <strong>result:VerdictKind, </strong>whose default value is <strong>pass</strong>. Now, the ActiveImage that represents the value <strong>pass</strong> will be shown in the panel. </p><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="UI Modeling Diagram Containing a UI Frame with Image Switcher" ac:alt="UI Modeling Diagram Containing a UI Frame with Image Switcher"><ri:attachment ri:filename="UI frame in UI modeling diagram.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">UI Modeling diagram containing a UI frame with Image Switcher.</h6><p>When the UI mockup opens in the MagicDraw window during model simulation, the image shown in the panel containing the ImageSwitcher will be the ActiveImage that represents the enumeration literal, which is the runtime value of the property. </p><p>You can simulate the Test Result dialog in the <em>SmallTestSamples.mdzip</em> sample project. You can also change a result value by selecting one of the radio buttons that represents the value: pass, fail, inconclusive, or error. Now the image that corresponds to the value will appear. </p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="UI Mockup Showing the Property's Value typed by Enumeration" ac:alt="UI Mockup Showing the Property's Value typed by Enumeration" ac:height="250"><ri:attachment ri:filename="UI mockup with property value.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">UI Mockup whowing the property's value typed by enumeration.</h6><p>You can view more information on reusing ImageSwitcher in the UI mockup in <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Nested UI Configuration stereotype" /></ac:link>. </p><p class="Textbody">The image that appears on the web UI through ImageSwitcher is the same image that represents the current State of simulation UI. If the image on the simulation UI is changed, the image on the Web UI will change accordingly and vice versa. Setting the <strong>show empty image for unspecified value</strong> option (true/ false) on the «ImageSwitcher» will change the image on the web UI through ImageSwitcher to be the same as the image that represents the simulation UI.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="3.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The generated HTML button on the toolbar of the UI diagram.</h6><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="imageSwitcher_enum.PNG" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">ImageSwitcher on the web UI representing the enumeration value that corresponds to the simulation UI.</h6><p>Another more comprehensive and realistic sample is available in Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit is <em>Blackjack.mdzip</em>. This sample contains a Blackjack game simulation in which Players can only stand or hit, and the Dealer can stand on 17 or higher. Once you run the Run Blackjack Simulation Config, the Blackjack game will begin by showing a Blackjack game table UI (see the following blackjack figure). </p><p>Note that this sample also demonstrates the <strong>Nested UI Configuration</strong> feature (see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Nested UI Configuration stereotype" /></ac:link> for more information). As the Dealer/Player has 7 possible card slots, according to the <strong>Nested</strong> <strong>UI</strong> <strong>Configuration</strong> feature, the Dealer/Player thus has 7 corresponding Value Properties, e.g., 'Card1', 'Card2', 'Card3', and so on. </p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="UI Showing Blackjack Table in Blackjack.mdzip" ac:alt="UI Showing Blackjack Table in Blackjack.mdzip"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Blackjack1.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">UI showing Blackjack Table in the Blackjack.mdzip sample.</h6><p>The image showing the card slots demonstrates the application of ImageSwitcher and enumeration. Each card slot is actually a UI Panel having the <strong>NestedUIConfig</strong> stereotype applied in which the <strong>NestedUIConfig</strong><strong>'s</strong> <strong>config</strong> tag is set to the <strong>Card Image Switcher</strong> ImageSwitcher, and the <strong>NestedUIConfig</strong><strong>'s</strong> <strong>feature</strong> tag is set to the corresponding Value Property, e.g., 'Card1'. The <strong>Card Image Switcher </strong>ImageSwitcher is set to represent the <strong>Card</strong> <strong>Value</strong> enumeration. The <strong>Card</strong> <strong>Image</strong> <strong>Switcher</strong> ImageSwitcher contains 53 ActiveImages, each of which is the corresponding Enumeration Literal of the <strong>Card Image</strong> enumeration. Note the <strong>Card Value</strong> enumeration also contains 53 Enumeration Literals. </p><ul><li>'0' Enumeration Literal represents a blank image; a card slot without any card.</li><li>'1' - '52' Enumeration Literals represent the images of 52 distinct cards. <br /><br /></li></ul><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Image Switcher Containing Active Image of 52 Cards and Blank Image" ac:alt="Image Switcher Containing Active Image of 52 Cards and Blank Image"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Blackjack2.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">Image Switcher containing active image of 52 cards and blank image.</h6><p>When the value of one of the 7 Value Properties changes, the card slot image will change accordingly. See the Player's cards in the figure to better understand how it works as follows</p><ul><li>The value of the Player's 'Card1' Value Property is '49' Enumeration Literal, which is the activeElement of the '49' ActiveImage. The '49' ActiveImage represents the ten of spades.</li><li>The value of the Player's 'Card2' Value Property is '28' Enumeration Literal, which is the activeElement of the '28' ActiveImage. The '28' ActiveImage represents the two of hearts.</li><li>The value of the Player's 'Card3' Value Property is '0' Enumeration Literal, which represents a blank card slot. Therefore, no card is open next to the two of hearts card. <br /><br /></li></ul><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="UI and Variable Panel Showing the Value of 7 Value Properties and the Displayed Image" ac:alt="UI and Variable Panel Showing the Value of 7 Value Properties and the Displayed Image"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Blackjack3.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">UI and Variables panel showing the value of 7 value properties and the displayed image.</h6></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing object states" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249569715 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00684: Representing object states

- page_id: `249569715`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249569715/Representing+object+states
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-03-13T07:43:49.414+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage
- labels: ['representing-object-states', 'state-representation']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can use an ActiveImage to represent each state of a runtime object whose Classifier is represented by the ImageSwitcher that owns the ActiveImage. The *FlashingLight.mdzip* sample is used throughout this page. You can open the sample by downloading it from the attached link.

To use the ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage to represent a state

1. Create an ImageSwitcher element, and set its represent tag definition with a Classifier whose state will be represented by an active image owned by the ImageSwitcher.
2. Create an ActiveImage in the ImageSwitcher for each State of the Classifier represented by the ImageSwitcher.
3. Specify the image that will be the image attribute of each created ActiveImage and set the State, which will be represented by the ActiveImage, as the tagged value of the activeElement tag definition.

The following figure illustrates an example of how to use ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage to represent the States of a runtime object (see the *FlashingLight.mdzip* sample)

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Using ImageSwitcher in the FlashingLight.mdzip sample with internal simulation clock.

The image that appears on the web UI through ImageSwitcher is the same image that represents the current State of simulation UI. If the image on the simulation UI is changed, the image on the Web UI will change accordingly and vice versa. Either image is able to accept a signal by clicking the mouse.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Setting startWebServer to true in the Simulation Config to show the web UI.

###### Sample model

The model used in the figures of this page is the FlashingLight.mdzip sample model that comes with MagicDraw. To open this sample, do either of the following

- Download [ATTACHMENT filename='FlashingLight.mdzip'] .
- Find it in the modeling tool <*install_root*>\samples\simulation\FlashingLight.mdzip.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Representing enumeration values' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>You can use an ActiveImage to represent each state of a runtime object whose Classifier is represented by the ImageSwitcher that owns the ActiveImage. The <em>FlashingLight.mdzip</em> sample is used throughout this page. You can open the sample by downloading it from the attached link.</p><p><br /></p><p>To use the ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage to represent a state</p><hr /><ol><li>Create an ImageSwitcher element, and set its <strong>represent</strong> tag definition with a Classifier whose state will be represented by an active image owned by the ImageSwitcher.</li><li>Create an ActiveImage in the ImageSwitcher for each State of the Classifier represented by the ImageSwitcher.</li><li>Specify the image that will be the <strong>image</strong> attribute of each created ActiveImage and set the State, which will be represented by the ActiveImage, as the tagged value of the <strong>activeElement</strong> tag definition. </li></ol><p><br /></p><p>The following figure illustrates an example of how to use ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage to represent the States of a runtime object (see the <em>FlashingLight.mdzip</em> sample)</p><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="6742-1.PNG" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Using ImageSwitcher in the FlashingLight.mdzip sample with internal simulation clock.</h6><p>The image that appears on the web UI through ImageSwitcher is the same image that represents the current State of simulation UI. If the image on the simulation UI is changed, the image on the Web UI will change accordingly and vice versa. Either image is able to accept a signal by clicking the mouse.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="6742-2.PNG" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Setting startWebServer to true in the Simulation Config to show the web UI.</h6><h4>Sample model</h4><p>The model used in the figures of this page is the FlashingLight.mdzip sample model that comes with MagicDraw. To open this sample, do either of the following</p><hr /><ul><li>Download <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="FlashingLight.mdzip" /></ac:link>.</li><li>Find it in the modeling tool <em>&lt;<em style="text-align: left;">install_root</em>&gt;\samples\simulation\FlashingLight.mdzip.</em></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing enumeration values" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249569843 space=MMA version=1 -->
## PAGE 00685: Representing parts of a simulation context

- page_id: `249569843`
- space_key: `MMA`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MMA/pages/249569843/Representing+parts+of+a+simulation+context
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-03-13T07:34:22.185+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst / Cameo Simulation Toolkit Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > Nested UI Configuration stereotype
- labels: ['displaying-parts-of-simulation-context', 'showing-parts-of-simulation-context', 'simulation-context']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A UI Panel or UI Group Box to which a NestedUIConfig stereotype is applied and a feature tag set can represent some parts and nest other components. One of the samples that shows such purpose is the *SmallTestSamples.mdzip* sample, which is located in the *<*install_root*>\samples\simulation* folder.

The following System Class diagram shows the structures of Class system and Class monitor

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Structures of Class system and Class monitor.

On the same Class diagram, there are Instance Specifications of Class system and Class monitor that will be used in the simulation as follows

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Instance Specifications of Class system and Class monitor.

The User Interface Modeling diagram displays the UI configuration that will be used in the simulation as follows

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### UI Configuration of Class system.

The following figure displays the UI configuration and the Specification of the UI Panel named panel1, which represents the monitor1 Property, as a part of Class system

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
UI Configuration and its Specification.

When the **test_nested_panels** SimulationConfig is run, the UI mockup will be displayed. The following figure exhibits the UI Panels and UI Group Boxes that represent the parts (Properties) of the Class system and in-depth nested parts as well 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Simulation Variables panel and Runtime UI Mockup.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

diagram displays the UI configuration that will be used in the simulation as follows</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="UI Configuration of Class System" ac:alt="UI Configuration of Class
